175	/-! ## §2. The Quot functor
176	
177	The Quot functor `Quot^{Φ,L}_{E/X/S}` sends an `S`-scheme `T ⟶ S` to the
178	set of equivalence classes `⟨F, q⟩` of pairs `(F, q)` where
179	- `F` is a coherent sheaf on `X_T = X ×_S T` whose schematic support is
180	  proper over `T` and which is `T`-flat,
181	- `q : E_T ↠ F` is a surjective `O_{X_T}`-linear homomorphism,
182	- the fiberwise Hilbert polynomial of `F|_{X_t}` with respect to `L|_{X_t}`
183	  equals `Φ` at every `t ∈ T`.
184	
185	Two pairs `(F, q)` and `(F', q')` are equivalent iff `ker(q) = ker(q')`.
186	
187	The Hilbert scheme is the special case `E = O_X`:
188	`Hilb^{Φ,L}_{X/S} = Quot^{Φ,L}_{O_X/X/S}`.
189	
190	Blueprint reference: `def:quot_functor` (Nitsure §1; FGA Explained Ch. 5). -/
191	
192	/-- The **Quot functor** `Quot^{Φ,L}_{E/X/S}` of coherent quotients of `E`
193	on `X ×_S -` with Hilbert polynomial `Φ`.
194	
195	Encoded as a contravariant functor `(Over S)ᵒᵖ ⥤ Type u`, sending an
196	`S`-scheme `T → S` (i.e. an object of `Over S`) to the set of
197	equivalence classes `⟨F, q⟩` of pairs `(F, q)` of a `T`-flat coherent
198	sheaf `F` on `X ×_S T` with proper support and a surjection
199	`q : E_T ↠ F` whose fiberwise Hilbert polynomial is `Φ`, modulo
200	`ker(q) = ker(q')`. Functoriality is pullback of the quotient along
201	`X ×_S T' ⟶ X ×_S T`.
202	
203	iter-177+: the body packages the on-objects / on-morphisms data using the
204	`Scheme.Modules.pullback` bifunctor on the relative product
205	`X ×_S T`, with the equivalence relation `ker(q) = ker(q')` quotiented
206	out via `Setoid` / `Quotient`. For the iter-176 file-skeleton the body
207	is a typed `sorry`. -/
208	noncomputable def QuotFunctor {S X : Scheme.{u}} [IsLocallyNoetherian S]
209	    (_π : X ⟶ S) [LocallyOfFiniteType _π] (_L _E : X.Modules)
210	    (_Φ : Polynomial ℚ) :
211	    (Over S)ᵒᵖ ⥤ Type u :=
212	  sorry
213	
214	end Scheme

216	/-! ## §3. The Grassmannian scheme
217	
218	Since Mathlib carries no Grassmannian *scheme*, we encode it here as a
219	contravariant functor on `Over S` together with a representability
220	statement. The construction proceeds by gluing `binom(r, d)` affine
221	charts `U^I ≅ A^{d(r-d)}_S` along the Plücker cocycle, yielding a smooth
222	projective `S`-scheme `Gr_S(V, d)` of relative dimension `d(r-d)`,
223	equipped with a tautological rank-`d` quotient
224	`π* V ↠ U` and the Plücker closed embedding into `ℙ_S(⋀^d V)`.
225	
226	Blueprint references: `def:grassmannian_scheme`,
227	`thm:grassmannian_representable` (Nitsure §1 Exercise (2),
228	"Construction of Grassmannian"; FGA Explained Ch. 5). -/
229	
230	namespace Scheme
231	
232	/-- The **Grassmannian functor** `Grass(V, d) : (Sch/S)^op ⥤ Set` of
233	rank-`d` quotients of a locally free `O_S`-module `V` of rank `r ≥ d`.
234	
235	Encoded as the functor sending an `S`-scheme `T → S` to the set of
236	equivalence classes `⟨F, q⟩` of pairs `(F, q)` with
237	`q : V_T ↠ F` a surjection of `O_T`-modules and `F` locally free of
238	rank `d`, modulo `ker(q) = ker(q')`. Concretely
239	`Grass(V, d) = Quot^{d, O_S}_{V/S/S}` (the Quot functor for `X = S`,
240	`E = V`, constant Hilbert polynomial `d`).
241	
242	iter-177+: the body re-exports `QuotFunctor (𝟙 S) (?) V Φ_d`, where
243	`Φ_d : Polynomial ℚ` is the constant polynomial `d`. For the iter-176
244	file-skeleton the body is a typed `sorry`. -/
245	noncomputable def Grassmannian {S : Scheme.{u}} [IsLocallyNoetherian S]
246	    (_V : S.Modules) (_d : ℕ) :
247	    (Over S)ᵒᵖ ⥤ Type u :=
248	  sorry
249	
250	/-- **Representability of the Grassmannian.**
251	
252	For a noetherian scheme `S`, a locally free `O_S`-module `V` of rank `r`,
253	and `1 ≤ d ≤ r`, the Grassmannian functor `Grass(V, d)` of
254	`Grassmannian` is representable by a smooth projective `S`-scheme
255	`Gr_S(V, d) ⟶ S` of relative dimension `d(r-d)`, equipped with a
256	tautological rank-`d` quotient `π* V ↠ U`. The determinant line bundle
257	`det(U)` is relatively very ample, giving a Plücker closed embedding
258	`Gr_S(V, d) ↪ ℙ_S(⋀^d V)`.
259	
260	We package the conclusion as the existence of a representing
261	`Y : Over S` together with a `Functor.RepresentableBy Y` witness for
262	`Grassmannian V d`; the additional projective / smooth / Plücker
263	structure is implicit in the construction and is iter-177+ refinement
264	work (once the proof body lands).
265	
266	iter-177+: the body follows Nitsure §1 "Construction of Grassmannian":
267	glue the `binom(r, d)` affine charts `U^I ≅ A^{d(r-d)}_S` along the
268	Plücker cocycle, verify separatedness via the diagonal cut, verify
269	properness by the DVR valuative criterion, build the tautological
270	quotient `U`, exhibit the Plücker embedding via the determinant line
271	bundle. For the iter-176 file-skeleton the body is a typed `sorry`. -/
272	theorem Grassmannian.representable {S : Scheme.{u}} [IsLocallyNoetherian S]
273	    (V : S.Modules) (d : ℕ) :
274	    ∃ (Y : Over S), Nonempty ((Grassmannian V d).RepresentableBy Y) := by
275	  sorry

277	/-! ## §4. Representability of the Quot scheme
278	
279	Grothendieck–Altman–Kleiman: for a noetherian `S`, a projective
280	`π : X ⟶ S`, a relatively very ample `L` on `X`, a coherent
281	`E` on `X`, and `Φ ∈ ℚ[λ]`, the Quot functor `Quot^{Φ,L}_{E/X/S}` is
282	representable by a *projective* `S`-scheme.
283	
284	The proof has four steps (Nitsure §5):
285	1. **Boundedness** via Castelnuovo–Mumford `m`-regularity (uniform across
286	   fibers of `π` and across coherent quotients of `E_s` with Hilbert
287	   polynomial `Φ`).
288	2. **Grassmannian embedding**
289	   `α : Quot^{Φ,L}_{E/X/S} ↪ Grass(W ⊗_{O_S} Sym^r V, Φ(r))`
290	   for `r ≥ m`, sending `⟨F, q⟩ ↦ ⟨(π_T)_* F(r), (π_T)_*(q(r))⟩`.
291	3. **Locally closed in Grassmannian** via the flattening stratification
292	   applied to the universal cokernel on the Grassmannian, producing the
293	   stratum `T_0^Φ`.
294	4. **Closed embedding** by the valuative criterion of properness for
295	   DVRs.
296	
297	The reduction to the universal case `X = ℙ(V)`, `E = π*W` is recorded as
298	`lem:quot_reduction_to_pi_star_W` in the blueprint chapter.
299	
300	Blueprint reference: `thm:quot_representable` (Nitsure §5; FGA Explained
301	Ch. 5; Grothendieck, FGA TDTE-IV). -/
302	
303	/-- **Representability of the Quot scheme** (Grothendieck, Altman–Kleiman).
304	
305	Let `S` be a noetherian scheme, `π : X ⟶ S` a projective morphism (here
306	encoded as a proper `LocallyOfFiniteType` morphism; the projectivity
307	upgrades once `IsProjective` lands in Mathlib), `L` a line bundle on `X`
308	(relatively very ample), `E` a coherent `O_X`-module, and
309	`Φ ∈ ℚ[λ]`. Then the Quot functor `Quot^{Φ,L}_{E/X/S}` of `QuotFunctor`
310	is representable by an `S`-scheme.
311	
312	We package the conclusion as the existence of `Q : Over S` together with
313	a `Functor.RepresentableBy Q` witness for `QuotFunctor π L E Φ`; the
314	*projectivity* of `Q ⟶ S` (and the universal quotient
315	`q^univ : π^*_Q E ↠ F^univ` on `X ×_S Q^{Φ,L}`) is implicit in the
316	construction (Plücker-embedded into a projective Grassmannian over `S`)
317	and is iter-177+ refinement work.
318	
319	iter-177+: the body follows the four-step Nitsure §5 proof
320	(boundedness ⟶ Grassmannian embedding ⟶ flattening stratification ⟶
321	valuative-criterion closed embedding); the sub-lemmas live in
322	`lem:quot_boundedness`, `lem:quot_alpha_injective`,
323	`lem:quot_valuative_criterion`, and the existential reduction in
324	`lem:quot_reduction_to_pi_star_W`. For the iter-176 file-skeleton the
325	body is a typed `sorry`. -/
326	theorem QuotScheme {S X : Scheme.{u}} [IsLocallyNoetherian S]
327	    (π : X ⟶ S) [LocallyOfFiniteType π] [IsProper π]
328	    (L E : X.Modules) (Φ : Polynomial ℚ) :
329	    ∃ (Q : Over S), Nonempty ((QuotFunctor π L E Φ).RepresentableBy Q) := by
330	  sorry
331	
332	end Scheme

334	/-! ## §5. Cohomology and base change
335	
336	The Quot construction uses cohomology-and-base-change in two places: the
337	boundedness step (Nitsure §5 "Use of m-Regularity") and the Grassmannian
338	embedding (Nitsure §5 "Embedding Quot into Grassmannian"). We record the
339	relevant statement as a named theorem so the Lean encoding can cite it
340	directly.
341	
342	The Stacks 02KH form is the statement for higher direct images
343	`R^i f_*` on quasi-coherent sheaves; for the iter-176 file-skeleton we
344	state the `i = 0` form on `Scheme.Modules`, which is the substantive
345	content of `lemma-flat-base-change-cohomology(ii)` of Stacks 02KH. The
346	`R^i` form is iter-177+ work after higher-direct-image infrastructure
347	is in scope.
348	
349	Blueprint reference: `thm:flat_base_change_cohomology` (Stacks 02KH). -/
350	
351	/-! ### Flat base change of cohomology (Stacks tag 02KH, `i = 0` form)
352	
353	Let
354	```
355	   g'
356	X' ───→ X
357	│       │
358	f'      f
359	↓       ↓
360	S' ───→ S
361	   g
362	```
363	be a cartesian square of schemes with `g` flat and `f` quasi-compact
364	quasi-separated. Let `F` be a sheaf of `O_X`-modules. Then the canonical
365	base-change map `g* (f_* F) ⟶ f'_* ((g')* F)` is an isomorphism in
366	`S'.Modules`.
367	
368	(The full Stacks 02KH statement covers all higher direct images
369	`R^i f_* F` for `i ≥ 0`; the `i = 0` form encoded here is the
370	substantive content of `lemma-flat-base-change-cohomology(ii)` of
371	Stacks 02KH, with the `i ≥ 1` form post-iter-177 work after the
372	higher-direct-image bifunctor lands.)
373	
374	iter-177 (Lane QS-FLAT): the body constructs the canonical base-change
375	natural transformation via the mate equivalence of the
376	`pullback ⊣ pushforward` adjunction (Mathlib's `mateEquiv` of
377	`Scheme.Modules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction`), then exhibits the iso
378	via the `canonicalBaseChangeMap_isIso` helper. The deep mathematical
379	content (Stacks tag 02KH / 02KE / 00H8) lives entirely in the helper;
380	it reduces affine-locally to: for a flat ring map `A → B` and an
381	`A`-algebra `R`, the canonical map `B ⊗_A H^i(X, F) → H^i(X_B, F_B)`
382	is an iso for any quasi-coherent `F` (the `i = 0` form is what we use).
383	The helper remains a typed `sorry` pending the affine-local reduction
384	+ algebraic flat base change; this is iter-178+ body work after
385	quasi-compact open-cover Mayer-Vietoris infrastructure is in scope. -/
386	
387	/-- The canonical base-change natural transformation `g* (f_* -) ⟶ f'_* ((g')* -)`
388	associated to a cartesian square
389	```
390	     g'
391	X' ─────→ X
392	│         │
393	f'        f
394	↓         ↓
395	S' ─────→ S
396	     g
397	```
398	in `Scheme`. Constructed as the *mate* (Beck–Chevalley transform)
399	under the `pullback ⊣ pushforward` adjunctions on sheaves of modules
400	of the canonical 2-isomorphism
401	`pullback g ⋙ pullback f' ≅ pullback f ⋙ pullback g'` coming from
402	`g' ≫ f = f' ≫ g`.
403	
404	This natural transformation always exists (no flatness needed). The
405	content of the flat base-change theorem (Stacks tag 02KH) is the
406	*isomorphism* claim under the hypotheses
407	`[QuasiCompact f] [QuasiSeparated f] [Flat g]`; that claim is the
408	helper `canonicalBaseChangeMap_isIso`. -/
409	noncomputable def canonicalBaseChangeMap
410	    {X X' S S' : Scheme.{u}}
411	    {f : X ⟶ S} {g : S' ⟶ S} {g' : X' ⟶ X} {f' : X' ⟶ S'}
412	    (sq : IsPullback g' f' f g) :
413	    Scheme.Modules.pushforward f ⋙ Scheme.Modules.pullback g ⟶
414	      Scheme.Modules.pullback g' ⋙ Scheme.Modules.pushforward f' :=
415	  CategoryTheory.mateEquiv
416	      (Scheme.Modules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction f)
417	      (Scheme.Modules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction f')
418	      (((Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp f' g) ≪≫
419	        Scheme.Modules.pullbackCongr sq.w.symm ≪≫
420	        (Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp g' f).symm).hom)
421	
422	/-- **Trivial bridge** (pushforward of pullback at sections — rfl).
423	
424	The section of `(pushforward f').obj ((pullback g').obj F)` over an
425	open `U ⊆ S'` identifies definitionally with the section of
426	`(pullback g').obj F` over `f' ⁻¹ᵁ U`, by `Scheme.Modules.pushforward_obj_obj`.
427	Factored as a separate (closed) lemma to document step (3) of the
428	intended-body plan in
429	`canonicalBaseChangeMap_app_app_isIso_of_isAffineOpen` cleanly. -/
430	private lemma pushforward_pullback_section_eq_pullback_section
431	    {X X' S' : Scheme.{u}} (f' : X' ⟶ S') (g' : X' ⟶ X)
432	    (F : X.Modules) (U : S'.Opens) :
433	    Γ((Scheme.Modules.pushforward f').obj ((Scheme.Modules.pullback g').obj F), U) =
434	      Γ((Scheme.Modules.pullback g').obj F, f' ⁻¹ᵁ U) := rfl

1146	    (sq : IsPullback g' f' f g)

1148	    (F : X.Modules) [F.IsQuasicoherent] (U : S'.Opens) :

986	\begin{lemma}
987	
988	\leanok
989	[Quasi-coherent module on an affine open is \texttt{tilde} of its sections (Stacks 01I8)]
990	  \label{lem:tildeIso_of_isQuasicoherent_isAffineOpen}
991	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.tildeIso_of_isQuasicoherent_isAffineOpen}
992	  \uses{def:quot_canonical_basechange_map}
993	  \textit{Source: Stacks~\href{https://stacks.math.columbia.edu/tag/01I8}{01I8}
994	  (a quasi-coherent module on an affine scheme is \(\widetilde M\) for
995	  some module).}
996	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, \(\mathcal N\) a quasi-coherent
997	  \(\mathcal O_X\)-module, and \(V \subset X\) an affine open. Then the
998	  pullback of \(\mathcal N\) along \texttt{hV.fromSpec} is canonically
999	  isomorphic to \texttt{tilde} of its sections:
1000	  \[
1001	    (\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.pullback}\,\mathtt{hV.fromSpec}).\mathrm{obj}\,
1002	      \mathcal N
1003	    \;\cong\;
1004	    \widetilde{\,\Gamma(\mathcal N, V)\,}
1005	    \quad\text{(on \(\mathrm{Spec}\,\Gamma(X, V)\)).}
1006	  \]
1007	  Mathlib \texttt{b80f227} ships the analog for \emph{free}
1008	  quasi-coherent modules (\texttt{isIso\_fromTildeΓ\_of\_presentation}
1009	  partial), but the general statement
1010	  ``\([N.IsQuasicoherent] \Rightarrow N|_V \cong \widetilde{\Gamma(N,V)}\)
1011	  on an affine open \(V\)'' (Stacks 01I8 \emph{lemma-affine-qc-module-iff})
1012	  is unowned at the \texttt{Scheme.Modules} layer.
1013	\end{lemma}
1014	\begin{proof}

1016	  Substantive body (Stacks 01I8): the QC sheaf on an affine open is
1017	  determined by its global sections — apply the \texttt{tilde.adjunction}
1018	  counit at the affine open and use that \texttt{tilde} is fully faithful
1019	  on quasi-coherent objects.

1083	proceeds in three phases.
1084	
1085	\paragraph{Phase 1: Hilbert polynomial (\cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}).}
1086	On a finite-type morphism \(\pi : X \to S\) with \(S\) noetherian and a
1087	coherent \(\mathcal{F}\) on \(X\) with proper support over \(S\), the per-fiber
1088	Hilbert polynomial is encoded as a function $s \mapsto
1089	\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]$. Mathlib provides
1090	\texttt{CategoryTheory.PolynomialModule} and Euler-characteristic
1091	infrastructure on coherent sheaves on noetherian schemes; the polynomial
1092	property of \(m \mapsto \chi(\mathcal{F} \otimes L^{\otimes m})\) requires
1093	Snapper's Lemma, which is a project-side sub-build on top of the
1094	graded-Euler-characteristic infrastructure.
1095	
1096	\paragraph{Phase 2: Grassmannian scheme (\cref{def:grassmannian_scheme},
1097	\cref{thm:grassmannian_representable}).} The Grassmannian
1098	\(\mathrm{Gr}_S(V, d)\) is encoded as the relative gluing of \(\binom{r}{d}\)
1099	affine charts \(U^I\) (each isomorphic to a relative affine space
1100	\(\mathbb{A}^{d(r-d)}_S\)) via the Pl\"ucker cocycle \(\theta_{I,J}\). The
1101	gluing backbone is \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.GlueData}. The
1102	universal quotient sheaf \(\mathcal{U}\) is glued by the transition cocycle
1103	\(g_{I,J} = (X^I_J)^{-1}\), and the determinant line bundle
1104	\(\det \mathcal{U}\) gives the Pl\"ucker closed embedding into
1105	\(\mathbb{P}_S(\bigwedge^d V)\). The representability statement uses
1106	\texttt{CategoryTheory.Functor.RepresentableBy} (as in
1107	\cref{chap:Picard_RelativeSpec}) on the universal pair
1108	\((\mathrm{Gr}_S(V, d), \mathcal{U})\).
1109	
1110	\paragraph{Phase 3: Quot scheme (\cref{thm:quot_representable}).}
1111	The Quot functor is constructed as a locally closed subfunctor of the
1112	Grassmannian
1113	\(\mathrm{Grass}(W \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_S} \mathrm{Sym}^r V, \Phi(r))\), via:
1114	\begin{enumerate}

349	noncomputable
350	def tildeSelf : tilde (ModuleCat.of R R) ≅ SheafOfModules.unit.{u} _ := .refl _
351	
352	instance : IsIso (Scheme.Modules.fromTildeΓ (SheafOfModules.unit.{u} (Spec R).ringCatSheaf)) :=
353	  isIso_fromTildeΓ_iff.mpr ⟨_, ⟨tildeSelf⟩⟩
354	
355	/-- Tilde of direct sums of `R` as an `R`-module is isomorphic to the free sheaf. -/
356	noncomputable
357	def tildeFinsupp (ι : Type u) : tilde (ModuleCat.of R (ι →₀ R)) ≅ SheafOfModules.free.{u} ι :=
358	  letI H : IsColimit <| (tilde.functor R).mapCocone (ModuleCat.finsuppCocone R R ι) :=
359	    isColimitOfPreserves (tilde.functor R) (ModuleCat.finsuppCoconeIsColimit R R ι)
360	  letI iso : (Discrete.functor fun (_ : ι) ↦ ModuleCat.of R R) ⋙ tilde.functor R ≅
361	         Discrete.functor fun _ ↦ SheafOfModules.unit.{u} _ :=
362	      Discrete.natIso (fun _ ↦ tildeSelf)
363	  IsColimit.coconePointUniqueUpToIso
364	    ((IsColimit.precomposeHomEquiv iso.symm _).symm H) (coproductIsCoproduct _)
365	
366	instance (ι : Type u) :
367	    IsIso (Scheme.Modules.fromTildeΓ (R := R) (SheafOfModules.free.{u} ι)) :=
368	  isIso_fromTildeΓ_iff.mpr ⟨_, ⟨tildeFinsupp _⟩⟩

371	/-- Given a presentation of a module `M`, we may construct an associated presentation of `M^~`. -/
372	noncomputable
373	def presentationTilde (s : Set M) (hs : Submodule.span R s = ⊤)
374	    (t : Set (s →₀ R))
375	    (ht : Submodule.span R t = LinearMap.ker (Finsupp.linearCombination R ((↑) : s → M))) :
376	    (tilde M).Presentation := by
377	  haveI H₁ : Function.Exact
378	      (ModuleCat.ofHom (Finsupp.linearCombination (α := t) R (↑)))
379	      (ModuleCat.ofHom (Finsupp.linearCombination (α := s) (M := M) R (↑))) :=
380	    (LinearMap.exact_iff.mpr (by simp [Finsupp.range_linearCombination, ht]))
381	  refine SheafOfModules.presentationOfIsCokernelFree.{u}
382	      ((tildeFinsupp t).inv ≫ tilde.map (ModuleCat.ofHom (Finsupp.linearCombination R (↑))) ≫
383	        (tildeFinsupp s).hom) ((tildeFinsupp s).inv ≫
384	          tilde.map (ModuleCat.ofHom (Finsupp.linearCombination R (↑)))) (by
385	    simp only [Category.assoc, Iso.hom_inv_id_assoc, Preadditive.IsIso.comp_left_eq_zero]
386	    rw [← tilde.map_comp, ← ModuleCat.ofHom_comp]
387	    convert tilde.map_zero
388	    exact congr(ModuleCat.ofHom $(H₁.linearMap_comp_eq_zero))) ?_
389	  letI h₁ := ModuleCat.isColimitCokernelCofork _ _ H₁
390	    (by simp [← LinearMap.range_eq_top, Finsupp.range_linearCombination, hs])
391	  refine IsCokernel.ofIso _ (CokernelCofork.mapIsColimit _ h₁ (tilde.functor R)) _ (tildeFinsupp t)
392	    (tildeFinsupp s) (.refl _) (by simp) (by simp)
393	
394	instance : (tilde M).IsQuasicoherent :=
395	  (presentationTilde.{u} _ .univ (by simp) _ (Submodule.span_eq _)).isQuasicoherent
396

398	lemma isIso_fromTildeΓ_of_presentation (M : (Spec R).Modules) (P : M.Presentation) :
399	    IsIso M.fromTildeΓ := by
400	  rw [isIso_fromTildeΓ_iff]
401	  let g := (tilde.functor _).preimage <| (tildeFinsupp _).hom ≫ P.relations.π ≫ kernel.ι _ ≫
402	    (tildeFinsupp _).inv
403	  let iso : cokernel ((tilde.functor R).map g) ≅ cokernel (P.relations.π ≫ kernel.ι _) := by
404	    refine cokernel.mapIso _ _ (tildeFinsupp _) (tildeFinsupp _) ?_
405	    simp only [g, (tilde.functor R).map_preimage]
406	    simp
407	  exact ⟨cokernel g, ⟨PreservesCokernel.iso (tilde.functor R) g ≪≫ iso ≪≫
408	    IsColimit.coconePointUniqueUpToIso (colimit.isColimit _) P.isColimit⟩⟩

316	/-- Restriction of an `𝒪ₓ`-module along an open immersion.
317	This is isomorphic to the pullback functor (see `restrictFunctorIsoPullback`)
318	but has better defeqs. -/
319	def restrictFunctor : Y.Modules ⥤ X.Modules :=
320	  letI α : X.presheaf ⟶ f.opensFunctor.op ⋙ Y.presheaf := { app U := (f.appIso U.unop).inv }
321	  SheafOfModules.pushforward (F := f.opensFunctor)
322	    ⟨Functor.whiskerRight α (forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat)⟩
323	
324	/-- The restriction of a module along an open immersion. -/
325	abbrev restrict (M : Y.Modules) (f : X ⟶ Y) [IsOpenImmersion f] : X.Modules :=
326	  (restrictFunctor f).obj M
327	
328	@[simp] lemma restrict_obj (M : Y.Modules) (f : X ⟶ Y) [IsOpenImmersion f] (U) :
329	    Γ(M.restrict f, U) = Γ(M, f ''ᵁ U) := rfl
330	
331	@[simp] lemma restrict_map (M : Y.Modules) (f : X ⟶ Y) [IsOpenImmersion f] {U V} (i : U ⟶ V) :
332	    (M.restrict f).presheaf.map i.op = M.presheaf.map (f.opensFunctor.map i).op := rfl
333	
334	/-- The restriction of a module along an open immersion. -/
335	def restrictFunctorAdjCounitIso : pushforward f ⋙ restrictFunctor f ≅ 𝟭 _ :=
336	  letI := CategoryTheory.Functor.isContinuous_comp.{u} f.opensFunctor (Opens.map f.base)
337	    (Opens.grothendieckTopology X) (Opens.grothendieckTopology Y) (Opens.grothendieckTopology X)
338	  (SheafOfModules.pushforwardComp _ _) ≪≫ pushforwardNatIso _ (NatIso.ofComponents
339	      (fun U ↦ eqToIso (f.preimage_image_eq U).symm) fun _ ↦ rfl) ≪≫
340	    SheafOfModules.pushforwardCongr (by ext U x; exact
341	      congr($(f.appIso_inv_app_presheafMap U.unop) x)) ≪≫ SheafOfModules.pushforwardId _
342	
343	/-- Restriction is right adjoint to pushforward. -/
344	def restrictAdjunction : restrictFunctor f ⊣ pushforward f := by
345	  refine pushforwardPushforwardAdj (by exact f.isOpenEmbedding.isOpenMap.adjunction) _ _ ?_ ?_

# AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/QuotScheme.lean

## Status: PARTIAL — sheaf-level iso chain landed; HARD BAR not met (0 sorry closures); 12 sorries (unchanged)

iter-193 Lane F target: close `_sectionLinearEquiv` body (the
"compositional bookkeeping" residual). HARD BAR: 1 axiom-clean closure.

**Result**: structural advance — the sheaf-level iso chain is now
in the body (5-step composition); the residual sorry is now localized
to the LinearEquiv extraction + Beck-Chevalley intertwining check.
But no sorry was fully eliminated this iter.

## `pullback_app_isoTensor_baseMap_sectionLinearEquiv` (line 815)

### Attempt 1 (this iter): sheaf-level iso chain assembly

- **Approach**: assemble the 5-step iso chain at the sheaf level
  using the obtained witnesses `step1`, `step2`, `step3` plus
  Mathlib's `IsAffineOpen.SpecMap_appLE_fromSpec` for the
  commutative square `hU.fromSpec ≫ g = Spec.map φ ≫ hV.fromSpec`.

- **Substantive new content** (~25 LOC):

  ```lean
  have h_eq : _hU.fromSpec ≫ g = Spec.map (g.appLE V U e) ≫ _hV.fromSpec :=
    (IsAffineOpen.SpecMap_appLE_fromSpec g _hV _hU e).symm
  let composedIso :=
    ((Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp _hU.fromSpec g).app N ≪≫
      (Scheme.Modules.pullbackCongr h_eq).app N ≪≫
      ((Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp (Spec.map (g.appLE V U e)) _hV.fromSpec).app N).symm ≪≫
      (Scheme.Modules.pullback (Spec.map (g.appLE V U e))).mapIso step1 ≪≫
      step2)
  let topAdd := { ... : Γ((pullback hU.fromSpec).obj ((pullback g).obj N), ⊤) ≃+ _ }
  ```

  The 5-step chain:
  1. `(pullback hU.fromSpec).obj ((pullback g).obj N)` (sheaf on Spec Γ(Y, U))
  2. ≅ `(pullback (hU.fromSpec ≫ g)).obj N`                          [pullbackComp]
  3. ≅ `(pullback (Spec.map φ ≫ hV.fromSpec)).obj N`                 [pullbackCongr h_eq]
  4. ≅ `(pullback (Spec.map φ)).obj ((pullback hV.fromSpec).obj N)`  [(pullbackComp).symm]
  5. ≅ `(pullback (Spec.map φ)).obj (tilde Γ(N, V))`                 [step1: Stacks 01I8]
  6. ≅ `tilde (TensorProduct Γ(X,V) Γ(Y,U) Γ(N,V))`                  [step2: Stacks 01HQ]

  Then ⊤-sections give the AddEquiv `topAdd`.

- **Result**: PARTIAL — sheaf-level iso chain landed axiom-clean.
  Residual: the Σ-pair construction (LinearEquiv with intertwining)
  still requires:
  (a) ⊤-section transport to TensorProduct via `tilde.isoTop.symm`
      (faces a `Γ(X, V)` notation ambiguity between the structure-sheaf
      `Γ(X, V) : CommRingCat` and the module-sheaf `Γ(X, V) : Ab`
      readings — needs careful `↑` type ascription or fresh `set`-name);
  (b) AddEquiv → Γ(Y, U)-LinearEquiv upgrade via `Hom.app_smul` +
      ΓSpecIso scalar transport;
  (c) compose with `step3.symm` to land in `Γ((pullback g).obj N, U)`;
  (d) Beck-Chevalley intertwining check `1 ⊗ x ↦ baseMap g N e x`
      via naturality of the adjunction unit
      `pullback_app_isoTensor_unitAtV`.

- **Key Mathlib finding**: `IsAffineOpen.SpecMap_appLE_fromSpec` at
  `Mathlib/AlgebraicGeometry/AffineScheme.lean:464` provides exactly
  the commutative square we need. Argument order is delicate (mathlib's
  `f` ↔ our `g`, mathlib's `hU` ↔ our `hV`, etc.).

### Dead-end warning

- `Γ(X, V)` notation is ambiguous (CommRingCat reading vs Ab reading
  via Modules.presheaf). Inside `ModuleCat.of Γ(Y, U) (TensorProduct
  Γ(X, V) Γ(Y, U) Γ(N, V))`, elaboration can't pick between them.
  Workarounds: (i) use `_ ` placeholder with inferred type (worked
  for `topAdd`); (ii) `set R : CommRingCat := Γ(X, V)` to bind the
  CommRingCat reading; (iii) explicit `↑Γ(X, V)` and `↑Γ(Y, U)`.
  iter-194 prover SHOULD use approach (ii) when extracting the
  LinearEquiv to avoid repeating this trap.

### Next-iter recipe

The chain is in place. Next iter:


## iter-193 · parallel prover

The `Γ(X, V)` notation in `AlgebraicGeometry` is scoped from two
separate Mathlib files (one for the structure sheaf `Γ(X, V) :
CommRingCat`, one for the module sheaf `Γ(M, V) : Ab`). When you
write a nested type like `ModuleCat.of Γ(Y, U) (TensorProduct
Γ(X, V) Γ(Y, U) Γ(N, V))`, Lean cannot disambiguate and produces
"Ambiguous term" errors with two `?m.xxx` interpretations.

This burned ~30 minutes of attempt-debugging on Lane F. A few project-
level workarounds worth knowing (suggest documenting in
`references/lean-lsp-tools-api.md` or a `references/gamma-notation-traps.md`):
- `set R : CommRingCat := Γ(X, V)` binds the CommRingCat reading and
  unblocks downstream elaboration.
- `↑Γ(X, V)` is *not enough* if both interpretations have `Coe to Type`.
- Letting Lean infer the type (`let foo := ...` with `_` placeholder
  or trailing `: ... ≃+ _`) sidesteps the ambiguity when the surrounding
  context disambiguates.

Not a tool bug, but a recurring Mathlib AlgebraicGeometry friction
for any prover working at the `Scheme.Modules` ↔ `tilde` boundary.
EOF

content:
1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.
12	
13	## Skills
14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. Supports DeepSeek, Kimi, OpenRouter, OpenAI, and Gemini; defaults to `--provider auto` which picks the best available key automatically. **Requires at least one key in env** (`DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` / `MOONSHOT_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` / `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY`) — check via `env | grep -E "DEEPSEEK|MOONSHOT|OPENROUTER|OPENAI|GEMINI"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)
47	
48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All Archon state files are in `.archon/`. The table below covers **phase agents** (plan, prover, review) and the **user**. Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row — they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See "Subagent permissions" after the table.
51	
52	| File | Plan | Prover | Review | User |
53	|------|------|--------|--------|------|
54	| `PROGRESS.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
55	| `STRATEGY.md` | read + write | — | — | read |
56	| `USER_HINTS.md` | **loop-managed** (captured + cleared by the CLI; injected into the plan prompt) | — | — | write |
57	| `task_pending.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
58	| `task_done.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
59	| `task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect) | write (own file) | read only | read |
60	| `proof-journal/` | read | — | **write** | read |
61	| `PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | — | **write** (Knowledge Base only — session log lives in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`) | read |
62	| `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` | **write** (this iter only) | — | read (recent window injected) | read |
63	| `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (recent window injected) | — | **write** (this iter only) | read |
64	| `iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md` | optional write | — | read | read |
65	| `archon-protected.yaml` | read | read | read | **write** |
66	| `.lean` files | — | write (own file; frozen protected signatures) | — | write (via comments) |
67	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints) | read | **write** (semantic markers: `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections — NOT `\leanok`) | read |
68	| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | — | — | read |
69	| `TO_USER.md` | — (review owns) | — | **write** (reset at review start) | read |
70	
71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.
82	
83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	## Agent Roles
112	
113	### Plan Agent
114	
115	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
116	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
117	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
118	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
119	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
120	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
121	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
122	
123	### Prover Agent
124	
125	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
126	- Your **active mode** is injected directly into your invocation prompt — read it carefully, it defines your goal, workflow, and constraints for this session.
127	- When your mode specifies `read_blueprint: true`, you are permitted to **read** your assigned blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<your_slug>.tex`) for the informal proof sketch. You may never write to blueprint chapters.
128	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
129	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
130	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
131	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
132	
133	### Subagents
134	
135	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
136	
137	### Review Agent
138	
139	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
140	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
141	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
142	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
143	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
144	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
145	
146	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
147	
148	These run automatically between phases:
149	
150	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
151	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
152	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.

1	# Prover — Prover Stage
2	
3	You fill `sorry` placeholders with complete proofs in your assigned `.lean` file.
4	
5	## Workflow
6	
7	1. Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
8	2. Read `task_pending.md` for prior attempts, dead ends, relevant lemmas on your file.
9	3. Check the `.lean` file for `/- USER: ... -/` comments (file-specific user hints).
10	4. **Read the relevant blueprint chapter before writing Lean code.** The chapter holds the mathematical proof sketch you must align with. When stuck, re-reading the blueprint is often the fastest path forward.
11	5. Replace `sorry` with Lean proofs. Push as far as possible.
12	6. **Always save partial progress in the code.** If you can't fully close a sorry, leave your best attempt — commented-out steps, helper lemmas, partial `by` blocks with `sorry` at the stuck point. The file must still compile (use scoped `sorry` if needed), but your work must be visible for the next agent to continue from. NEVER revert to a bare `sorry` — that wastes the work.
13	7. Write your results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
14	
15	**Write permissions**: only your assigned `.lean` file(s) and `task_results/<your_file>.md`. Do NOT edit `PROGRESS.md`, `task_pending.md`, `task_done.md`, blueprint chapters, or other agents' files.
16	
17	## Protected declarations
18	
19	`archon-protected.yaml` lists declarations with **frozen signatures**. You may fill their proof bodies but must not rename, re-type, reorder arguments, or weaken hypotheses. Only the mathematician edits protected signatures.
20	
21	## Avoid early termination
22	
23	- Don't abandon a proof prematurely. Many complex proofs run to thousands of lines.
24	- Difficulty is NOT a valid reason to leave a `sorry`.
25	- Don't delegate to "the next iteration" or "another prover" if more effort could close it.
26	- Only modify the proof for your assigned task — leave unrelated proofs untouched.
27	- **Decompose**: break the proof into smaller sub-problems (following the blueprint's lemma structure when available: L1, L2, L3, …) and solve each individually until the entire goal closes.
28	
29	## Task completion criteria
30	
31	Your task is complete ONLY when ALL of:
32	
33	1. Every `sorry` in scope is replaced with a complete proof.
34	2. Zero axioms introduced.
35	3. The file compiles cleanly.
36	
37	## Never weaken the type to dodge the proof
38	
39	When the substantive type is unattainable this iter, you have one honest move: leave `sorry` with the **intended type signature** so the type itself encodes the claim. You must NOT substitute a tautologically-true type to trivialize the body. Three patterns are banned:
40	
41	- **Reflexive-iso placeholder.** Replacing a non-trivial `X ≅ Y` (or `Nonempty (X ≅ Y)`) target with `Nonempty (X ≅ X) := ⟨Iso.refl _⟩`. The type now asserts nothing; the docstring's "iter-N placeholder" disclosure does not undo the structural lie, and the blueprint's `\lean{...}` cross-reference will resolve to this hollow declaration.
42	- **`Classical.choice` body around an explicit witness.** Writing `Classical.choice (α := X) ⟨witness⟩` to dissolve `Type` vs `Prop` friction — the witness is unreachable through any unfold, so the def is indistinguishable from `Classical.choice ⟨any-other-X⟩` downstream. Either use `Classical.choose` on the existential chain (preserves the spec), or `Nonempty.some`-with-witness; or ship a typed `sorry`.
43	- **Empty-content `proof_wanted`.** `proof_wanted` discards the declaration post-elaboration — the named decl never enters the environment, so the blueprint's `\lean{...}` cross-reference breaks. Use `theorem name : T := by sorry` with the intended signature instead.
44	
45	**Litmus test**: if you `unfold` your declaration, does it expose the named substantive content (Kähler differential module, the explicit iso, …) or does it stop at `Classical.choice` / `Iso.refl _` / nothing? If the latter, the body is structurally vacuous — ship the typed `sorry` instead.
46	
47	## When infrastructure is missing
48	
49	Do NOT report "Mathlib lacks X" and stop. Before giving up:
50	
51	1. **Use the informal agent** (`.claude/tools/archon-informal-agent.py`): "Prove [goal] without using [missing infrastructure], only Mathlib." Even an imperfect sketch is valuable.
52	2. **Try the alternative** — formalize whatever the informal agent suggests.
53	3. **If you still can't**: write the alternative sketch to `informal/<theorem_name>.md` and record in your task result what you tried, why it failed, AND the alternative route you found. "I couldn't prove X, but here's approach Y that might work because Z" is far more useful than "infrastructure missing".
54	
55	When stuck more generally: break into smaller subgoals, search Mathlib more thoroughly, prove missing helpers yourself, try alternative strategies, re-read the blueprint, use Web Search for published proofs.
56	
57	**Impossibility vs difficulty**: technical difficulty → keep trying. Mathematical impossibility → immediately backtrack and document why.
58	
59	## Proof style
60	
61	- **Never modify working proofs** — if a declaration has no `sorry` and compiles, do not touch its body.
62	- Keep edits minimal; don't delete comments or change labels; don't add unrelated declarations.
63	- Helper lemmas you introduced may be modified if they turn out wrong.
64	- Add a concise comment above each helper lemma so reuse is easy.
65	- **`change` vs `show`** — `change` reshapes the goal up to defeq; `show` is purely display-level annotation. Using `show` where `change` is needed produces a linter warning. Default to `change` when in doubt.
66	
67	## Mathlib tags in PROGRESS.md
68	
69	The plan agent tags suggested lemmas:
70	
71	- `[verified]` — confirmed to exist.
72	- `[expected]` — guessed by naming convention. Quick `lean_local_search`; pivot if it doesn't exist.
73	- `[gap]` — verified NOT in Mathlib. Don't waste search time; formalize a workaround.
74	
75	## LSP MCP tools
76	
77	The `archon-lean-lsp` server exposes Lean LSP operations as **MCP tool calls** (full names like `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_goal` / `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_diagnostic_messages`). The lean4 skill reference uses short names (`lean_goal`, `lean_local_search`, …) — same tools, NOT shell binaries.
78	
79	- Always invoke through your tool-call interface, the same way as `Read` or `Grep`.
80	- **Never** call `Bash` with `lean_goal …` etc. — there is no such shell command.
81	- First LSP action: `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_diagnostic_messages` on your file. If it returns `success: false` the server is cold; retry once or run `lake build` once via Bash, then retry.
82	
83	## Search protocol
84	
85	Follow `references/lean-lsp-tools-api.md`. Key priority:
86	
87	1. `lean_local_search` first.
88	2. `lean_leansearch` for semantic search — describe the mathematical content, not just the name.
89	3. `lean_loogle` for simple type patterns only.
90	4. Never use shell `find` / `grep` to locate Mathlib theorems.
91	
92	## Tooling traps
93	
94	- **Trust `goals_after`, not just empty diagnostics**: in `lean_multi_attempt`, `diagnostics: []` can be misleading (line-scoping quirks attach errors to the enclosing `by` block). Check that `goals_after` is empty or has advanced.
95	- **Beware `lean_run_code` with imports**: standalone snippets can silently swallow elaboration errors when they contain imports. Rely on the actual file's `lean_diagnostic_messages` for authoritative verification.
96	
97	## Logging
98	
99	Write to `task_results/<your_file>.md` (mirror your `.lean` path: `Algebra/WLocal.lean` → `task_results/Algebra_WLocal.lean.md`):
100	
101	```markdown
102	# Algebra/WLocal.lean
103	
104	## wLocal_iff (line 45)
105	### Attempt 1
106	- **Approach:** Direct case split on maximal ideals
107	- **Result:** FAILED — needed IsLocalRing instance, not available
108	- **Dead end:** direct case split without IsLocalRing
109	
110	### Attempt 2
111	- **Approach:** Stacks 0A31, characterize via bijection on spectra
112	- **Result:** RESOLVED
113	- **Key insight:** `PrimeSpectrum.comap_injective` bridges the gap
114	
115	## helper_bijective (line 78)
116	### Attempt 1
117	- **Approach:** Split into injectivity + surjectivity; prove injectivity
118	- **Result:** PARTIAL (branch closed) — injectivity closed; surjectivity remains
119	- **Next step:** `PrimeSpectrum.range_comap_of_surjective` for the right branch
120	- **Lemmas found:** `PrimeSpectrum.comap_surjective`
121	```
122	
123	**Rules**: one section per theorem/lemma in your file. Each attempt records approach, result (RESOLVED / FAILED / PARTIAL / IN PROGRESS), dead-end warnings or next steps. Log negative search results ("Searched 'projective module infinite rank' — nothing in Mathlib"). The plan agent merges these into `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md` next iter.
124	
125	**Read-only context**: read `task_pending.md` (prior attempts on your file) and `task_done.md` (when the problem resembles a completed one). Do not write to either.
126	
127	## End-of-session handoff
128	
129	Before stopping:
130	
131	1. Write `task_results/<your_file>.md` with: current result, lemmas discovered, concrete next step, dead-end warnings.
132	2. Save all changes; ensure the file compiles (use scoped `sorry` for stuck parts if needed).

10	/-!
11	# Vanishing of `H¹` for skyscraper sheaves on a curve (RR.2.H¹)
12	
13	This file is the **RR.2.H¹** project-side build of the closed-point
14	skyscraper `H¹`-vanishing identity
15	
16	  `dim_{k̄} H¹(C, skyscraperSheaf P (ModuleCat.of k̄ k̄)) = 0`
17	
18	on a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve `C / k̄`. The file is
19	the iter-191 Lane H **file-skeleton**: each of the eight pinned
20	declarations carries the *intended* substantive type signature (matching
21	the blueprint `\lean{...}` pin in `chapters/RiemannRoch_H1Vanishing.tex`),
22	with `sorry` bodies; the iter-192+ closure follows the classical
23	Hartshorne III.2.5 flasque-vanishing argument.
24	
25	The chapter strategy (Hartshorne III §2): an injective resolution of a
26	flasque sheaf yields a flasque quotient at each stage; the
27	global-sections functor is exact on a flasque-to-flasque-to-flasque
28	short exact sequence; hence the right-derived global-sections functor
29	vanishes on a flasque input in positive degree. We specialise the
30	abstract flasque-vanishing to the closed-point skyscraper sheaf, which
31	is flasque because it is the pushforward, along the closed embedding of
32	a one-point subspace, of the constant sheaf on an irreducible base.
33	
34	## Eight pinned declarations
35	
36	1. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.IsFlasque` — predicate on
37	   `Sheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology X) (ModuleCat.{u} kbar)`.
38	2. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.IsFlasque.pushforward` — pushforward of a
39	   flasque sheaf is flasque (Hartshorne II.1, Ex. 1.16(d)).
40	3. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.IsFlasque.constant_of_irreducible` —
41	   constant sheaf on an irreducible space is flasque (Hartshorne
42	   II.1, Ex. 1.16(a)).
43	4. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.HModule_flasque_eq_zero` — flasque sheaves
44	   have zero `HModule` in positive degree (Hartshorne III.2.5).
45	5. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.skyscraperSheaf_eq_pushforward_const` —
46	   skyscraper sheaf is the pushforward of a constant sheaf along the
47	   closed embedding of the closure of the support point.
48	6. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PrimeDivisor.closure_isIrreducible` — the
49	   closure of the support point of a `PrimeDivisor` is irreducible.
50	7. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.skyscraperSheaf_isFlasque` —
51	   closed-point skyscraper sheaf is flasque.
52	8. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.H1_skyscraperSheaf_finrank_eq_zero` — the
53	   `RR.2.H¹` headline (`dim_{k̄} H¹(C, k(P)) = 0`), obtained by composing
54	   declarations 4 and 7.
55	
56	The eighth declaration also lives as a `private` typed-`sorry` helper at
57	`AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/RRFormula.lean`. The `private` modifier
58	mangles the internal name of the RRFormula copy, so the public
59	declaration in this file is the one resolved by the blueprint's
60	`\lean{...}` pin and by downstream consumers (`sync_leanok` keys on the
61	fully-qualified public name).
62	
63	## References
64	
65	Blueprint: `blueprint/src/chapters/RiemannRoch_H1Vanishing.tex`.
66	Source: Hartshorne, *Algebraic Geometry*, II.1, Exercise 1.16
67	(flasque sheaves), Exercise 1.17 (skyscraper sheaves);
68	III.2, Proposition 2.5 (flasque sheaves are acyclic).
69	-/

71	set_option autoImplicit false
72	
73	universe u v
74	
75	open CategoryTheory Limits TopologicalSpace TopCat
76	open scoped AlgebraicGeometry
77	
78	namespace AlgebraicGeometry
79	
80	/-! ## §1. Flasque sheaves of `kbar`-modules -/
81	
82	/-- **Flasque sheaf of `kbar`-modules on a topological space**
83	(Hartshorne II.1, Exercise 1.16; III.2, paragraph preceding Lemma 2.4).
84	
85	A sheaf `F : Sheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology X) (ModuleCat.{u} kbar)`
86	is **flasque** when, for every inclusion of opens `V ≤ U` in `X`, the
87	restriction map
88	`F.val.obj (op U) → F.val.obj (op V)` is surjective as a map of
89	`kbar`-modules.
90	
91	The predicate is read off the underlying presheaf of `kbar`-modules and
92	applies uniformly to the project's `ModuleCat kbar`-flavoured cohomology
93	pipeline (it does not depend on any topological-space hypothesis on `X`,
94	so the curve specialisation is by instance synthesis on
95	`C.left.toTopCat`).
96	
97	Blueprint reference: `def:isFlasque_sheaf`. -/
98	def Scheme.IsFlasque
99	    {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] {X : TopCat.{u}}
100	    (F : Sheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology X) (ModuleCat.{u} kbar)) : Prop :=
101	  ∀ ⦃U V : TopologicalSpace.Opens X⦄ (h : V ≤ U),
102	    Function.Surjective ((F.val.map (homOfLE h).op).hom)
103	
104	/-- **Pushforward of a flasque sheaf is flasque** (Hartshorne II.1,
105	Exercise 1.16(d)).
106	
107	For a continuous map of topological spaces `f : X ⟶ Y` and a flasque
108	sheaf `F` of `kbar`-modules on `X`, the pushforward sheaf `f _* F` is
109	flasque on `Y`. The reason is purely formal: the restriction map of
110	`f _* F` along `V ≤ U` in `Y` is, by definition of pushforward, the
111	restriction map of `F` along `f ⁻¹ V ≤ f ⁻¹ U` in `X`, which is
112	surjective by hypothesis on `F`.
113	
114	**iter-191 Lane H prover dispatch** — closed via the unfolding
115	`pushforward.map (homOfLE h).op = F.val.map ((Opens.map f).map (homOfLE h)).op`
116	(by `rfl`), so flasqueness of `F` along `(Opens.map f).map (homOfLE h)` gives
117	the conclusion. Blueprint reference: `lem:isFlasque_pushforward`. -/
118	theorem Scheme.IsFlasque.pushforward
119	    {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] {X Y : TopCat.{u}} (f : X ⟶ Y)
120	    {F : Sheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology X) (ModuleCat.{u} kbar)}
121	    (hF : Scheme.IsFlasque F) :
122	    Scheme.IsFlasque
123	      ((TopCat.Sheaf.pushforward (ModuleCat.{u} kbar) f).obj F) := by
124	  intro U V h
125	  exact hF ((Opens.map f).map (homOfLE h)).le
126	
127	/-- **Constant sheaf on an irreducible topological space is flasque**
128	(Hartshorne II.1, Exercise 1.16(a)).
129	
130	For an irreducible topological space `X`, every nonempty open is
131	connected and dense; the constant presheaf `U ↦ A` already satisfies the
132	sheaf condition, and its restriction maps are the identity on `A` (for
133	the nonempty branches) and factor through `0` (for the empty branch).
134	
135	**iter-191 Lane H file-skeleton** — Tier-3 honest typed sorry; closure
136	is iter-192+. Blueprint reference:
137	`lem:isFlasque_constant_irreducible`. -/
138	theorem Scheme.IsFlasque.constant_of_irreducible
139	    (kbar : Type u) [Field kbar] {X : TopCat.{u}}
140	    [IrreducibleSpace X] (A : ModuleCat.{u} kbar) :
141	    Scheme.IsFlasque
142	      ((constantSheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology X)
143	        (ModuleCat.{u} kbar)).obj A) := by
144	  sorry
145	
146	/-- **Injective sheaves have vanishing higher cohomology** (axiom-clean
147	helper, Hartshorne III §1).
148	
149	For a topological space `X` and an injective object `I` of the
150	`kbar`-module sheaf category, the `kbar`-flavoured derived
151	global-sections cohomology `HModule kbar I i` is the zero `kbar`-module
152	for every `i ≥ 1`. This is immediate from Mathlib's
153	`HasInjectiveDimensionLT` framework: `Injective I` gives
154	`HasInjectiveDimensionLT I 1` (Mathlib
155	`instHasInjectiveDimensionLTOfNatNatOfInjective`), and
156	`HasInjectiveDimensionLT.subsingleton` yields
157	`Subsingleton (Abelian.Ext Y I i)` for `i ≥ 1` and any `Y`; specialising
158	`Y` to the constant sheaf at `kbar` and applying
159	`Module.finrank_zero_of_subsingleton` closes the goal.
160	
161	**iter-192 Lane H prover dispatch** — closed axiom-clean. Used as the
162	`Ext^i(_, I) = 0` input inside the Hartshorne III.2.5 long-exact-sequence
163	chase for `HModule_flasque_eq_zero` below. -/
164	theorem Scheme.HModule_injective_finrank_eq_zero
165	    {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] {X : TopCat.{u}}
166	    [HasSheafify (Opens.grothendieckTopology X) (ModuleCat.{u} kbar)]
167	    [HasExt (Sheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology X) (ModuleCat.{u} kbar))]
168	    {I : Sheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology X) (ModuleCat.{u} kbar)}
169	    [Injective I] (i : ℕ) (hi : 1 ≤ i) :
170	    Module.finrank kbar (Scheme.HModule kbar I i) = 0 := by
171	  have hsub : Subsingleton (Scheme.HModule kbar I i) :=
172	    HasInjectiveDimensionLT.subsingleton I 1 i hi _
173	  exact Module.finrank_zero_of_subsingleton
174	
175	/-- **The canonical injective-embedding short exact sequence**
176	`0 → F → Injective.under F → cokernel(Injective.ι F) → 0` of sheaves of
177	`kbar`-modules (axiom-clean helper). The embedding `Injective.ι F` is
178	mono by `Injective.ι_mono`, the cokernel projection is epi by
179	construction, and the middle row is exact at `Injective.under F` by
180	`ShortComplex.exact_cokernel`. -/
181	noncomputable def Scheme.injectiveSES
182	    {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] {X : TopCat.{u}}
183	    [HasSheafify (Opens.grothendieckTopology X) (ModuleCat.{u} kbar)]
184	    [HasExt (Sheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology X) (ModuleCat.{u} kbar))]
185	    (F : Sheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology X) (ModuleCat.{u} kbar)) :
186	    CategoryTheory.ShortComplex
187	      (Sheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology X) (ModuleCat.{u} kbar)) :=
188	  ShortComplex.mk (Injective.ι F) (Limits.cokernel.π (Injective.ι F))
189	    (Limits.cokernel.condition (Injective.ι F))
190	
191	/-- The injective-embedding short complex is short exact. -/
192	theorem Scheme.injectiveSES_shortExact
193	    {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] {X : TopCat.{u}}
194	    [HasSheafify (Opens.grothendieckTopology X) (ModuleCat.{u} kbar)]
195	    [HasExt (Sheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology X) (ModuleCat.{u} kbar))]
196	    (F : Sheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology X) (ModuleCat.{u} kbar)) :
197	    (Scheme.injectiveSES F).ShortExact :=
198	  { exact := ShortComplex.exact_cokernel (Injective.ι F)
199	    mono_f := Injective.ι_mono F
200	    epi_g := Limits.coequalizer.π_epi }
201	
202	/-- **Generic LES vanishing lemma**: in an abelian category with enough Ext,
203	if `0 → S.X₁ → S.X₂ → S.X₃ → 0` is short exact with `S.X₂` injective and the
204	post-composition `Hom(X, S.X₂) → Hom(X, S.X₃)` is surjective, then
205	`Ext X S.X₁ 1` is the zero `Subsingleton`.
206	
207	This is the structural skeleton of the Hartshorne III.2.5 argument
208	specialised at degree 1: the long exact sequence
209	`Ext X S.X₂ 0 → Ext X S.X₃ 0 → Ext X S.X₁ 1 → Ext X S.X₂ 1`
210	collapses because the rightmost term vanishes (injectivity of `S.X₂`)
211	and the leftmost map is surjective by the `Hom`-level hypothesis. The
212	proof composes:
213	- `HasInjectiveDimensionLT.subsingleton` (injective ⇒ `Ext X S.X₂ 1 = 0`).
214	- `Abelian.Ext.covariant_sequence_exact₁` (any `x₁ ∈ Ext X S.X₁ 1` lifts
215	  to some `x₃ ∈ Ext X S.X₃ 0` via the connecting morphism).
216	- `Abelian.Ext.addEquiv₀` (identifies `Ext X S.X₃ 0` with `Hom(X, S.X₃)`).
217	- `comp_extClass_assoc` (the LES "complex" identity used to discharge
218	  `(Ext.mk₀ S.g).comp extClass = 0`).
219	
220	**iter-192 Lane H prover dispatch** — closed axiom-clean. Used to peel
221	off the `i = 1` case of `HModule_flasque_eq_zero` once the
222	`Hom(X, I) → Hom(X, G)`-surjectivity input (Hartshorne II Ex. 1.16(b))
223	is supplied for an injective resolution. -/
224	theorem ext_one_eq_zero_of_hom_surjective_of_injective
225	    {C : Type v} [Category.{u} C] [Abelian C] [HasExt C]
226	    (X : C) {S : ShortComplex C} (hS : S.ShortExact) [Injective S.X₂]
227	    (hsurj : Function.Surjective (fun (f : X ⟶ S.X₂) => f ≫ S.g))
228	    (x₁ : Abelian.Ext X S.X₁ 1) : x₁ = 0 := by
229	  have hinj_subs : Subsingleton (Abelian.Ext X S.X₂ 1) :=
230	    HasInjectiveDimensionLT.subsingleton S.X₂ 1 1 le_rfl _
231	  have hker : x₁.comp (Abelian.Ext.mk₀ S.f) (show (1 : ℕ) + 0 = 1 by omega) = 0 :=
232	    Subsingleton.elim _ _
233	  obtain ⟨x₃, hx₃⟩ :=
234	    Abelian.Ext.covariant_sequence_exact₁ X hS x₁ hker
235	      (n₀ := 0) (show 0 + 1 = 1 by omega)
236	  obtain ⟨y, hy⟩ := hsurj (Abelian.Ext.addEquiv₀ x₃)
237	  simp only at hy
238	  have hx3eq : Abelian.Ext.mk₀ (Abelian.Ext.addEquiv₀ x₃) = x₃ := by
239	    rw [← Abelian.Ext.addEquiv₀_symm_apply, AddEquiv.symm_apply_apply]
240	  have hx3_factored :
241	      x₃ = (Abelian.Ext.mk₀ y).comp (Abelian.Ext.mk₀ S.g)
242	        (show (0 : ℕ) + 0 = 0 by omega) := by
243	    rw [Abelian.Ext.mk₀_comp_mk₀, hy, hx3eq]
244	  rw [← hx₃, hx3_factored]
245	  rw [Abelian.Ext.comp_assoc (Abelian.Ext.mk₀ y) (Abelian.Ext.mk₀ S.g) hS.extClass
246	      (show (0 : ℕ) + 0 = 0 by omega) (show (0 : ℕ) + 1 = 1 by omega)
247	      (show (0 : ℕ) + 0 + 1 = 1 by omega)]
248	  have hSg_extClass_zero :
249	      (Abelian.Ext.mk₀ S.g).comp hS.extClass (show (0 : ℕ) + 1 = 1 by omega) = 0 := by
250	    have key := hS.comp_extClass_assoc (Y := S.X₁) (n := 0)
251	      (Abelian.Ext.mk₀ (𝟙 S.X₁)) (n' := 1) (h := show (1 : ℕ) + 0 = 1 by omega)
252	    rw [Abelian.Ext.comp_mk₀_id] at key
253	    exact key
254	  rw [hSg_extClass_zero, Abelian.Ext.comp_zero]
255	
256	/-- **Flasque sheaves have vanishing higher cohomology** (Hartshorne
257	III.2, Proposition 2.5).
258	
259	For a topological space `X` and a flasque sheaf `F` of `kbar`-modules on
260	`X`, the `kbar`-flavoured derived global-sections cohomology
261	`HModule kbar F i` is the zero `kbar`-module for every `i ≥ 1`. In
262	particular, `dim_{kbar} HModule kbar F 1 = 0`.
263	
264	The proof structure mirrors Hartshorne III §2 verbatim: embed `F` into
265	an injective `I` of the abelian sheaf category, form the quotient short
266	exact sequence `0 → F → I → G → 0` (Mathlib: `Scheme.injectiveSES`,
267	axiom-clean), observe that `G` inherits flasqueness from `F` and `I`
268	(the latter by Hartshorne III Lemma 2.4), read off the short exact
269	sequence on global sections from the flasque input
270	(Hartshorne II Ex. 1.16(b)), and apply the long exact sequence
271	(`Abelian.Ext.covariant_sequence_exact₁`) to get `HModule kbar F 1 = 0`
272	and a reduction `HModule kbar F i ≅ HModule kbar G (i - 1)` for `i ≥ 2`;
273	iteration closes the higher-degree cases.
274	
275	**iter-192 Lane H prover dispatch** — partial structural progress:
276	helpers `HModule_injective_finrank_eq_zero` (Ext of injective vanishes,
277	axiom-clean), `injectiveSES` and `injectiveSES_shortExact` (canonical
278	SES for `Injective.under`, axiom-clean) all landed. The full body still
279	requires the project-side Hartshorne II Ex. 1.16(b) and (c) inputs:
280	(b) `Γ(X, F) → Γ(X, I) → Γ(X, G) → 0` is exact when F is flasque,
281	(c) `G = cokernel(F → I)` is flasque when both `F` and `I` are flasque.
282	Both inputs are scheduled across iter-193+ via the `mathlib-build`
283	dispatch chain (~150-200 LOC each).
284	
285	Blueprint reference: `thm:H1_vanishing_flasque`. -/
286	theorem Scheme.HModule_flasque_eq_zero
287	    {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] {X : TopCat.{u}}
288	    [HasSheafify (Opens.grothendieckTopology X) (ModuleCat.{u} kbar)]
289	    [HasExt (Sheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology X) (ModuleCat.{u} kbar))]
290	    {F : Sheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology X) (ModuleCat.{u} kbar)}
291	    (hF : Scheme.IsFlasque F) (i : ℕ) (hi : 1 ≤ i) :

294	  -- with `G := cokernel(Injective.ι F)` (axiom-clean via `injectiveSES`).

305	/-! ## §2. Skyscraper sheaves are flasque -/
306	
307	/-- **Skyscraper sheaf as pushforward of a constant sheaf** (Hartshorne
308	II.1, Exercise 1.17).
309	
310	The closed-point skyscraper sheaf `skyscraperSheaf P A` on a topological
311	space `X` is naturally isomorphic to the pushforward, along the constant
312	map `PUnit → X` at `P`, of the constant sheaf with value `A` on
313	`PUnit`. The constant sheaf on `PUnit` is itself isomorphic to the
314	skyscraper sheaf at `PUnit.unit`, so this is the sheaf-level
315	counterpart of Mathlib's presheaf-level
316	`skyscraperPresheaf_eq_pushforward` (Mathlib snapshot `b80f227`
317	`Topology/Sheaves/Skyscraper.lean`).
318	
319	For our usage on a curve, `PUnit` plays the role of `closure {P}`
320	(which on a Noetherian space with `P` closed is a singleton, hence
321	homeomorphic to `PUnit`).
322	
323	**iter-191 Lane H file-skeleton** — Tier-3 honest typed sorry; closure
324	is iter-192+. Blueprint reference:
325	`lem:skyscraperSheaf_eq_pushforward`. -/
326	theorem Scheme.skyscraperSheaf_eq_pushforward_const
327	    (kbar : Type u) [Field kbar]
328	    {X : TopCat.{u}} (P : X)
329	    [∀ U : TopologicalSpace.Opens X, Decidable (P ∈ U)]
330	    [∀ U : TopologicalSpace.Opens (TopCat.of PUnit.{u + 1}),
331	      Decidable (PUnit.unit ∈ U)]

333	    Nonempty
334	      (skyscraperSheaf (C := ModuleCat.{u} kbar) P A ≅
335	        (TopCat.Sheaf.pushforward (ModuleCat.{u} kbar)
336	            (TopCat.ofHom (ContinuousMap.const (TopCat.of PUnit.{u + 1}) P))).obj
337	          ((constantSheaf
338	              (Opens.grothendieckTopology (TopCat.of PUnit.{u + 1}))
339	              (ModuleCat.{u} kbar)).obj A)) := by

341	
342	/-- **Closure of the support point of a `PrimeDivisor` is irreducible**.
343	
344	For any scheme `X` and any `P : X.PrimeDivisor`, the topological
345	closure of the singleton `{P.point}` is an irreducible subset of `X`.
346	This is a project-bespoke ancillary that holds in full generality: the
347	closure of any irreducible set in a topological space is irreducible,
348	and a singleton `{x}` is irreducible (it is nonempty and contains no
349	proper non-empty closed subsets).
350	
351	**iter-191 Lane H prover dispatch** — closed via
352	`isIrreducible_singleton.closure`. Blueprint reference:

355	    {X : Scheme.{u}} (P : X.PrimeDivisor) :
356	    IsIrreducible (closure ({P.point} : Set X)) :=

358	
359	/-- **The closed-point skyscraper sheaf is flasque**.
360	
361	For a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve `C / kbar` and a
362	prime divisor `P : C.left.PrimeDivisor` (equivalently, a closed point
363	on the curve), the closed-point skyscraper sheaf
364	`skyscraperSheaf P.point (ModuleCat.of kbar kbar)` is flasque as a
365	sheaf of `kbar`-modules on the underlying topological space of `C`.
366	
367	The proof originally was planned to compose the four lemma-blocks of this
368	chapter (`skyscraperSheaf_eq_pushforward_const`,
369	`PrimeDivisor.closure_isIrreducible`, `IsFlasque.constant_of_irreducible`,
370	`IsFlasque.pushforward`). The iter-191 prover dispatch takes a more direct
371	route: unfold `skyscraperSheaf.val = skyscraperPresheaf`, then on the
372	`p ∈ V` branch the restriction map is an `eqToHom` (hence iso, hence
373	surjective in `ConcreteCategory`); on the `p ∉ V` branch the codomain is
374	the terminal object of `ModuleCat kbar`, which is the zero object, hence
375	`Subsingleton` on the underlying type. Either branch closes the
376	`Function.Surjective` obligation. This bypasses (3) and (5).
377	
378	**iter-191 Lane H prover dispatch** — closed directly via
379	`skyscraperPresheaf_map`. Blueprint reference:
380	`lem:skyscraperSheaf_isFlasque`. -/
381	theorem Scheme.skyscraperSheaf_isFlasque
382	    {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]
383	    (C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))) [IsProper C.hom]
384	    [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]
385	    [GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom] [IsIntegral C.left]
386	    (P : C.left.PrimeDivisor)
387	    [∀ U : TopologicalSpace.Opens C.left, Decidable (P.point ∈ U)] :
388	    Scheme.IsFlasque
389	      (skyscraperSheaf (C := ModuleCat.{u} kbar) P.point
390	        (ModuleCat.of kbar kbar)) := by
391	  intro U V h
392	  change Function.Surjective
393	    (((skyscraperPresheaf P.point (ModuleCat.of kbar kbar)).map
394	      (homOfLE h).op).hom)
395	  by_cases hV : P.point ∈ V
396	  · -- restriction is `eqToHom`, hence an iso, hence surjective
397	    have heq : (skyscraperPresheaf P.point (ModuleCat.of kbar kbar)).obj
398	        (Opposite.op U) =
399	        (skyscraperPresheaf P.point (ModuleCat.of kbar kbar)).obj
400	        (Opposite.op V) := by
401	      simp [skyscraperPresheaf, h hV, hV]
402	    have hmap := skyscraperPresheaf_map P.point (ModuleCat.of kbar kbar)
403	      (i := (homOfLE h).op)
404	    rw [dif_pos hV] at hmap
405	    rw [hmap]
406	    have : IsIso (eqToHom heq) := inferInstance
407	    exact (ConcreteCategory.bijective_of_isIso (eqToHom heq)).2
408	  · -- codomain is zero (terminal in ModuleCat), so surjective trivially
409	    have hzero : Limits.IsZero
410	        ((skyscraperPresheaf P.point (ModuleCat.of kbar kbar)).obj
411	          (Opposite.op V)) := by
412	      simp [skyscraperPresheaf, hV]
413	      exact (terminalIsTerminal).isZero
414	    have : Subsingleton ((skyscraperPresheaf P.point
415	        (ModuleCat.of kbar kbar)).obj (Opposite.op V)) :=
416	      ModuleCat.subsingleton_of_isZero hzero
417	    intro y; exact ⟨0, Subsingleton.elim _ _⟩
418	
419	/-! ## §3. The closed-point skyscraper sheaf has vanishing `H¹` -/
420	
421	/-- **`H¹` of the closed-point skyscraper sheaf vanishes** (Hartshorne
422	III.2.5 applied to the flasque skyscraper).
423	
424	For a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve `C / kbar` and a
425	prime divisor `P : C.left.PrimeDivisor` (a closed point on the curve),
426	`dim_{kbar} H¹(C, skyscraperSheaf P (ModuleCat.of kbar kbar)) = 0`.

428	The proof composes the two substrate inputs of this chapter:
429	`skyscraperSheaf_isFlasque` (the skyscraper sheaf is flasque) and
430	`HModule_flasque_eq_zero` at `i = 1` (flasque ⇒ `H¹ = 0`); then
431	`Module.finrank kbar 0 = 0` closes the dimension identity.
432	
433	**iter-191 Lane H prover dispatch** — closed via composition of
434	`HModule_flasque_eq_zero` (still typed-`sorry` pending iter-192+
435	Hartshorne III.2.5 closure) and `skyscraperSheaf_isFlasque` (now closed
436	directly in this file) at `i = 1`. The same name also occurs as a
437	`private` typed-`sorry` helper at
438	`AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/RRFormula.lean`; the `private` modifier
439	mangles that copy's internal name, so the public name resolved by the
440	blueprint's `\lean{...}` pin (and by `sync_leanok`) is the declaration
441	below. Blueprint reference:
442	`lem:H1_skyscraperSheaf_finrank_eq_zero_main`. -/
443	theorem Scheme.H1_skyscraperSheaf_finrank_eq_zero
444	    {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]
445	    (C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))) [IsProper C.hom]
446	    [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]
447	    [GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom] [IsIntegral C.left]
448	    (P : C.left.PrimeDivisor)
449	    [∀ U : TopologicalSpace.Opens C.left, Decidable (P.point ∈ U)] :
450	    Module.finrank kbar
451	        (Scheme.HModule kbar
452	          (skyscraperSheaf (C := ModuleCat.{u} kbar) P.point
453	            (ModuleCat.of kbar kbar)) 1) = 0 :=
454	  Scheme.HModule_flasque_eq_zero (Scheme.skyscraperSheaf_isFlasque C P) 1 le_rfl
455
