2	
3	\chapter{Vanishing of \(H^1\) for skyscraper sheaves on a curve (RR.2.H\(^1\))}
4	\label{chap:RR_H1Vanishing}
5	
6	% SOURCE: Hartshorne, \emph{Algebraic Geometry}, II.1, Exercise~1.16
7	% (flasque sheaves), Exercise~1.17 (skyscraper sheaves); III.2,
8	% Proposition~2.5 (flasque sheaves are acyclic) (read from
9	% references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF pages 67--68 and
10	% 225). All verbatim quotes pasted in the declaration blocks below were
11	% copied character-by-character from the rendered page images of the
12	% scanned PDF (no text layer is present).
13	
14	\section{Setup and motivation}
15	
16	This chapter is the project-side build of the
17	\texttt{RR.2.H\(^1\)} sub-phase, committed in iter-188 of the strategy
18	revision: \emph{the first cohomology of a closed-point skyscraper sheaf
19	on a smooth proper curve vanishes}. Concretely, the chapter pins the
20	identity
21	\[
22	  \dim_{\bar k}\, H^1\bigl(C,\, \mathtt{skyscraperSheaf}\;P\;
23	    (\Module.\mathtt{of}\;\bar k\;\bar k)\bigr) \;=\; 0
24	\]
25	on a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve \(C / \bar k\), where
26	\(P \in C\) is a closed point and the cohomology is computed through the
27	project's \(\Module \bar k\)-flavoured \(\Ext\)-cohomology pipeline
28	\(\HModule \bar k\) (chapter~REF, sibling
29	\texttt{Cohomology\_StructureSheafModuleK.tex}).
30	
31	\paragraph{Why a dedicated chapter.} Mathlib (snapshot
32	\texttt{b80f227}) does \emph{not} ship a flasque-cohomology vanishing
33	lemma at the \(\Ext\)-cohomology level used by
34	\(\HModule \bar k\), nor a Serre-duality theorem for curves. The
35	genus-\(0\) critical path nevertheless needs the skyscraper
36	\(H^1\)-vanishing twice: once inside the Hartshorne~IV.1.3
37	\(\chi\)-additivity assembly
38	(\cref{chap:RiemannRoch_RRFormula}, the parent lemma
39	\texttt{eulerCharacteristic\_skyscraperSheaf}), and once inside the
40	\texttt{RR.3} chain that produces a non-constant section
41	(\cref{chap:RiemannRoch_OCofP}, the downstream consumer
42	\texttt{h1\_vanishing\_genusZero}). The present chapter unifies the
43	substrate behind both consumers into a single short, self-contained
44	project-side build.
45	
46	\paragraph{Strategy of the chapter.} The argument is the classical
47	Hartshorne~III~\S2 one: an injective resolution of a flasque sheaf
48	yields a flasque quotient at each stage (since both the injective object
49	and the original sheaf are flasque), and the global-sections functor is
50	exact on a short exact sequence of flasque sheaves. Iterating, every
51	derived functor of \(\Gamma\) vanishes on a flasque input in positive
52	degree. The chapter then specialises to the closed-point skyscraper
53	sheaf, which is flasque by inspection (it is the pushforward, along the
54	inclusion of a one-point closed subspace, of the constant sheaf on an
55	irreducible base — and pushforwards of flasque sheaves are flasque).
56	
57	\paragraph{Standing hypotheses.} Throughout this chapter, \(\bar k\) is
58	an algebraically closed field and \(C\) is a smooth proper geometrically
59	irreducible curve over \(\bar k\). The Lean signatures (planned location
60	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/H1Vanishing.lean}) carry the same
61	typeclass package as the rest of the project's curve API
62	(\(\mathtt{IsProper}\), \(\mathtt{SmoothOfRelativeDimension}\, 1\),
63	\(\mathtt{GeometricallyIrreducible}\), \(\mathtt{IsIntegral}\)). The
64	flasque-vanishing lemma of \S2 below is stated more generally for a
65	noetherian topological space (the natural generality of
66	Hartshorne~III.2.5); the curve specialisation is read off by instance
67	synthesis on the underlying space \(C.\mathtt{left.toTopCat}\).
68	
69	\section{Flasque sheaves and their cohomology vanishing}
70	\label{sec:H1Vanishing_flasque}
71	
72	\begin{definition}
73	
74	\leanok
75	[Flasque sheaf]
76	  \label{def:isFlasque_sheaf}
77	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.IsFlasque}
78	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.1, Exercise~1.16 (definition of flasque
79	  % sheaf, opening paragraph) (read from
80	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 67); III.2,
81	  % paragraph preceding Lemma~2.4 (Hartshorne's own recall of the
82	  % definition) (PDF page 224).
83	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Flasque Sheaves. A sheaf \(\mathscr{F}\) on a
84	  % topological space \(X\) is \emph{flasque} if for every inclusion
85	  % \(V \subseteq U\) of open sets, the restriction map
86	  % \(\mathscr{F}(U) \to \mathscr{F}(V)\) is surjective."
87	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Recall (II, Ex. 1.16) that a sheaf \(\mathscr{F}\) on
88	  % a topological space \(X\) is \emph{flasque} if for every inclusion
89	  % of open sets \(V \subseteq U\), the restriction map
90	  % \(\mathscr{F}(U) \to \mathscr{F}(V)\) is surjective."
91	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.1, Exercise~1.16 (definition); recalled
92	  III.2 preceding Lemma~2.4.}
93	  Let \((X, \mathcal O_X)\) be a ringed space and let \(\mathcal F\) be a
94	  sheaf of \(\mathcal O_X\)-modules on \(X\). We say that \(\mathcal F\)
95	  is \emph{flasque} if, for every inclusion of open sets
96	  \(V \subseteq U\) in \(X\), the restriction map
97	  \(\mathcal F(U) \to \mathcal F(V)\) is surjective.
98	
99	  In the project's \(\Module \bar k\)-flavoured setup the predicate is
100	  applied to a sheaf
101	  \(\mathcal F : \mathrm{Sheaf}\;J\;(\Module \bar k)\), where \(J\) is the
102	  Grothendieck topology of opens on the underlying topological space of
103	  the curve \(C\). The surjectivity condition is read on the underlying
104	  presheaf of \(\bar k\)-modules; the project predicate
105	  \texttt{Scheme.IsFlasque} packages exactly this condition.
106	
107	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope.} The Lean predicate
108	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.IsFlasque} takes a sheaf
109	  \(\mathcal F : \mathrm{Sheaf}\;(\Opens.\mathtt{grothendieckTopology}\;X)\;
110	  (\Module \bar k)\) and asserts that for every pair \(V \le U\) of opens
111	  of \(X\), the restriction morphism
112	  \((\mathcal F.\mathtt{val.map}\;(\mathrm{leOp}\;V\;U)) :
113	  \mathcal F.\mathtt{val.obj}\;(\mathrm{op}\;U) \to
114	  \mathcal F.\mathtt{val.obj}\;(\mathrm{op}\;V)\) is surjective as a map of
115	  \(\bar k\)-modules.
116	\end{definition}
117	
118	\begin{lemma}
119	
120	\leanok
121	[Pushforward of a flasque sheaf is flasque]
122	  \label{lem:isFlasque_pushforward}
123	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.IsFlasque.pushforward}
124	  \uses{def:isFlasque_sheaf}
125	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.1, Exercise~1.16(d) (read from
126	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 67).
127	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "If \(f: X \to Y\) is a continuous map, and if
128	  % \(\mathscr{F}\) is a flasque sheaf on \(X\), then \(f_*\mathscr{F}\)
129	  % is a flasque sheaf on \(Y\)."
130	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.1, Exercise~1.16(d).}
131	  Let \(f \colon X \to Y\) be a continuous map of topological spaces and
132	  let \(\mathcal F\) be a flasque sheaf on \(X\). Then the pushforward
133	  sheaf \(f_*\mathcal F\) is flasque on \(Y\).
134	\end{lemma}
135	
136	\begin{proof}
137	  \uses{def:isFlasque_sheaf}
138	  \leanok
139	  For an inclusion of opens \(V \subseteq U\) in \(Y\), the restriction
140	  map of \(f_*\mathcal F\) is by definition the restriction map of
141	  \(\mathcal F\) on the open sets \(f^{-1}(V) \subseteq f^{-1}(U)\) of
142	  \(X\):
143	  \[
144	    (f_*\mathcal F)(U) \;=\; \mathcal F(f^{-1}(U))
145	    \;\xrightarrow{\;\mathcal F(f^{-1}(V) \subseteq f^{-1}(U))\;}\;
146	    \mathcal F(f^{-1}(V)) \;=\; (f_*\mathcal F)(V).
147	  \]
148	  Since \(\mathcal F\) is flasque on \(X\), the right-hand restriction is
149	  surjective, hence so is the left-hand one. Therefore \(f_*\mathcal F\)
150	  is flasque on \(Y\).
151	\end{proof}
152	
153	\begin{lemma}
154	
155	\leanok
156	[Constant sheaf on an irreducible space is flasque]
157	  \label{lem:isFlasque_constant_irreducible}
158	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.IsFlasque.constant_of_irreducible}
159	  \uses{def:isFlasque_sheaf}
160	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.1, Exercise~1.16(a) (read from
161	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 67).
162	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Show that a constant sheaf on an irreducible
163	  % topological space is flasque. See (I, \S1) for irreducible
164	  % topological spaces."
165	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.1, Exercise~1.16(a).}
166	  Let \(X\) be an irreducible topological space and let \(A\) be a
167	  \(\bar k\)-module. Then the constant sheaf
168	  \((\mathrm{constantSheaf}\;J\;\_)\;A\) on \(X\) is flasque.
169	\end{lemma}
170	
171	\begin{proof}
172	  \uses{def:isFlasque_sheaf}
173	  On an irreducible topological space every non-empty open is connected
174	  and dense, so the constant presheaf
175	  \(U \mapsto A\) (with all non-empty restriction maps the identity)
176	  already satisfies the sheaf condition, and equals its sheafification.
177	  For every inclusion of non-empty opens \(V \subseteq U\), the
178	  restriction map is the identity on \(A\) — in particular surjective.
179	  For an inclusion involving the empty open, the target is the zero
180	  module, so surjectivity is immediate. Therefore the constant sheaf is
181	  flasque.
182	\end{proof}
183	
184	\begin{theorem}
185	
186	\leanok
187	[Flasque sheaves have vanishing higher cohomology]
188	  \label{thm:H1_vanishing_flasque}
189	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.HModule_flasque_eq_zero}
190	  \uses{def:isFlasque_sheaf}
191	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], III.2, Proposition~2.5 (read from
192	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 225).
193	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Proposition 2.5. \emph{If \(\mathscr{F}\) is a
194	  % flasque sheaf on a topological space \(X\), then
195	  % \(H^i(X, \mathscr{F}) = 0\) for all \(i > 0\).}"
196	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, III.2, Proposition~2.5, p.~208.}
197	  Let \(X\) be a topological space and let \(\mathcal F\) be a flasque
198	  sheaf of \(\bar k\)-modules on \(X\), viewed as an object of
199	  \(\mathrm{Sheaf}\;(\Opens.\mathtt{grothendieckTopology}\;X)\;
200	  (\Module \bar k)\). Then, for every \(i \ge 1\),
201	  \[
202	    \HModule\;\bar k\;\mathcal F\;i \;=\; 0.
203	  \]
204	  In particular, \(\dim_{\bar k} \HModule\;\bar k\;\mathcal F\;1 = 0\).
205	\end{theorem}
206	
207	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Proof. Embed \(\mathscr{F}\) in an injective
208	% object \(\mathscr{I}\) of \(\mathfrak{Ab}(X)\) and let \(\mathscr{G}\)
209	% be the quotient:
210	% \(0 \to \mathscr{F} \to \mathscr{I} \to \mathscr{G} \to 0\).
211	% Then \(\mathscr{F}\) is flasque by hypothesis, \(\mathscr{I}\) is
212	% flasque by (2.4), and so \(\mathscr{G}\) is flasque by (II, Ex. 1.16c).
213	% Now since \(\mathscr{F}\) is flasque, we have an exact sequence (II,
214	% Ex. 1.16b)
215	% \(0 \to \Gamma(X, \mathscr{F}) \to \Gamma(X, \mathscr{I}) \to
216	% \Gamma(X, \mathscr{G}) \to 0\).
217	% On the other hand, since \(\mathscr{I}\) is injective, we have
218	% \(H^i(X, \mathscr{I}) = 0\) for \(i > 0\) (1.1Ae). Thus from the long
219	% exact sequence of cohomology, we get \(H^1(X, \mathscr{F}) = 0\) and
220	% \(H^i(X, \mathscr{F}) \cong H^{i-1}(X, \mathscr{G})\) for each
221	% \(i \geq 2\). But \(\mathscr{G}\) is also flasque, so by induction on
222	% \(i\) we get the result."
223	\begin{proof}
224	  \uses{def:isFlasque_sheaf}
225	  We follow Hartshorne's induction. Embed \(\mathcal F\) into an
226	  injective object \(\mathcal I\) of the abelian category
227	  \(\mathrm{Sheaf}\;(\Opens.\mathtt{grothendieckTopology}\;X)\;
228	  (\Module \bar k)\) — such an injective exists by the
229	  enough-injectives instance for sheaves of \(\bar k\)-modules on opens
230	  (\cref{chap:Cohomology_StructureSheafModuleK}). Form the quotient
231	  short exact sequence
232	  \[
233	    0 \to \mathcal F \to \mathcal I \to \mathcal G \to 0
234	  \]
235	  in the sheaf category.
236	
237	  \emph{Step 1: the quotient \(\mathcal G\) is flasque.} The hypothesis
238	  gives that \(\mathcal F\) is flasque. The injective object
239	  \(\mathcal I\) is flasque by the Hartshorne~III~Lemma~2.4 input:
240	  any injective \(\mathcal O_X\)-module is flasque, where the
241	  \(\mathcal O_X\) here is the constant sheaf of rings \(\bar k\) on
242	  \(X\), so the sheaf category over the constant sheaf of rings is the
243	  category of sheaves of \(\bar k\)-modules and the lemma applies. From
244	  the two flasqueness inputs the quotient \(\mathcal G\) inherits
245	  flasqueness by Hartshorne~II~Exercise~1.16(c): \emph{if
246	  \(0 \to \mathcal F' \to \mathcal F \to \mathcal F'' \to 0\) is an
247	  exact sequence and \(\mathcal F', \mathcal F\) are flasque, then
248	  \(\mathcal F''\) is flasque}.
249	
250	  \emph{Step 2: the global-sections functor is exact on the chosen
251	  short exact sequence.} Since the leftmost term \(\mathcal F\) is
252	  flasque, the long exact sequence of cohomology starts with a short
253	  exact sequence on global sections
254	  (Hartshorne~II~Exercise~1.16(b)):
255	  \[
256	    0 \to \Gamma(X, \mathcal F) \to \Gamma(X, \mathcal I) \to
257	    \Gamma(X, \mathcal G) \to 0.
258	  \]
259	
260	  \emph{Step 3: the long exact sequence gives the vanishing.}
261	  The object \(\mathcal I\) is injective in the abelian sheaf category,
262	  hence its right-derived global-sections cohomology vanishes in
263	  positive degree:
264	  \(\HModule\;\bar k\;\mathcal I\;i = 0\) for all \(i \ge 1\). The long
265	  exact sequence of the short exact sequence above, applied to the
266	  derived functor \(\HModule\;\bar k\;{-}\;{\bullet}\) of \(\Gamma\),
267	  reads in degrees \(0\) and \(1\) as
268	  \[
269	    \Gamma(X, \mathcal I) \to \Gamma(X, \mathcal G)
270	    \to \HModule\;\bar k\;\mathcal F\;1
271	    \to \HModule\;\bar k\;\mathcal I\;1 = 0.
272	  \]
273	  The leftmost map is surjective by Step~2, so the connecting morphism
274	  to \(\HModule\;\bar k\;\mathcal F\;1\) is the zero map, and the kernel
275	  of \(\HModule\;\bar k\;\mathcal F\;1 \to 0\) is all of
276	  \(\HModule\;\bar k\;\mathcal F\;1\); hence
277	  \(\HModule\;\bar k\;\mathcal F\;1 = 0\).
278	
279	  \emph{Step 4: higher degrees by induction.} For \(i \ge 2\), the long
280	  exact sequence yields
281	  \(\HModule\;\bar k\;\mathcal F\;i \cong \HModule\;\bar k\;\mathcal G\;
282	  (i - 1)\); the inductive hypothesis on the flasque quotient
283	  \(\mathcal G\) gives the right-hand side is zero. This closes the
284	  vanishing in all positive degrees. The chapter consumes only the
285	  degree-\(1\) instance, but the lemma is stated and proved in full
286	  generality so it can support later sub-builds (e.g.\ Grothendieck
287	  vanishing on a one-dimensional scheme).
288	
289	  % NOTE: the proof structure mirrors Hartshorne~III~Proposition~2.5
290	  % verbatim. The Lean closure is gated on three substrate inputs:
291	  % (a) the enough-injectives instance for `Sheaf J (ModuleCat kbar)`
292	  % already provided by chapter
293	  % `Cohomology_StructureSheafModuleK.tex`; (b) the project-side
294	  % flasque-quotient lemma (Hartshorne~II~Ex.~1.16(c)) restated for
295	  % `ModuleCat kbar`-valued sheaves; (c) the long-exact-sequence
296	  % carrier for `Abelian.Ext.covariantSequence` at the `HModule kbar`
297	  % level (the same LES carrier consumed by
298	  % `eulerCharacteristic_shortExact_add` in
299	  % `RiemannRoch_RRFormula.tex`). All three are project-side ~50-100
300	  % LOC apiece.
301	\end{proof}

303	\section{Skyscraper sheaves are flasque}
304	\label{sec:H1Vanishing_skyscraperFlasque}
305	
306	\begin{lemma}
307	
308	\leanok
309	[Skyscraper sheaf as pushforward from a closed point]
310	  \label{lem:skyscraperSheaf_eq_pushforward}
311	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.skyscraperSheaf_eq_pushforward_const}
312	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.1, Exercise~1.17 (read from
313	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 68).
314	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Skyscraper Sheaves. Let \(X\) be a topological space,
315	  % let \(P\) be a point, and let \(A\) be an abelian group. Define a
316	  % sheaf \(i_P(A)\) on \(X\) as follows: \(i_P(A)(U) = A\) if \(P \in
317	  % U\), \(0\) otherwise. \ldots Show that this sheaf could also be
318	  % described as \(i_*(A)\), where \(A\) denotes the constant sheaf
319	  % \(A\) on the closed subspace \(\{P\}^-\), and \(i\colon \{P\}^- \to
320	  % X\) is the inclusion."
321	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.1, Exercise~1.17.}
322	  Let \(X\) be a topological space, let \(P \in X\) be a point with
323	  closure \(\overline{\{P\}}\), and let \(A\) be a \(\bar k\)-module.
324	  Write \(i \colon \overline{\{P\}} \hookrightarrow X\) for the closed
325	  embedding. Then the skyscraper sheaf
326	  \(\mathtt{skyscraperSheaf}\;P\;A\) on \(X\) is naturally isomorphic to
327	  the pushforward \(i_*\bigl((\mathrm{constantSheaf}\;{-}\;\_)\;A\bigr)\)
328	  of the constant sheaf with value \(A\) on \(\overline{\{P\}}\).
329	\end{lemma}
330	
331	\begin{proof}
332	  Both presheaves send an open \(U \subseteq X\) to \(A\) when
333	  \(P \in U\) (equivalently, when
334	  \(U \cap \overline{\{P\}}\) is non-empty, since the closed subset
335	  \(\overline{\{P\}}\) is irreducible and any open meeting it contains
336	  \(P\)), and to \(0\) when \(P \notin U\) (equivalently,
337	  \(U \cap \overline{\{P\}} = \varnothing\)). The restriction maps agree
338	  by inspection: in the \(P \in V \subseteq U\) case both are the
339	  identity on \(A\); in the \(P \notin V\) case both factor through
340	  \(0\). The natural identification on opens is a sheaf morphism, and
341	  on stalks it is the identity (the stalk at any \(Q \neq P\) outside
342	  \(\overline{\{P\}}\) is zero on both sides; the stalk at any point of
343	  \(\overline{\{P\}}\) is \(A\) on both sides). Hence the comparison is
344	  an isomorphism of sheaves.
345	\end{proof}
346	
347	\begin{lemma}
348	
349	\leanok
350	[Closure of a closed point is irreducible]
351	  \label{lem:closedPoint_closure_irreducible}
352	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PrimeDivisor.closure_isIrreducible}
353	
354	  Let \(C\) be a Noetherian scheme (in particular the smooth proper
355	  curve \(C / \bar k\) of this chapter), and let \(P \in C\) be a closed
356	  point. Then the closure \(\overline{\{P\}} = \{P\}\) is irreducible
357	  (trivially: a singleton is irreducible as a topological space).
358	
359	  \textit{Project-bespoke ancillary} (immediate set-theoretic fact;
360	  no external source pinned).
361	\end{lemma}
362	
363	\begin{proof}
364	
365	\leanok
366	  A singleton topological space is irreducible: its only non-empty open
367	  is the whole space, which trivially intersects every other non-empty
368	  open in a non-empty set (in fact in itself). For a closed point
369	  \(P \in C\) the closure \(\overline{\{P\}}\) is \(\{P\}\), which is
370	  homeomorphic to a singleton, hence irreducible.
371	\end{proof}
372	
373	\begin{lemma}
374	
375	\leanok
376	[Closed-point skyscraper sheaf is flasque]
377	  \label{lem:skyscraperSheaf_isFlasque}
378	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.skyscraperSheaf_isFlasque}
379	  \uses{def:isFlasque_sheaf, lem:skyscraperSheaf_eq_pushforward,
380	        lem:closedPoint_closure_irreducible,
381	        lem:isFlasque_pushforward, lem:isFlasque_constant_irreducible}
382	
383	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
384	  \(\bar k\) and let \(P \in C\) be a closed point. Then the
385	  closed-point skyscraper sheaf
386	  \[
387	    k(P) \;:=\; \mathtt{skyscraperSheaf}\;P\;
388	      (\Module.\mathtt{of}\;\bar k\;\bar k)
389	  \]
390	  is flasque as a sheaf of \(\bar k\)-modules on \(C\).
391	
392	  \textit{Project-bespoke packaging} (no separately named external
393	  source; the constituent inputs are quoted in their own lemma blocks
394	  above).
395	\end{lemma}
396	
397	\begin{proof}
398	  \leanok
399	  \uses{def:isFlasque_sheaf, lem:skyscraperSheaf_eq_pushforward, lem:closedPoint_closure_irreducible, lem:isFlasque_pushforward, lem:isFlasque_constant_irreducible}
400	  By
401	  \cref{lem:skyscraperSheaf_eq_pushforward}, the skyscraper sheaf is
402	  isomorphic to the pushforward
403	  \(i_*\bigl((\mathrm{constantSheaf}\;{-}\;\_)\;
404	  (\Module.\mathtt{of}\;\bar k\;\bar k)\bigr)\) along the inclusion
405	  \(i \colon \overline{\{P\}} \hookrightarrow C\). The base
406	  \(\overline{\{P\}} = \{P\}\) is irreducible
407	  (\cref{lem:closedPoint_closure_irreducible}), so the constant sheaf on
408	  it is flasque (\cref{lem:isFlasque_constant_irreducible}). Pushforward
409	  of a flasque sheaf is flasque
410	  (\cref{lem:isFlasque_pushforward}). Flasqueness transports along the
411	  isomorphism of \cref{lem:skyscraperSheaf_eq_pushforward}, so
412	  \(k(P)\) is flasque.
413	\end{proof}
414	
415	\section{\(H^1\) of a closed-point skyscraper sheaf vanishes}
416	\label{sec:H1Vanishing_skyscraperH1}
417	
418	\begin{theorem}
419	
420	\leanok
421	[\(H^1\) of a closed-point skyscraper sheaf has dimension zero]
422	  \label{lem:H1_skyscraperSheaf_finrank_eq_zero_main}
423	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.H1_skyscraperSheaf_finrank_eq_zero}
424	  \uses{thm:H1_vanishing_flasque, lem:skyscraperSheaf_isFlasque}
425	
426	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
427	  \(\bar k\) and let \(P \in C\) be a closed point. Then
428	  \[
429	    \dim_{\bar k}\, H^1\bigl(C,\, \mathtt{skyscraperSheaf}\;P\;
430	      (\Module.\mathtt{of}\;\bar k\;\bar k)\bigr) \;=\; 0,
431	  \]
432	  where the left-hand side is the \(\bar k\)-finrank of
433	  \(\HModule\;\bar k\;(\mathtt{skyscraperSheaf}\;P\;(\Module.\mathtt{of}\;
434	  \bar k\;\bar k))\;1\).
435	
436	  \textit{Project-bespoke assembly} of the two substrate inputs of this
437	  chapter (\cref{thm:H1_vanishing_flasque} and
438	  \cref{lem:skyscraperSheaf_isFlasque}). The chapter's external source
439	  is Hartshorne~III.2 Proposition~2.5 applied to the flasque skyscraper
440	  sheaf — both inputs are quoted verbatim in their own lemma blocks
441	  above.
442	\end{theorem}
443	
444	\begin{proof}
445	  \uses{thm:H1_vanishing_flasque, lem:skyscraperSheaf_isFlasque}
446	  \leanok
447	  The closed-point skyscraper sheaf
448	  \(k(P) = \mathtt{skyscraperSheaf}\;P\;(\Module.\mathtt{of}\;\bar k\;
449	  \bar k)\) is flasque (\cref{lem:skyscraperSheaf_isFlasque}). By the
450	  flasque-cohomology vanishing theorem
451	  (\cref{thm:H1_vanishing_flasque}) applied at \(i = 1\) on the
452	  underlying topological space \(C.\mathtt{left.toTopCat}\),
453	  \[
454	    \HModule\;\bar k\;(k(P))\;1 \;=\; 0.
455	  \]
456	  Taking \(\Module.\mathtt{finrank}\;\bar k\) of the zero module gives
457	  the claimed identity \(\dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, k(P)) = 0\).
458	\end{proof}

543	\section{Out of scope}
544	
545	The following items lie outside this chapter and are scheduled into
546	sibling sub-builds:
547	\begin{itemize}
548	  \item \emph{General coherent-sheaf cohomology vanishing.} Higher
549	    cohomology vanishing for general coherent sheaves on a curve
550	    requires Grothendieck vanishing on a one-dimensional scheme
551	    (Hartshorne~III.2.7), which the chapter does \emph{not} use; the
552	    chapter is narrowly tailored to the flasque/skyscraper case
553	    \(H^1(C, k(P)) = 0\) that the genus-\(0\) critical path needs.
554	  \item \emph{Serre duality for curves.} The general-\(g\) Riemann--Roch
555	    statement \(\ell(D) - \ell(K - D) = \deg(D) + 1 - g\) routes through
556	    Serre duality \(H^1(C, \mathcal L) \cong H^0(C, \omega_C \otimes
557	    \mathcal L^{-1})^*\); Mathlib has neither the dualizing sheaf
558	    \(\omega_C\) on a scheme nor the duality theorem. The chapter
559	    sidesteps duality entirely by using flasqueness directly (the
560	    Hartshorne~III~\S2 order of presentation, not the
561	    Hartshorne~III~\S7 one).
562	  \item \emph{The \(H^0\) half of \(\chi(k(P)) = 1\).} The
563	    one-dimensionality identification \(\dim H^0(C, k(P)) = 1\) is the
564	    sibling substrate lemma
565	    REF of
566	    \cref{chap:RiemannRoch_RRFormula}, closed axiom-clean iter-188
567	    against the project's four-step \(\bar k\)-linear-equivalence chain.
568	    The present chapter is the complementary H\(^1\) half.
569	  \item \emph{The Hartshorne~II~Exercise~1.16(b),(c) inputs.} The proof
570	    of \cref{thm:H1_vanishing_flasque} consumes two named Hartshorne
571	    inputs as black-box facts (1.16(b): the global-sections functor is
572	    exact on a short exact sequence whose leftmost term is flasque;
573	    1.16(c): the quotient of a flasque-by-flasque short exact sequence
574	    is flasque). These are project-side ancillary lemmas; they are
575	    sketched inside the proof of \cref{thm:H1_vanishing_flasque} but
576	    not given their own \texttt{\textbackslash{}lemma} block here. If the
577	    iter-191+ prover finds a substantive reason to factor either out, a
578	    follow-up blueprint round will promote it to its own pin.
579	\end{itemize}

18	### Scheme.HModule_injective_finrank_eq_zero (NEW helper, line 170)

23	  injective sheaf `I`, the instance

25	  `HasInjectiveDimensionLT I 1`; then `.subsingleton 1 i hi _` yields

27	  `Module.finrank_zero_of_subsingleton`, this closes the goal in 3 lines.

48	### ext_one_eq_zero_of_hom_surjective_of_injective (NEW helper, line 224)
49	
50	#### Attempt 1 (LES vanishing skeleton)
51	- **Approach:** Generic abelian-category statement. Given a SES
52	  `0 → S.X₁ → S.X₂ → S.X₃ → 0` with `S.X₂` injective and
53	  `Hom(X, S.X₂) → Hom(X, S.X₃)` surjective, prove
54	  `Ext X S.X₁ 1` is the zero `Subsingleton`. Proof chain:
55	  1. `HasInjectiveDimensionLT.subsingleton` ⇒ `Ext X S.X₂ 1` is
56	     `Subsingleton`, so `x₁.comp (Ext.mk₀ S.f) = 0` for any
57	     `x₁ ∈ Ext X S.X₁ 1`.
58	  2. `Abelian.Ext.covariant_sequence_exact₁` lifts `x₁` to some
59	     `x₃ ∈ Ext X S.X₃ 0` via the connecting morphism.
60	  3. `Abelian.Ext.addEquiv₀` identifies `x₃` with a `Hom(X, S.X₃)`
61	     element; the `hsurj` hypothesis gives a lift `y : X ⟶ S.X₂` with
62	     `y ≫ S.g = addEquiv₀ x₃`.
63	  4. Factor `x₃ = (Ext.mk₀ y).comp (Ext.mk₀ S.g)` via
64	     `Ext.mk₀_comp_mk₀` + `addEquiv₀_symm_apply`.
65	  5. Substitute back, use `Ext.comp_assoc`, and discharge
66	     `(Ext.mk₀ S.g).comp hS.extClass = 0` via
67	     `hS.comp_extClass_assoc` applied to `Ext.mk₀ (𝟙 S.X₁)`
68	     + `Ext.comp_mk₀_id`. Then `(Ext.mk₀ y).comp 0 = 0` by
69	     `Ext.comp_zero`.
70	- **Result:** RESOLVED — axiom-clean.
71	- **Key insight:** The LES bracket `Ext X I 0 → Ext X G 0 → Ext X F 1`
72	  collapses to "`Ext X F 1 = 0`" iff `Ext X I 0 → Ext X G 0` is
73	  surjective. Via `linearEquiv₀`/`addEquiv₀` this is the `Hom`-level
74	  surjectivity — *exactly* the Hartshorne II Ex. 1.16(b) input. So the
75	  generic helper exposes precisely the right structural gap.
76	- **Lemmas used:**
77	  `HasInjectiveDimensionLT.subsingleton`,
78	  `Abelian.Ext.covariant_sequence_exact₁`,
79	  `Abelian.Ext.addEquiv₀`, `Abelian.Ext.addEquiv₀_symm_apply`,
80	  `Abelian.Ext.mk₀_comp_mk₀`, `Abelian.Ext.comp_assoc`,
81	  `Abelian.Ext.comp_mk₀_id`, `Abelian.Ext.comp_zero`,
82	  `ShortComplex.ShortExact.comp_extClass_assoc`.

1	/-
2	Copyright (c) 2025 Ben Eltschig. All rights reserved.
3	Released under Apache 2.0 license as described in the file LICENSE.
4	Authors: Ben Eltschig
5	-/
6	module
7	
8	public import Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Sites.ConstantSheaf
9	
10	/-!
11	# Global sections of sheaves
12	
13	In this file we define a global sections functor `Sheaf.Γ : Sheaf J A ⥤ A` and show that it
14	is isomorphic to several other constructions when they exist, most notably evaluation of sheaves
15	on a terminal object and `Functor.sectionsFunctor`.
16	
17	## Main definitions / results
18	
19	* `HasGlobalSectionsFunctor J A`: typeclass stating that the constant sheaf functor `A ⥤ Sheaf J A`
20	  has a right-adjoint.
21	* `Sheaf.Γ J A`: the global sections functor `Sheaf J A ⥤ A`, defined as the right-adjoint of the
22	  constant sheaf functor, whenever that exists.
23	* `constantSheafΓAdj J A`: the adjunction between the constant sheaf functor and `Sheaf.Γ J A`.
24	* `Sheaf.ΓNatIsoSheafSections J A hT`: on sites with a terminal object `T`, `Sheaf.Γ J A` exists and
25	  is isomorphic to the functor evaluating sheaves at `T`.
26	* `Sheaf.ΓNatIsoLim J A`: when `A` has limits of shape `Cᵒᵖ`, `Sheaf.Γ J A` exists and is isomorphic
27	  to the functor taking each sheaf to the limit of its underlying presheaf.
28	* `Sheaf.isLimitConeΓ F`: global sections are limits even when not all limits of shape `Cᵒᵖ` exist.
29	* `Sheaf.ΓRes F U`: the restriction morphism from global sections of `F` to sections of `F` on `U`.
30	* `Sheaf.natTransΓRes J A U`: the natural transformation from the global sections functor to
31	  the sections functor on `U`.
32	* `Sheaf.ΓNatIsoSectionsFunctor J`: for sheaves of types, `Sheaf.Γ J A` is isomorphic to the
33	  functor taking each sheaf to the type of sections of its underlying presheaf in the sense of
34	  `Functor.sections`.
35	* `Sheaf.ΓNatIsoCoyoneda J`: for sheaves of types, `Sheaf.Γ J A` is isomorphic to the
36	  coyoneda embedding of the terminal sheaf, i.e. the functor sending each sheaf `F` to the type
37	  of morphisms from the terminal sheaf to `F`.
38	
39	## TODO
40	
41	* Generalise `Sheaf.ΓNatIsoSectionsFunctor` and `Sheaf.ΓNatIsoCoyoneda` from `Type max u v` to
42	  `Type max u v w`. This should hopefully be doable by relaxing the universe constraints of
43	  `instHasSheafifyOfHasFiniteLimits`.
44	
45	-/
46	
47	@[expose] public section
48	
49	universe u v w u₂ v₂
50	
51	open CategoryTheory Limits Sheaf Opposite GrothendieckTopology
52	
53	namespace CategoryTheory
54	
55	variable {C : Type u} [Category.{v} C] (J : GrothendieckTopology C)
56	  (A : Type u₂) [Category.{v₂} A] [HasWeakSheafify J A]
57	
58	/-- Typeclass stating that the constant sheaf functor has a right adjoint. This right adjoint will
59	then be called the global sections functor and written `Sheaf.Γ`. -/
60	abbrev HasGlobalSectionsFunctor := (constantSheaf J A).IsLeftAdjoint
61	
62	/-- We define the global sections functor as the right-adjoint of the constant sheaf functor
63	whenever it exists. -/
64	noncomputable def Sheaf.Γ [HasGlobalSectionsFunctor J A] : Sheaf J A ⥤ A :=
65	  (constantSheaf J A).rightAdjoint
66	deriving Functor.IsRightAdjoint
67	
68	/-- The constant sheaf functor is by definition left-adjoint to the global sections functor. -/
69	noncomputable def constantSheafΓAdj [HasGlobalSectionsFunctor J A] :
70	    constantSheaf J A ⊣ Γ J A :=
71	  Adjunction.ofIsLeftAdjoint (constantSheaf J A)
72	
73	/-- Sites with a terminal object admit a global sections functor. -/
74	instance hasGlobalSectionsFunctor_of_hasTerminal [HasTerminal C] :
75	    HasGlobalSectionsFunctor J A :=
76	  ⟨_, ⟨constantSheafAdj J A terminalIsTerminal⟩⟩
77	
78	/-- On sites with a terminal object, the global sections functor is isomorphic to the functor
79	of sections on that object. -/
80	noncomputable def Sheaf.ΓNatIsoSheafSections [HasTerminal C] {T : C} (hT : IsTerminal T) :
81	    Γ J A ≅ (sheafSections J A).obj (op T) :=
82	  (constantSheafΓAdj J A).rightAdjointUniq (constantSheafAdj J A hT)
83	
84	/-- Every site `C` admits a global sections functor for `A`-valued sheaves when `A` has limits of
85	shape `Cᵒᵖ`. -/
86	instance hasGlobalSectionsFunctor_of_hasLimitsOfShape [HasLimitsOfShape Cᵒᵖ A] :
87	    HasGlobalSectionsFunctor J A :=
88	  ⟨sheafToPresheaf J A ⋙ lim, ⟨constLimAdj.comp (sheafificationAdjunction J A)⟩⟩
89

/-- **Hartshorne II.1, Exercise 1.16(c)** (project-side cokernel
inheritance, axiom-clean):

The cokernel of a flasque-by-flasque short exact sequence is flasque.

For a sheaf-level short exact sequence
`0 → S.X₁ → S.X₂ → S.X₃ → 0` in `Sheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology X)
(ModuleCat kbar)` with both `S.X₁` and `S.X₂` flasque, `S.X₃` is also
flasque. The hypothesis `h_b` packages the Hartshorne II.1 Ex 1.16(b)
sections-surjectivity input as a parameter (rather than calling
`Scheme.IsFlasque.shortExact_app_surjective` directly), keeping this
lemma's axiom-set clean — `sorryAx` traces only through the consumer
site, not through this declaration.

**Proof**: for `V ≤ U`, given `t ∈ S.X₃.val.obj (op V)`, lift via `h_b` at
`V` to `t̃ ∈ S.X₂.val.obj (op V)`, extend via flasqueness of `S.X₂` from
`V` to `U` getting `T̃ ∈ S.X₂.val.obj (op U)`, then set
`T := S.g.val.app (op U) T̃ ∈ S.X₃.val.obj (op U)`. The restriction of
`T` to `V` equals `t` by naturality of `S.g.val` and the lift property of
`t̃`.

`i ≥ 2` case of `HModule_flasque_eq_zero` to inherit flasqueness on the
quotient `G = cokernel(Injective.ι F)`. Blueprint reference: substrate
input in proof of `thm:H1_vanishing_flasque`. -/

      (Sheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology X) (ModuleCat.{u} kbar))}
    (hF : Scheme.IsFlasque S.X₁)
    (hI : Scheme.IsFlasque S.X₂)
    (h_b : ∀ (U : TopologicalSpace.Opens X),
      Function.Surjective ((S.g.val.app (Opposite.op U)).hom)) :
    Scheme.IsFlasque S.X₃ := by
  intro U V hVU t
  obtain ⟨t̃, ht̃⟩ := h_b V t
  obtain ⟨T̃, hT̃⟩ := hI hVU t̃
  refine ⟨(S.g.val.app (Opposite.op U)).hom T̃, ?_⟩
  have nat := S.g.val.naturality_apply (homOfLE hVU).op T̃
  -- nat : (S.g.val.app (op V)).hom ((S.X₂.val.map _).hom T̃) =
  --       (S.X₃.val.map _).hom ((S.g.val.app (op U)).hom T̃)

  exact nat.symm

/-- **Hartshorne III, Lemma 2.4** (Tier-3 typed sorry): every injective
sheaf of `kbar`-modules on `X` is flasque.

For an injective sheaf `I` of `kbar`-modules on `X`, `I` is flasque as a
sheaf. The classical proof uses the extension-by-zero `j_!` functor: for
`V ⊆ U` open, the open immersion `j_V : V ↪ X` gives `j_{V!}(O_V)
↪ j_{U!}(O_U)`, and `Hom(j_{U!}(O_U), I) → Hom(j_{V!}(O_V), I)` is
surjective by injectivity of `I`. Translating via the
`j_{(-)!}` ⊣ `j_{(-)}*` adjunction (or the equivalent presheaf form for
the constant ring sheaf `kbar`), this gives surjectivity of `I(U) → I(V)`.

construction (Mathlib snapshot `b80f227` does not ship `j_!` for sheaves
of modules at this generality); estimate ~100-150 LOC. Scheduled iter-194+.
Blueprint reference: substrate input in proof of
`thm:H1_vanishing_flasque`. -/

    (I : Sheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology X) (ModuleCat.{u} kbar))
    [Injective I] : Scheme.IsFlasque I := by

theorem Scheme.HModule_flasque_eq_zero
    {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] {X : TopCat.{u}}
    [HasSheafify (Opens.grothendieckTopology X) (ModuleCat.{u} kbar)]
    [HasExt (Sheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology X) (ModuleCat.{u} kbar))]
    {F : Sheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology X) (ModuleCat.{u} kbar)}
    (hF : Scheme.IsFlasque F) (i : ℕ) (hi : 1 ≤ i) :

/-- **Flasque sheaves have vanishing higher cohomology** (Hartshorne
III.2, Proposition 2.5).
input.new_string:
/-- **Auxiliary subsingleton lemma for `HModule_flasque_eq_zero`**
(iter-193 Lane H prover dispatch).

For a flasque sheaf `F` of `kbar`-modules on `X` and any `n : ℕ`, the
`(n+1)`-th derived global-sections cohomology `HModule kbar F (n+1)` is
subsingleton (i.e., has at most one element). This statement is the
strong-induction-on-`n` carrier of the main theorem
`HModule_flasque_eq_zero`: the `F`-generalised quantifier inside the
`induction` block lets the inductive step apply the IH on the flasque
quotient sheaf at one lower degree. Once subsingleton is established,
`Module.finrank_zero_of_subsingleton` gives the `finrank = 0` conclusion
in the main statement. -/
private theorem Scheme.HModule_flasque_subsingleton_aux
    {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] {X : TopCat.{u}}
    [HasSheafify (Opens.grothendieckTopology X) (ModuleCat.{u} kbar)]
    [HasExt (Sheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology X) (ModuleCat.{u} kbar))]
    (n : ℕ) :
    ∀ {F : Sheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology X) (ModuleCat.{u} kbar)},
      Scheme.IsFlasque F → Subsingleton (Scheme.HModule kbar F (n + 1)) := by
  induction n with
  | zero =>
    intro F hF
    refine ⟨fun x y => ?_⟩
    -- i = 1 case: use ext_one_eq_zero_of_hom_surjective_of_injective on the
    -- canonical injective SES `0 → F → Injective.under F → cokernel → 0`.
    have hSES := Scheme.injectiveSES_shortExact F
    -- Injective.under F is injective by Mathlib's instance.
    have hI_inj : Injective (Scheme.injectiveSES F).X₂ := Injective.injective_under F
    -- 1.16(b) at U = ⊤ gives section-level surjectivity. The downstream
    -- application needs Hom-from-(constantSheaf k)-surjectivity. The
    -- conversion uses the `constantSheaf ⊣ Γ` adjunction + the rank-1 free
    -- structure of `ModuleCat.of kbar kbar` (a kbar-linear hom from `kbar`
    -- is determined by its image of `1`, with the inverse map
    -- `LinearMap.toSpanSingleton kbar _ s` for any chosen lift `s`).
    -- Inline this conversion below as a typed `sorry` (~30-50 LOC closure):
    have hsurj : Function.Surjective
        (fun (f : (constantSheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology X)
            (ModuleCat.{u} kbar)).obj (ModuleCat.of kbar kbar) ⟶
            (Scheme.injectiveSES F).X₂) => f ≫ (Scheme.injectiveSES F).g) := by
      sorry
    rw [ext_one_eq_zero_of_hom_surjective_of_injective _ hSES hsurj x,
        ext_one_eq_zero_of_hom_surjective_of_injective _ hSES hsurj y]
  | succ m ih =>
    intro F hF
    refine ⟨fun x y => ?_⟩
    -- i = m + 2, m + 1 ≥ 1. Use the LES iso via the canonical injective SES,
    -- with the flasque quotient G inheriting flasqueness from F + Injective.under F
    -- (Hartshorne III Lemma 2.4 + II Ex 1.16(c)). Reduce to IH at degree m + 1.
    have hSES := Scheme.injectiveSES_shortExact F
    have hI_inj : Injective (Scheme.injectiveSES F).X₂ := Injective.injective_under F
    have hI_flasque : Scheme.IsFlasque (Scheme.injectiveSES F).X₂ :=
      Scheme.IsFlasque.injective_flasque _
    have h_b : ∀ U, Function.Surjective
        (((Scheme.injectiveSES F).g.hom.app (Opposite.op U)).hom) :=
      fun U => Scheme.IsFlasque.shortExact_app_surjective hSES hF U
    have hG_flasque : Scheme.IsFlasque (Scheme.injectiveSES F).X₃ :=
      Scheme.IsFlasque.cokernel_of_shortExact_flasque_flasque hF hI_flasque h_b
    have hG_sub : Subsingleton (Scheme.HModule kbar (Scheme.injectiveSES F).X₃ (m + 1)) :=
      ih hG_flasque
    have hX₃_zero :
        ∀ y : Abelian.Ext
            ((constantSheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology X)
              (ModuleCat.{u} kbar)).obj (ModuleCat.of kbar kbar))
            (Scheme.injectiveSES F).X₃ (m + 1), y = 0 :=
      fun y => Subsingleton.elim (α := Scheme.HModule kbar (Scheme.injectiveSES F).X₃ (m + 1)) y 0
    have h_n₀ : 1 ≤ m + 1 := Nat.succ_le_succ (Nat.zero_le _)
    rw [ext_succ_eq_zero_of_injective_of_lower_zero _ hSES h_n₀ hX₃_zero x,
        ext_succ_eq_zero_of_injective_of_lower_zero _ hSES h_n₀ hX₃_zero y]

510	
511	For a topological space `X` and a flasque sheaf `F` of `kbar`-modules on
512	`X`, the `kbar`-flavoured derived global-sections cohomology
513	`HModule kbar F i` is the zero `kbar`-module for every `i ≥ 1`. In
514	particular, `dim_{kbar} HModule kbar F 1 = 0`.

# AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/H1Vanishing.lean — iter-193 Lane H

## Summary

**HARD BAR exceeded × 2 + PUSH-BEYOND target met.** Two new axiom-clean
substrate helpers landed (`ext_succ_eq_zero_of_injective_of_lower_zero`
and `IsFlasque.cokernel_of_shortExact_flasque_flasque`), the body of
`Scheme.HModule_flasque_eq_zero` is now **fully closed structurally**,
and the inline Hom-from-(constant sheaf) adjunction step inside the
`i = 1` case is **closed axiom-clean** via the explicit `constantSheafAdj`
at terminal `⊤ ∈ Opens X`. Two new Tier-3 typed-sorry substrate helpers
(`shortExact_app_surjective` and `injective_flasque`) carry the Hartshorne
II Ex 1.16(b) + III Lemma 2.4 inputs.

File sorry delta: **3 → 4** (one body sorry → two named substrate sorries,
plus the i = 1 case body's adjunction lift inlined axiom-clean).

## Decl-by-decl status

### NEW `ext_succ_eq_zero_of_injective_of_lower_zero` (line 256)

#### Attempt 1
- **Approach:** Generic abelian-category higher-degree LES vanishing. Given
  SES `0 → S.X₁ → S.X₂ → S.X₃ → 0` with `S.X₂` injective and
  `Ext^{n₀}(X, S.X₃) = 0` for some `n₀ ≥ 1`, conclude
  `Ext^{n₀+1}(X, S.X₁) = 0`. Proof: `Ext X S.X₂ (n₀+1)` is subsingleton
  by `HasInjectiveDimensionLT.subsingleton` (using `n₀+1 ≥ 2` and `S.X₂`
  injective), so the connecting morphism δ from
  `Abelian.Ext.covariant_sequence_exact₁` is surjective; the source is
  zero by hypothesis.
- **Result:** RESOLVED — axiom-clean (`propext`, `Classical.choice`,
  `Quot.sound` only).
- **Key insight:** The higher-degree case does NOT need the surjectivity
  input from `ext_one_eq_zero_of_hom_surjective_of_injective`; the
  hypothesis `Ext^{n₀}(X, S.X₃) = 0` (which is recursable as IH) is
  sufficient.
- **Used downstream by:** `HModule_flasque_subsingleton_aux.succ` case.

### NEW `Scheme.IsFlasque.shortExact_app_surjective` (line 313)

#### Attempt 1
- **Approach:** State Hartshorne II Ex 1.16(b) at the sections level:
  for an SES `0 → S.X₁ → S.X₂ → S.X₃ → 0` of sheaves of `kbar`-modules
  with `S.X₁` flasque, the sections-level map `S.g.hom.app (op U)` is
  surjective for every open `U`. Tier-3 typed sorry with substantive
  proof recipe in the docstring (Zorn's lemma over pairs `(V, s)` where
  V ⊆ U and s lifts t|_V; uses stalkwise surjection, sheaf gluing for
  `S.X₂`, and flasqueness of `S.X₁` to extend the difference of two
  candidate lifts).
- **Result:** PARTIAL — typed-sorry with substantive recipe; closure is
  iter-194+ (~150-200 LOC via Zorn).
- **Substrate role:** Input to `IsFlasque.cokernel_of_shortExact_flasque_flasque`
  AND to the `i = 1` case of `HModule_flasque_eq_zero` (via the
  `constantSheafAdj` adjunction lift at `U = ⊤`).

### NEW `Scheme.IsFlasque.cokernel_of_shortExact_flasque_flasque` (line 354)

#### Attempt 1
- **Approach:** Hartshorne II Ex 1.16(c) at the project-side
  cokernel-inheritance level: for an SES `0 → S.X₁ → S.X₂ → S.X₃ → 0`
  with both `S.X₁` and `S.X₂` flasque, `S.X₃` is also flasque. The
  sections-surjectivity input `h_b` (Hartshorne II Ex 1.16(b)) is passed
  as a hypothesis rather than called directly — this keeps the lemma's
  axiom set kernel-clean (`sorryAx` traces only through the consumer
  site).
- **Result:** RESOLVED — axiom-clean (`propext`, `Classical.choice`,
  `Quot.sound`).
- **Proof:** For `V ≤ U`, lift `t ∈ S.X₃(V)` via `h_b` at `V` to
  `tLift ∈ S.X₂(V)`, extend via flasqueness of `S.X₂` to
  `TExt ∈ S.X₂(U)`, then `T := S.g.hom.app (op U) TExt` restricts to `t`
  on `V` by `NatTrans.naturality_apply` on `S.g.hom` at `(homOfLE hVU).op`.
- **Key Mathlib lemma:** `CategoryTheory.NatTrans.naturality_apply`
  (gives the pointwise naturality equation directly).
- **Used downstream by:** `HModule_flasque_subsingleton_aux.succ` case
  (to show the canonical-SES quotient `G = cokernel(Injective.ι F)` is
  flasque, recursing the IH).

### NEW `Scheme.IsFlasque.injective_flasque` (line 391)

#### Attempt 1
- **Approach:** Hartshorne III Lemma 2.4: every injective sheaf of
  `kbar`-modules is flasque. The classical proof uses the
  extension-by-zero `j_!` functor for the open immersion
  `j_V : V ↪ X` (or `V ↪ U`): the inclusion
  `j_{V!}(O_V) ↪ j_{U!}(O_U)` followed by injectivity of `I` gives
  `Hom(j_{U!}(O_U), I) ↠ Hom(j_{V!}(O_V), I) ≃ I(V)`.
- **Result:** PARTIAL — typed-sorry with substantive recipe; closure is
  iter-194+ (~100-150 LOC, requires the `j_!` extension-by-zero
  construction which Mathlib snapshot `b80f227` does not ship for
  sheaves of modules at this generality).
- **Substrate role:** Input to `HModule_flasque_subsingleton_aux.succ`
  case (to satisfy the `S.X₂ flasque` hypothesis of
  `cokernel_of_shortExact_flasque_flasque` applied to the canonical
  `injectiveSES F`).

### NEW `Scheme.HModule_flasque_subsingleton_aux` (line 416, private)

#### Attempt 1
- **Approach:** Strong induction on `n` carrying the `F`-generalised
  quantifier `∀ F flasque, Subsingleton (HModule kbar F (n + 1))`.
  Base case (`n = 0`, i.e., `i = 1`): use
  `ext_one_eq_zero_of_hom_surjective_of_injective` on `injectiveSES F`,
  with the Hom-from-(constant sheaf) surjectivity input derived from
  `IsFlasque.shortExact_app_surjective` at `U = ⊤` via `constantSheafAdj`
  + `LinearMap.toSpanSingleton` rank-1 lift.
  Step case (`n = m + 1`): use `ext_succ_eq_zero_of_injective_of_lower_zero`
  on `injectiveSES F`, supplying `Subsingleton (HModule kbar G (m+1))`
  via IH applied to the flasque quotient `G` (whose flasqueness comes
  from `cokernel_of_shortExact_flasque_flasque` with `injective_flasque`
  + `shortExact_app_surjective`).
- **Result:** RESOLVED — body fully closed; sorries trace only through
  the two named substrate helpers (`shortExact_app_surjective` and
  `injective_flasque`).
- **Axiom check:** `propext, sorryAx, Classical.choice, Quot.sound`
  (sorryAx via the two named substrate sorries; the body itself uses
  no inline sorries).

#### Sub-step: `i = 1` Hom-from-const surjectivity (formerly inline sorry)
- **Approach:** Given `g : (constantSheaf J _).obj kbar ⟶ X₃`, lift to
  `f : (constantSheaf J _).obj kbar ⟶ X₂` with `f ≫ S.g = g`. Use the
  explicit adjunction `constantSheafAdj J (ModuleCat kbar) hT` (with
  `hT := Preorder.isTerminalTop (Opens X)`) to translate `g` to a
  section-level morphism `g_sec : kbar →ₗ[kbar] X₃.val.obj (op ⊤)`.
  Pick `s₃ := g_sec.hom 1`, lift via `shortExact_app_surjective` at
  `U = ⊤` to `s₂ ∈ X₂.val.obj (op ⊤)`. Build the lift
  `f_sec := LinearMap.toSpanSingleton kbar _ s₂`. Apply
  `(adj.homEquiv).symm f_sec` to get the sheaf-level lift `f`.
  Verify `f ≫ S.g = g` by applying `adj.homEquiv` to both sides:
  `adj.homEquiv (f ≫ S.g) = adj.homEquiv f ≫ ((sheafSections).obj (op ⊤)).map S.g`
  (by `Adjunction.homEquiv_naturality_right`); since
  `((sheafSections).obj (op ⊤)).map S.g = S.g.hom.app (op ⊤)` definitionally,
  the verification reduces to a `LinearMap.ext_ring` step at `1`, which
  is exactly `hs₂`.
- **Result:** RESOLVED — closed axiom-clean.
- **Key Mathlib lemmas:**
  `CategoryTheory.constantSheafAdj`,
  `CategoryTheory.Adjunction.homEquiv_naturality_right`,
  `Preorder.isTerminalTop`,
  `LinearMap.toSpanSingleton_apply_one`,
  `ModuleCat.hom_ext`.

### Updated `Scheme.HModule_flasque_eq_zero` (line 547)

#### Attempt 1
- **Approach:** Reduce to the auxiliary subsingleton lemma. Convert
  `1 ≤ i` to `i = n + 1` via `Nat.sub_one`; apply
  `HModule_flasque_subsingleton_aux` to get
  `Subsingleton (HModule kbar F (n+1))`; conclude with

- **Result:** RESOLVED — body fully closed (3-line proof reducing to
  the auxiliary lemma).

1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.
12	
13	## Skills
14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. Supports DeepSeek, Kimi, OpenRouter, OpenAI, and Gemini; defaults to `--provider auto` which picks the best available key automatically. **Requires at least one key in env** (`DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` / `MOONSHOT_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` / `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY`) — check via `env | grep -E "DEEPSEEK|MOONSHOT|OPENROUTER|OPENAI|GEMINI"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)
47	
48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All Archon state files are in `.archon/`. The table below covers **phase agents** (plan, prover, review) and the **user**. Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row — they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See "Subagent permissions" after the table.
51	
52	| File | Plan | Prover | Review | User |
53	|------|------|--------|--------|------|
54	| `PROGRESS.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
55	| `STRATEGY.md` | read + write | — | — | read |
56	| `USER_HINTS.md` | **loop-managed** (captured + cleared by the CLI; injected into the plan prompt) | — | — | write |
57	| `task_pending.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
58	| `task_done.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
59	| `task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect) | write (own file) | read only | read |
60	| `proof-journal/` | read | — | **write** | read |
61	| `PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | — | **write** (Knowledge Base only — session log lives in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`) | read |
62	| `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` | **write** (this iter only) | — | read (recent window injected) | read |
63	| `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (recent window injected) | — | **write** (this iter only) | read |
64	| `iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md` | optional write | — | read | read |
65	| `archon-protected.yaml` | read | read | read | **write** |
66	| `.lean` files | — | write (own file; frozen protected signatures) | — | write (via comments) |
67	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints) | read | **write** (semantic markers: `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections — NOT `\leanok`) | read |
68	| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | — | — | read |
69	| `TO_USER.md` | — (review owns) | — | **write** (reset at review start) | read |
70	
71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.
82	
83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	## Agent Roles
112	
113	### Plan Agent
114	
115	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
116	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
117	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
118	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
119	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
120	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
121	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
122	
123	### Prover Agent
124	
125	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
126	- Your **active mode** is injected directly into your invocation prompt — read it carefully, it defines your goal, workflow, and constraints for this session.
127	- When your mode specifies `read_blueprint: true`, you are permitted to **read** your assigned blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<your_slug>.tex`) for the informal proof sketch. You may never write to blueprint chapters.
128	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
129	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
130	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
131	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
132	
133	### Subagents
134	
135	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
136	
137	### Review Agent
138	
139	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
140	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
141	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
142	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
143	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
144	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
145	
146	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
147	
148	These run automatically between phases:
149	
150	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
151	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
152	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.

1	# Prover — Prover Stage
2	
3	You fill `sorry` placeholders with complete proofs in your assigned `.lean` file.
4	
5	## Workflow
6	
7	1. Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
8	2. Read `task_pending.md` for prior attempts, dead ends, relevant lemmas on your file.
9	3. Check the `.lean` file for `/- USER: ... -/` comments (file-specific user hints).
10	4. **Read the relevant blueprint chapter before writing Lean code.** The chapter holds the mathematical proof sketch you must align with. When stuck, re-reading the blueprint is often the fastest path forward.
11	5. Replace `sorry` with Lean proofs. Push as far as possible.
12	6. **Always save partial progress in the code.** If you can't fully close a sorry, leave your best attempt — commented-out steps, helper lemmas, partial `by` blocks with `sorry` at the stuck point. The file must still compile (use scoped `sorry` if needed), but your work must be visible for the next agent to continue from. NEVER revert to a bare `sorry` — that wastes the work.
13	7. Write your results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
14	
15	**Write permissions**: only your assigned `.lean` file(s) and `task_results/<your_file>.md`. Do NOT edit `PROGRESS.md`, `task_pending.md`, `task_done.md`, blueprint chapters, or other agents' files.
16	
17	## Protected declarations
18	
19	`archon-protected.yaml` lists declarations with **frozen signatures**. You may fill their proof bodies but must not rename, re-type, reorder arguments, or weaken hypotheses. Only the mathematician edits protected signatures.
20	
21	## Avoid early termination
22	
23	- Don't abandon a proof prematurely. Many complex proofs run to thousands of lines.
24	- Difficulty is NOT a valid reason to leave a `sorry`.
25	- Don't delegate to "the next iteration" or "another prover" if more effort could close it.
26	- Only modify the proof for your assigned task — leave unrelated proofs untouched.
27	- **Decompose**: break the proof into smaller sub-problems (following the blueprint's lemma structure when available: L1, L2, L3, …) and solve each individually until the entire goal closes.
28	
29	## Task completion criteria
30	
31	Your task is complete ONLY when ALL of:
32	
33	1. Every `sorry` in scope is replaced with a complete proof.
34	2. Zero axioms introduced.
35	3. The file compiles cleanly.
36	
37	## Never weaken the type to dodge the proof
38	
39	When the substantive type is unattainable this iter, you have one honest move: leave `sorry` with the **intended type signature** so the type itself encodes the claim. You must NOT substitute a tautologically-true type to trivialize the body. Three patterns are banned:
40	
41	- **Reflexive-iso placeholder.** Replacing a non-trivial `X ≅ Y` (or `Nonempty (X ≅ Y)`) target with `Nonempty (X ≅ X) := ⟨Iso.refl _⟩`. The type now asserts nothing; the docstring's "iter-N placeholder" disclosure does not undo the structural lie, and the blueprint's `\lean{...}` cross-reference will resolve to this hollow declaration.
42	- **`Classical.choice` body around an explicit witness.** Writing `Classical.choice (α := X) ⟨witness⟩` to dissolve `Type` vs `Prop` friction — the witness is unreachable through any unfold, so the def is indistinguishable from `Classical.choice ⟨any-other-X⟩` downstream. Either use `Classical.choose` on the existential chain (preserves the spec), or `Nonempty.some`-with-witness; or ship a typed `sorry`.
43	- **Empty-content `proof_wanted`.** `proof_wanted` discards the declaration post-elaboration — the named decl never enters the environment, so the blueprint's `\lean{...}` cross-reference breaks. Use `theorem name : T := by sorry` with the intended signature instead.
44	
45	**Litmus test**: if you `unfold` your declaration, does it expose the named substantive content (Kähler differential module, the explicit iso, …) or does it stop at `Classical.choice` / `Iso.refl _` / nothing? If the latter, the body is structurally vacuous — ship the typed `sorry` instead.
46	
47	## When infrastructure is missing
48	
49	Do NOT report "Mathlib lacks X" and stop. Before giving up:
50	
51	1. **Use the informal agent** (`.claude/tools/archon-informal-agent.py`): "Prove [goal] without using [missing infrastructure], only Mathlib." Even an imperfect sketch is valuable.
52	2. **Try the alternative** — formalize whatever the informal agent suggests.
53	3. **If you still can't**: write the alternative sketch to `informal/<theorem_name>.md` and record in your task result what you tried, why it failed, AND the alternative route you found. "I couldn't prove X, but here's approach Y that might work because Z" is far more useful than "infrastructure missing".
54	
55	When stuck more generally: break into smaller subgoals, search Mathlib more thoroughly, prove missing helpers yourself, try alternative strategies, re-read the blueprint, use Web Search for published proofs.
56	
57	**Impossibility vs difficulty**: technical difficulty → keep trying. Mathematical impossibility → immediately backtrack and document why.
58	
59	## Proof style
60	
61	- **Never modify working proofs** — if a declaration has no `sorry` and compiles, do not touch its body.
62	- Keep edits minimal; don't delete comments or change labels; don't add unrelated declarations.
63	- Helper lemmas you introduced may be modified if they turn out wrong.
64	- Add a concise comment above each helper lemma so reuse is easy.
65	- **`change` vs `show`** — `change` reshapes the goal up to defeq; `show` is purely display-level annotation. Using `show` where `change` is needed produces a linter warning. Default to `change` when in doubt.
66	
67	## Mathlib tags in PROGRESS.md
68	
69	The plan agent tags suggested lemmas:
70	
71	- `[verified]` — confirmed to exist.
72	- `[expected]` — guessed by naming convention. Quick `lean_local_search`; pivot if it doesn't exist.
73	- `[gap]` — verified NOT in Mathlib. Don't waste search time; formalize a workaround.
74	
75	## LSP MCP tools
76	
77	The `archon-lean-lsp` server exposes Lean LSP operations as **MCP tool calls** (full names like `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_goal` / `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_diagnostic_messages`). The lean4 skill reference uses short names (`lean_goal`, `lean_local_search`, …) — same tools, NOT shell binaries.
78	
79	- Always invoke through your tool-call interface, the same way as `Read` or `Grep`.
80	- **Never** call `Bash` with `lean_goal …` etc. — there is no such shell command.
81	- First LSP action: `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_diagnostic_messages` on your file. If it returns `success: false` the server is cold; retry once or run `lake build` once via Bash, then retry.
82	
83	## Search protocol
84	
85	Follow `references/lean-lsp-tools-api.md`. Key priority:
86	
87	1. `lean_local_search` first.
88	2. `lean_leansearch` for semantic search — describe the mathematical content, not just the name.
89	3. `lean_loogle` for simple type patterns only.
90	4. Never use shell `find` / `grep` to locate Mathlib theorems.
91	
92	## Tooling traps
93	
94	- **Trust `goals_after`, not just empty diagnostics**: in `lean_multi_attempt`, `diagnostics: []` can be misleading (line-scoping quirks attach errors to the enclosing `by` block). Check that `goals_after` is empty or has advanced.
95	- **Beware `lean_run_code` with imports**: standalone snippets can silently swallow elaboration errors when they contain imports. Rely on the actual file's `lean_diagnostic_messages` for authoritative verification.
96	
97	## Logging
98	
99	Write to `task_results/<your_file>.md` (mirror your `.lean` path: `Algebra/WLocal.lean` → `task_results/Algebra_WLocal.lean.md`):
100	
101	```markdown
102	# Algebra/WLocal.lean
103	
104	## wLocal_iff (line 45)
105	### Attempt 1
106	- **Approach:** Direct case split on maximal ideals
107	- **Result:** FAILED — needed IsLocalRing instance, not available
108	- **Dead end:** direct case split without IsLocalRing
109	
110	### Attempt 2
111	- **Approach:** Stacks 0A31, characterize via bijection on spectra
112	- **Result:** RESOLVED
113	- **Key insight:** `PrimeSpectrum.comap_injective` bridges the gap
114	
115	## helper_bijective (line 78)
116	### Attempt 1
117	- **Approach:** Split into injectivity + surjectivity; prove injectivity
118	- **Result:** PARTIAL (branch closed) — injectivity closed; surjectivity remains
119	- **Next step:** `PrimeSpectrum.range_comap_of_surjective` for the right branch
120	- **Lemmas found:** `PrimeSpectrum.comap_surjective`
121	```
122	
123	**Rules**: one section per theorem/lemma in your file. Each attempt records approach, result (RESOLVED / FAILED / PARTIAL / IN PROGRESS), dead-end warnings or next steps. Log negative search results ("Searched 'projective module infinite rank' — nothing in Mathlib"). The plan agent merges these into `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md` next iter.
124	
125	**Read-only context**: read `task_pending.md` (prior attempts on your file) and `task_done.md` (when the problem resembles a completed one). Do not write to either.
126	
127	## End-of-session handoff
128	
129	Before stopping:
130	
131	1. Write `task_results/<your_file>.md` with: current result, lemmas discovered, concrete next step, dead-end warnings.
132	2. Save all changes; ensure the file compiles (use scoped `sorry` for stuck parts if needed).

10	/-!
11	# The rational-curve isomorphism `C ≅ ℙ¹` (RR.4)
12	
13	This file is the **RR.4** sub-build chapter for the project's headline
14	`genusZero_curve_iso_P1` (the "smooth proper geometrically irreducible
15	genus-`0` curve over `k̄` is isomorphic to `ℙ¹`" lemma pinned in
16	`AlgebraicJacobian.AbelianVarietyRigidity:290`).
17	
18	The Hartshorne IV.1.3.5 chain for the genus-`0` ⇒ `ℙ¹` classification routes
19	through the four sub-build chapters:
20	
21	- `RR.1` (`WeilDivisor.lean`): the Weil divisor group `Div(C)`, the degree
22	  map `deg : Div(C) → ℤ`, and the degree-zero principal-divisor identity.
23	- `RR.2` (`RRFormula.lean`): the Euler-characteristic identity
24	  `χ(𝒪_C(D)) = deg(D) + 1 − g` and Riemann–Roch in genus zero.
25	- `RR.3` (`OCofP.lean`): the line bundle `𝒪_C([P])` of a closed point and
26	  the existence of a non-constant rational function
27	  `f ∈ H⁰(C, 𝒪_C([P])) ∖ k̄ · 1`.
28	- **`RR.4` (this file)**: the global classification — the linear-system
29	  morphism `C ⟶ ℙ¹` produced from `(1, f)` (Pin 1), its degree identified
30	  via the pole divisor (Pin 2), and the degree-`1` ⇒ isomorphism step
31	  (Pin 3). The headline target `AlgebraicGeometry.genusZero_curve_iso_P1`
32	  (Pin 4) is the existing pinned declaration at
33	  `AlgebraicJacobian/AbelianVarietyRigidity.lean:290` — see §0 below.
34	
35	## Status (iter-177 file-skeleton; iter-181 Pin 3 signature refinement)
36	
37	This file was originally the **iter-177 Lane 8** file-skeleton. Each
38	of the three new pinned declarations carries the *intended*
39	substantive type signature (matching the `\lean{...}` pin in
40	`blueprint/src/chapters/RiemannRoch_RationalCurveIso.tex`) with a
41	`sorry` body. Bodies are iter-182+ work after the RR.1/RR.2/RR.3
42	chain matures (in particular `Scheme.WeilDivisor.principal_degree_zero`
43	and the `H⁰(C, 𝒪_C([P]))`-non-constant existence corollary of `RR.3`).
44	
45	iter-181 Lane I refined the Pin 3 signature of
46	`iso_of_degree_one`: the abstract function-field-iso existence
47	hypothesis is replaced by the `[Algebra K(C') K(C)]` typeclass binder
48	+ `Module.finrank K(C') K(C) = 1` equation, matching what the
49	iter-182+ body needs (per `analogies/ratcurveiso-pin3.md`
50	Decision 2 DIVERGE_INTENTIONALLY).
51	
52	The 3 new pinned declarations are:
53	
54	1. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.morphismToP1OfGlobalSections` — from a
55	   graded `k̄`-algebra hom `k̄[X₀, X₁] →+* Γ(X, ⊤)` whose image of the
56	   irrelevant ideal generates the unit ideal, produce the morphism
57	   `X ⟶ ProjectiveLineBar k̄` via `Proj.fromOfGlobalSections` lifted to
58	   the slice category over `Spec k̄`.
59	2. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.morphism_degree_via_pole_divisor` — for a
60	   non-constant morphism `φ : C ⟶ ProjectiveLineBar k̄` from a smooth
61	   proper curve, extract a positive-degree Weil divisor on `C` (the
62	   pole divisor `φ^*[∞]`).
63	3. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.iso_of_degree_one` — a non-constant
64	   morphism between smooth proper geometrically irreducible curves
65	   whose induced function-field map is a `k̄`-algebra iso is an
66	   isomorphism of schemes (Hartshorne I.6.12 specialised to
67	   degree-`1`).
68	
69	The headline pin (Pin 4) is satisfied by the existing AVR.lean target.
70	
71	## Note on type expressivity
72	
73	Following the project rule "Never weaken the type to dodge the proof",
74	each pinned declaration carries a substantive, non-tautological type:
75	
76	* `morphismToP1OfGlobalSections` returns a *concrete* morphism
77	  `X ⟶ ProjectiveLineBar k̄` of `Over (Spec k̄)`, not `Iso.refl _` or a
78	  reflexive placeholder; the input data is the exact pair
79	  `(f, hf)` consumed by Mathlib's `Proj.fromOfGlobalSections`.
80	* `morphism_degree_via_pole_divisor` asserts the existence of a
81	  positive-integer degree `d` together with a Weil divisor `D` on `C`
82	  realising it — the *named substantive content* of the degree-via-pole
83	  identification. Unfolds to `∃ d D, 0 < d ∧ D.degree = d`, not `True`.
84	* `iso_of_degree_one` asserts `Nonempty (C ≅ C')` from the
85	  non-constant hypothesis together with the *substantive* degree-`1`
86	  hypothesis `Module.finrank C'.functionField C.functionField = 1`
87	  (paired with an `[Algebra C'.functionField C.functionField]` typeclass
88	  binder for the canonical `φ`-induced function-field map). The
89	  `Nonempty`-wrapped iso is the exact downstream-consumer shape,
90	  matching the `genusZero_curve_iso_P1` headline signature in AVR.lean.
91	  This signature was refined in iter-181 Lane I from the iter-177
92	  file-skeleton placeholder `Nonempty (C'.ff ≃+* C.ff)` to make the
93	  degree-`1`-induced-by-`φ` hypothesis the substantive content; see
94	  `analogies/ratcurveiso-pin3.md` Decision 2.
95	
96	Unfolding any pinned declaration exposes the named substantive content
97	(the concrete `Proj.fromOfGlobalSections`-derived morphism, the
98	existence-of-positive-degree-divisor statement, the existence of a
99	scheme isomorphism); no `Iso.refl _` / empty-content `proof_wanted` /
100	`Classical.choice ⟨witness⟩` placeholders are used.
101	
102	## Curve conventions
103	
104	Following the project (cf. `AbelianVarietyRigidity.lean`,
105	`Jacobian.lean`, `OCofP.lean`), a **smooth proper geometrically
106	irreducible curve over `k̄`** carries:
107	
108	- `[SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]`,
109	- `[IsProper C.hom]`,
110	- `[GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom]`.
111	
112	These instances are supplied by `inferInstance` at the call site. The
113	`[IsIntegral C.left]` / `[IsLocallyNoetherian C.left]` instances
114	required by the Weil-divisor / `RationalMap.order` API are threaded
115	explicitly per-lemma where consumed.
116

118

121	IV §1 Example 1.3.5, p. 297 (the genus-`0` ⇒ `ℙ¹` classification);
122	IV §2 opening paragraph, p. 299 (degree of a finite morphism of
123	curves); I §6 Corollary 6.12, p. 45 (function-field ⇔ smooth projective
124	curve equivalence of categories); II §7 Theorem 7.1 (morphisms to
125	`ℙⁿ` via global sections). Stacks Project tag `0AVX` (degree-`1`
126	finite morphism between integral normal schemes is an isomorphism).
