31	namespace AlgebraicGeometry
32	
33	variable (X : Scheme)
34	
35	/-- The function field of an irreducible scheme is the local ring at its generic point.
36	Despite the name, this is a field only when the scheme is integral. -/
37	noncomputable abbrev Scheme.functionField [IrreducibleSpace X] : CommRingCat :=
38	  X.presheaf.stalk (genericPoint X)
39	
40	/-- The restriction map from a component to the function field. -/
41	noncomputable abbrev Scheme.germToFunctionField [IrreducibleSpace X] (U : X.Opens)
42	    [h : Nonempty U] : Γ(X, U) ⟶ X.functionField :=
43	  X.presheaf.germ U
44	    (genericPoint X)
45	      (((genericPoint_spec X).mem_open_set_iff U.isOpen).mpr (by simpa using h))
46	
47	noncomputable instance [IrreducibleSpace X] (U : X.Opens) [Nonempty U] :
48	    Algebra Γ(X, U) X.functionField :=
49	  (X.germToFunctionField U).hom.toAlgebra
50	
51	noncomputable instance [IsIntegral X] : Field X.functionField :=
52	  (isField_stalk_of_closure_mem_irreducibleComponents X _
53	    (by simp [irreducibleComponents_eq_singleton])).toField
54	
55	theorem germ_injective_of_isIntegral [IsIntegral X] {U : X.Opens} (x : X) (hx : x ∈ U) :
56	    Function.Injective (X.presheaf.germ U x hx) := by
57	  rw [injective_iff_map_eq_zero]
58	  intro y hy
59	  rw [← (X.presheaf.germ U x hx).hom.map_zero] at hy
60	  obtain ⟨W, hW, iU, iV, e⟩ := X.presheaf.germ_eq _ hx hx _ _ hy
61	  cases Subsingleton.elim iU iV
62	  haveI : Nonempty W := ⟨⟨_, hW⟩⟩
63	  exact map_injective_of_isIntegral X iU e

content:
1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.
12	
13	## Skills
14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. Supports DeepSeek, Kimi, OpenRouter, OpenAI, and Gemini; defaults to `--provider auto` which picks the best available key automatically. **Requires at least one key in env** (`DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` / `MOONSHOT_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` / `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY`) — check via `env | grep -E "DEEPSEEK|MOONSHOT|OPENROUTER|OPENAI|GEMINI"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)
47	
48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All Archon state files are in `.archon/`. The table below covers **phase agents** (plan, prover, review) and the **user**. Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row — they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See "Subagent permissions" after the table.
51	
52	| File | Plan | Prover | Review | User |
53	|------|------|--------|--------|------|
54	| `PROGRESS.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
55	| `STRATEGY.md` | read + write | — | — | read |
56	| `USER_HINTS.md` | **loop-managed** (captured + cleared by the CLI; injected into the plan prompt) | — | — | write |
57	| `task_pending.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
58	| `task_done.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
59	| `task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect) | write (own file) | read only | read |
60	| `proof-journal/` | read | — | **write** | read |
61	| `PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | — | **write** (Knowledge Base only — session log lives in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`) | read |
62	| `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` | **write** (this iter only) | — | read (recent window injected) | read |
63	| `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (recent window injected) | — | **write** (this iter only) | read |
64	| `iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md` | optional write | — | read | read |
65	| `archon-protected.yaml` | read | read | read | **write** |
66	| `.lean` files | — | write (own file; frozen protected signatures) | — | write (via comments) |
67	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints) | read | **write** (semantic markers: `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections — NOT `\leanok`) | read |
68	| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | — | — | read |
69	| `TO_USER.md` | — (review owns) | — | **write** (reset at review start) | read |
70	
71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.
82	
83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	## Agent Roles
112	
113	### Plan Agent
114	
115	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
116	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
117	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
118	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
119	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
120	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
121	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
122	
123	### Prover Agent
124	
125	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
126	- Your **active mode** is injected directly into your invocation prompt — read it carefully, it defines your goal, workflow, and constraints for this session.
127	- When your mode specifies `read_blueprint: true`, you are permitted to **read** your assigned blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<your_slug>.tex`) for the informal proof sketch. You may never write to blueprint chapters.
128	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
129	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
130	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
131	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
132	
133	### Subagents
134	
135	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
136	
137	### Review Agent
138	
139	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
140	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
141	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
142	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
143	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
144	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
145	
146	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
147	
148	These run automatically between phases:
149	
150	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
151	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
152	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.
153

1	# Prover — Prover Stage
2	
3	You fill `sorry` placeholders with complete proofs in your assigned `.lean` file.
4	
5	## Workflow
6	
7	1. Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
8	2. Read `task_pending.md` for prior attempts, dead ends, relevant lemmas on your file.
9	3. Check the `.lean` file for `/- USER: ... -/` comments (file-specific user hints).
10	4. **Read the relevant blueprint chapter before writing Lean code.** The chapter holds the mathematical proof sketch you must align with. When stuck, re-reading the blueprint is often the fastest path forward.
11	5. Replace `sorry` with Lean proofs. Push as far as possible.
12	6. **Always save partial progress in the code.** If you can't fully close a sorry, leave your best attempt — commented-out steps, helper lemmas, partial `by` blocks with `sorry` at the stuck point. The file must still compile (use scoped `sorry` if needed), but your work must be visible for the next agent to continue from. NEVER revert to a bare `sorry` — that wastes the work.
13	7. Write your results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
14	
15	**Write permissions**: only your assigned `.lean` file(s) and `task_results/<your_file>.md`. Do NOT edit `PROGRESS.md`, `task_pending.md`, `task_done.md`, blueprint chapters, or other agents' files.
16	
17	## Protected declarations
18	
19	`archon-protected.yaml` lists declarations with **frozen signatures**. You may fill their proof bodies but must not rename, re-type, reorder arguments, or weaken hypotheses. Only the mathematician edits protected signatures.
20	
21	## Avoid early termination
22	
23	- Don't abandon a proof prematurely. Many complex proofs run to thousands of lines.
24	- Difficulty is NOT a valid reason to leave a `sorry`.
25	- Don't delegate to "the next iteration" or "another prover" if more effort could close it.
26	- Only modify the proof for your assigned task — leave unrelated proofs untouched.
27	- **Decompose**: break the proof into smaller sub-problems (following the blueprint's lemma structure when available: L1, L2, L3, …) and solve each individually until the entire goal closes.
28	
29	## Task completion criteria
30	
31	Your task is complete ONLY when ALL of:
32	
33	1. Every `sorry` in scope is replaced with a complete proof.
34	2. Zero axioms introduced.
35	3. The file compiles cleanly.
36	
37	## Never weaken the type to dodge the proof
38	
39	When the substantive type is unattainable this iter, you have one honest move: leave `sorry` with the **intended type signature** so the type itself encodes the claim. You must NOT substitute a tautologically-true type to trivialize the body. Three patterns are banned:
40	
41	- **Reflexive-iso placeholder.** Replacing a non-trivial `X ≅ Y` (or `Nonempty (X ≅ Y)`) target with `Nonempty (X ≅ X) := ⟨Iso.refl _⟩`. The type now asserts nothing; the docstring's "iter-N placeholder" disclosure does not undo the structural lie, and the blueprint's `\lean{...}` cross-reference will resolve to this hollow declaration.
42	- **`Classical.choice` body around an explicit witness.** Writing `Classical.choice (α := X) ⟨witness⟩` to dissolve `Type` vs `Prop` friction — the witness is unreachable through any unfold, so the def is indistinguishable from `Classical.choice ⟨any-other-X⟩` downstream. Either use `Classical.choose` on the existential chain (preserves the spec), or `Nonempty.some`-with-witness; or ship a typed `sorry`.
43	- **Empty-content `proof_wanted`.** `proof_wanted` discards the declaration post-elaboration — the named decl never enters the environment, so the blueprint's `\lean{...}` cross-reference breaks. Use `theorem name : T := by sorry` with the intended signature instead.
44	
45	**Litmus test**: if you `unfold` your declaration, does it expose the named substantive content (Kähler differential module, the explicit iso, …) or does it stop at `Classical.choice` / `Iso.refl _` / nothing? If the latter, the body is structurally vacuous — ship the typed `sorry` instead.
46	
47	## When infrastructure is missing
48	
49	Do NOT report "Mathlib lacks X" and stop. Before giving up:
50	
51	1. **Use the informal agent** (`.claude/tools/archon-informal-agent.py`): "Prove [goal] without using [missing infrastructure], only Mathlib." Even an imperfect sketch is valuable.
52	2. **Try the alternative** — formalize whatever the informal agent suggests.
53	3. **If you still can't**: write the alternative sketch to `informal/<theorem_name>.md` and record in your task result what you tried, why it failed, AND the alternative route you found. "I couldn't prove X, but here's approach Y that might work because Z" is far more useful than "infrastructure missing".
54	
55	When stuck more generally: break into smaller subgoals, search Mathlib more thoroughly, prove missing helpers yourself, try alternative strategies, re-read the blueprint, use Web Search for published proofs.
56	
57	**Impossibility vs difficulty**: technical difficulty → keep trying. Mathematical impossibility → immediately backtrack and document why.
58	
59	## Proof style
60	
61	- **Never modify working proofs** — if a declaration has no `sorry` and compiles, do not touch its body.
62	- Keep edits minimal; don't delete comments or change labels; don't add unrelated declarations.
63	- Helper lemmas you introduced may be modified if they turn out wrong.
64	- Add a concise comment above each helper lemma so reuse is easy.
65	- **`change` vs `show`** — `change` reshapes the goal up to defeq; `show` is purely display-level annotation. Using `show` where `change` is needed produces a linter warning. Default to `change` when in doubt.
66	
67	## Mathlib tags in PROGRESS.md
68	
69	The plan agent tags suggested lemmas:
70	
71	- `[verified]` — confirmed to exist.
72	- `[expected]` — guessed by naming convention. Quick `lean_local_search`; pivot if it doesn't exist.
73	- `[gap]` — verified NOT in Mathlib. Don't waste search time; formalize a workaround.
74	
75	## LSP MCP tools
76	
77	The `archon-lean-lsp` server exposes Lean LSP operations as **MCP tool calls** (full names like `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_goal` / `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_diagnostic_messages`). The lean4 skill reference uses short names (`lean_goal`, `lean_local_search`, …) — same tools, NOT shell binaries.
78	
79	- Always invoke through your tool-call interface, the same way as `Read` or `Grep`.
80	- **Never** call `Bash` with `lean_goal …` etc. — there is no such shell command.
81	- First LSP action: `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_diagnostic_messages` on your file. If it returns `success: false` the server is cold; retry once or run `lake build` once via Bash, then retry.
82	
83	## Search protocol
84	
85	Follow `references/lean-lsp-tools-api.md`. Key priority:
86	
87	1. `lean_local_search` first.
88	2. `lean_leansearch` for semantic search — describe the mathematical content, not just the name.
89	3. `lean_loogle` for simple type patterns only.
90	4. Never use shell `find` / `grep` to locate Mathlib theorems.
91	
92	## Tooling traps
93	
94	- **Trust `goals_after`, not just empty diagnostics**: in `lean_multi_attempt`, `diagnostics: []` can be misleading (line-scoping quirks attach errors to the enclosing `by` block). Check that `goals_after` is empty or has advanced.
95	- **Beware `lean_run_code` with imports**: standalone snippets can silently swallow elaboration errors when they contain imports. Rely on the actual file's `lean_diagnostic_messages` for authoritative verification.
96	
97	## Logging
98	
99	Write to `task_results/<your_file>.md` (mirror your `.lean` path: `Algebra/WLocal.lean` → `task_results/Algebra_WLocal.lean.md`):
100	
101	```markdown
102	# Algebra/WLocal.lean
103	
104	## wLocal_iff (line 45)
105	### Attempt 1
106	- **Approach:** Direct case split on maximal ideals
107	- **Result:** FAILED — needed IsLocalRing instance, not available
108	- **Dead end:** direct case split without IsLocalRing
109	
110	### Attempt 2
111	- **Approach:** Stacks 0A31, characterize via bijection on spectra
112	- **Result:** RESOLVED
113	- **Key insight:** `PrimeSpectrum.comap_injective` bridges the gap
114	
115	## helper_bijective (line 78)
116	### Attempt 1
117	- **Approach:** Split into injectivity + surjectivity; prove injectivity
118	- **Result:** PARTIAL (branch closed) — injectivity closed; surjectivity remains
119	- **Next step:** `PrimeSpectrum.range_comap_of_surjective` for the right branch
120	- **Lemmas found:** `PrimeSpectrum.comap_surjective`
121	```
122	
123	**Rules**: one section per theorem/lemma in your file. Each attempt records approach, result (RESOLVED / FAILED / PARTIAL / IN PROGRESS), dead-end warnings or next steps. Log negative search results ("Searched 'projective module infinite rank' — nothing in Mathlib"). The plan agent merges these into `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md` next iter.
124	
125	**Read-only context**: read `task_pending.md` (prior attempts on your file) and `task_done.md` (when the problem resembles a completed one). Do not write to either.
126	
127	## End-of-session handoff
128	
129	Before stopping:
130	
131	1. Write `task_results/<your_file>.md` with: current result, lemmas discovered, concrete next step, dead-end warnings.
132	2. Save all changes; ensure the file compiles (use scoped `sorry` for stuck parts if needed).

9	/-!
10	# Weil divisors on a smooth proper curve (RR.1)
11	
12	This file is the **RR.1** sub-build chapter for the project's headline
13	`genusZero_curve_iso_P1` (the "smooth proper geom-irred genus-`0` curve over `k̄` is
14	isomorphic to `ℙ¹`" lemma in `AlgebraicJacobian.AbelianVarietyRigidity`).
15	
16	Mathlib `b80f227` ships no `WeilDivisor` on a scheme; adjacent pieces
17	(`MeromorphicOn.divisor`, `CommRing.Pic`, `Scheme.RationalMap`) cover different
18	ground. This file is therefore **project-bespoke**, scaffolding the formal-sum data
19	type `Div(X) = ⨁_{Y ⊂ X codim 1} ℤ` on a Noetherian integral scheme `X` satisfying
20	Hartshorne's condition `(*)`, the principal-divisor homomorphism
21	`div : K(X)^× → Div(X)` on a curve, the degree map `deg : Div(C) → ℤ` on a smooth
22	proper curve over an algebraically closed field, the degree-zero of principal divisors
23	on a complete nonsingular curve (Hartshorne Cor. II.6.10), and the linear-equivalence
24	relation `D ~ D'`.
25	
26	## Status (iter-172 file-skeleton)
27	
28	This file is the **iter-172 Lane C** file-skeleton: each declaration carries the
29	intended signature (matching the blueprint `\lean{...}` pin) with a `sorry` body.
30	The bodies are iter-173+ work after the sibling chapters `RR.2`

37	2. `Scheme.RationalMap.order` — order of a rational function along a prime divisor.

39	4. `Scheme.WeilDivisor.degree` — degree map over an algebraically closed base.

60	namespace AlgebraicGeometry
61	
62	/-! ## §1. Codim-1 cycle group / Weil divisor group
63	
64	Hartshorne's condition `(*)` (II §6, p. 130) is: `X` is a Noetherian integral
65	separated scheme that is regular in codimension one. Under `(*)`, prime divisors
66	are closed integral subschemes of codimension one, and the local ring at the
67	generic point of a prime divisor is a DVR with quotient field the function field
68	`K(X)`.
69	
70	A prime divisor is encoded by its **generic point** together with the
71	codimension-one witness `Order.coheight point = 1`: the data field `point : X`
72	selects the generic point of the closed integral subscheme, and the predicate
73	field `coheight` enforces codimension one in the specialisation preorder
74	(Hartshorne II §6 p. 130; blueprint pin `def:prime_divisor`). Integrality of the
75	closure is automatic, so no separate integrality witness is needed.
76	-/
77	
78	/-- A **prime divisor** on a scheme `X`: a closed integral subscheme of codimension
79	one, encoded (for a scheme satisfying Hartshorne's condition `(*)`) by its generic
80	point. On a curve, prime divisors correspond bijectively to closed points.
81	
82	The codimension-one witness is the predicate `Order.coheight point = 1` on the
83	specialisation preorder of `X.carrier` (Mathlib's convention is
84	`x ≤ y ↔ y ⤳ x`, so the generic point is the unique maximal element and a
85	generic point of a codim-1 closed integral subscheme has a length-one chain
86	above it). The closure `{point}̄` is automatically irreducible (so the reduced
87	induced subscheme structure is automatically integral); hence no separate
88	integrality field is needed.
89	
90	Blueprint reference: `def:prime_divisor` / `def:codim1_cycles` (Hartshorne II §6
91	p. 130; Stacks 02RW). -/
92	structure Scheme.PrimeDivisor (X : Scheme.{u}) where
93	  /-- The generic point of the closed integral subscheme. -/
94	  point : X
95	  /-- Codimension-1 witness: `point` has coheight `1` in the specialisation
96	  preorder on `X.carrier`. Per iter-173 `wd-spec-refine` (`def:prime_divisor`). -/
97	  coheight : Order.coheight point = 1
98	
99	/-- The **Weil divisor group** of a scheme `X` satisfying Hartshorne's condition
100	`(*)`: the free abelian group on the set of prime divisors of `X`,
101	`Div(X) = ⨁_{Y prime divisor of X} ℤ · Y`. An element `D = Σ nᵢ · Yᵢ` with finitely
102	many nonzero coefficients is a **Weil divisor**; if all `nᵢ ≥ 0` it is **effective**.
103	
104	Blueprint reference: `def:codim1_cycles` (Hartshorne II §6 p. 130). -/
105	def Scheme.WeilDivisor (X : Scheme.{u}) : Type u := X.PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ
106	
107	namespace Scheme.WeilDivisor
108	
109	noncomputable instance (X : Scheme.{u}) : AddCommGroup X.WeilDivisor :=
110	  inferInstanceAs (AddCommGroup (X.PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ))
111	
112	instance (X : Scheme.{u}) : Inhabited X.WeilDivisor :=
113	  inferInstanceAs (Inhabited (X.PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ))
114	
115	end Scheme.WeilDivisor
116	
117	/-! ## §2. Order of a rational function at a prime divisor
118	
119	For a scheme `X` satisfying `(*)`, every prime divisor `Y` carries a discrete
120	valuation `v_Y` on the function field `K(X)`. The order of a nonzero rational
121	function `f ∈ K(X)^×` along `Y` is the integer `ord_Y(f) := v_Y(f)`. -/
122	
123	namespace Scheme.RationalMap
124	
125	/-- **Order of a nonzero rational function `f ∈ K(X)^×` along a prime divisor `Y`.**
126	
127	For `X` satisfying Hartshorne's `(*)`, the local ring `O_{X,η}` at the generic
128	point `η` of `Y` is a discrete valuation ring with quotient field the function
129	field `K(X)`, and `ord_Y(f) = v_Y(f) ∈ ℤ` is the value of the associated normalised
130	discrete valuation on `f`.
131	
132	On a smooth proper curve `C` over `k̄`, every closed point `P ∈ C` is a prime
133	divisor and `ord_P(f) = v_P(f)` is the standard DVR valuation at `P`.
134	
135	Blueprint reference: `def:order_at_point` (Hartshorne II §6 pp. 130–131; Stacks 02ME).
136	
137	iter-176 body (per analogist `dvr-rationalmap-order`): the body uses Mathlib's
138	`Ring.ordFrac` (the canonical `K →*₀ ℤᵐ⁰` monoid-with-zero hom from
139	`Mathlib.RingTheory.OrderOfVanishing.Basic`, Stacks `02MD`) on the stalk
140	`X.presheaf.stalk Y.point`, then projects through `WithZero.log : ℤᵐ⁰ → ℤ`
141	(the canonical projection with junk-on-zero, `Mathlib.Algebra.GroupWithZero.WithZero`).
142	On `f = 0` this gives `order Y 0 = 0` (junk convention from `WithZero.log_zero`).
143	
144	The required Mathlib typeclasses on the stalk are:
145	- `IsNoetherianRing` — from `[IsLocallyNoetherian X]`.
146	- `IsDomain` ⟹ `Nontrivial` — from `[IsIntegral X]`.
147	- `IsFractionRing (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point) X.functionField` — from `[IsIntegral X]`.
148	- `Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)` — threaded explicitly (the
149	  topological-coheight-to-Krull-dim bridge `Order.coheight Y.point = 1 ⟹
150	  Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)` is a Mathlib-upstream-pending
151	  gap; see Stacks `02IZ` / `005X`). -/
152	noncomputable def order {X : Scheme.{u}} [IsIntegral X]
153	    [IsLocallyNoetherian X] (Y : X.PrimeDivisor)
154	    [Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)]
155	    (f : X.functionField) : ℤ :=
156	  WithZero.log (Ring.ordFrac (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point) f)
157	
158	end Scheme.RationalMap
159	
160	/-! ### Regular-in-codimension-one bridge class
161	
162	Hartshorne's condition `(*)` includes "regular in codimension one", i.e.\ every
163	prime divisor `Y` of `X` has a DVR stalk `O_{X,Y.point}`. Mathlib has no direct
164	typeclass for this; we package it as the project-bespoke class
165	`Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne`, with an instance synthesising the per-`Y`
166	Krull-dim-≤-1 condition required by `Scheme.RationalMap.order`. Blueprint pin
167	("Iter-173+ may introduce a `Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne` predicate to
168	abbreviate this"; chapter `RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor.tex` §2 "Standing hypothesis
169	`(*)` in the Lean encoding"). -/
170	
171	/-- Project-bespoke class encoding Hartshorne's "regular in codimension one"
172	clause of `(*)`: every prime divisor `Y` of `X` has a DVR stalk
173	`O_{X,Y.point}`. The `[IsIntegral X]` precondition makes
174	`IsDomain (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)` available so that the
175	`IsDiscreteValuationRing` predicate is well-formed
176	(`AlgebraicGeometry.instIsDomainCarrierStalkCommRingCatPresheafOfIsIntegral`). -/
177	class Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne (X : Scheme.{u}) [IsIntegral X] : Prop where
178	  /-- The defining content: every prime divisor's stalk is a discrete valuation
179	  ring. (This is the precise content of Hartshorne's `(*)`; the weaker
180	  `Ring.KrullDimLE 1` derives via the `IsDiscreteValuationRing` ⟹
181	  `IsPrincipalIdealRing` ⟹ `Ring.KrullDimLE 1` chain via the bridge
182	  instance below.) -/
183	  out : ∀ Y : Scheme.PrimeDivisor X, IsDiscreteValuationRing (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)
184	
185	/-- Bridge instance: from `[Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne X]`, typeclass
186	synthesis can derive `IsDiscreteValuationRing (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)` for
187	every prime divisor `Y`. -/
188	instance Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne.instIsDiscreteValuationRingStalk
189	    {X : Scheme.{u}} [IsIntegral X] [Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne X]
190	    (Y : Scheme.PrimeDivisor X) :
191	    IsDiscreteValuationRing (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point) :=
192	  Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne.out Y
193	
194	/-- Bridge instance: from `[Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne X]`, typeclass
195	synthesis can derive `Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)` for every
196	prime divisor `Y`, via the `IsDiscreteValuationRing` ⟹ `IsPrincipalIdealRing`
197	⟹ `Ring.KrullDimLE 1` chain (Mathlib's `IsDiscreteValuationRing.toIsPrincipalIdealRing`
198	+ `IsPrincipalIdealRing.krullDimLE_one`). -/
199	instance Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne.instKrullDimLEStalk
200	    {X : Scheme.{u}} [IsIntegral X] [Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne X]
201	    (Y : Scheme.PrimeDivisor X) :
202	    Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point) :=
203	  haveI : IsDiscreteValuationRing (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point) :=
204	    Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne.out Y
205	  haveI : IsPrincipalIdealRing (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point) :=
206	    IsDiscreteValuationRing.toIsPrincipalIdealRing
207	  IsPrincipalIdealRing.krullDimLE_one _
208	
209	/-- **Hartshorne II.6.1**: for a nonzero rational function `f` on a Noetherian
210	integral scheme `X` satisfying `(*)`, the order function `Y ↦ ord_Y(f)` is
211	nonzero at only finitely many prime divisors `Y`. This is the well-definedness
212	side condition for `Scheme.WeilDivisor.principal`.
213	
214	iter-177 status: this packages Hartshorne's Lemma 6.1, which Mathlib does not
215	ship. The body is a Mathlib-upstream-pending gap (Stacks tag `02RV` — for a
216	nonzero element `f ∈ K(X)^×` of a Noetherian integral scheme, only finitely
217	many height-one primes can divide either numerator or denominator); the proof
218	factors through `IsLocallyNoetherian X` + the principal-ideal generation of
219	height-1 primes + the finite irreducible-component decomposition of
220	`V(f₀) ∪ V(f∞)`. The chapter pins this as a separate sub-build deferral
221	(`RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor.tex` §5).
222	
223	The statement is generic in `f` (no `f ≠ 0` hypothesis is threaded): on
224	`f = 0` the function `Y ↦ ord_Y(0) = WithZero.log 0 = 0` has empty support,
225	which is finite, so the conclusion holds vacuously. -/
226	private theorem rationalMap_order_finite_support {X : Scheme.{u}} [IsIntegral X]
227	    [IsLocallyNoetherian X] [Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne X]
228	    (f : X.functionField) :
229	    (Function.support (fun Y : X.PrimeDivisor =>
230	      Scheme.RationalMap.order Y f)).Finite := by
231	  -- **iter-192 case split + f = 0 branch closed axiom-clean.**
232	  --
233	  -- Case 1 (f = 0): the order function evaluates to
234	  -- `WithZero.log (Ring.ordFrac _ 0) = WithZero.log 0 = 0` at every
235	  -- prime divisor, so the support is empty (finite vacuously).
236	  by_cases hf : f = 0
237	  · subst hf
238	    convert Set.finite_empty
239	    ext Y
240	    simp only [Function.mem_support, ne_eq, Set.mem_empty_iff_false, iff_false,
241	      Decidable.not_not, Scheme.RationalMap.order, map_zero, WithZero.log_zero]
242	  -- Case 2 (f ≠ 0): genuinely needs Hartshorne II.6.1 (Stacks 02RV).
243	  -- The proof for nonzero `f` requires the substrate
244	  -- "for a Noetherian integral scheme, only finitely many height-1
245	  -- primes can divide the numerator or denominator of a nonzero rational
246	  -- function" — a Mathlib-pending gap noted in the chapter docstring.
247	  -- Body owed iter-193+; iter-192 closes only the f = 0 vacuous case.
248	  · sorry
249	
250	namespace Scheme.WeilDivisor
251	
252	variable {X : Scheme.{u}}
253	
254	/-! ## §3. Divisor of a closed point on a curve
255	
256	On a smooth proper curve `C` over a field, every closed point `P ∈ C` is a prime
257	divisor (it is closed, integral, of codimension one in the one-dimensional integral
258	scheme `C`). The associated Weil divisor is `[P] = 1 · P ∈ Div(C)`. -/
259	
260	/-- **The Weil divisor associated to a closed point `P` on a curve.** The element
261	`[P] := 1 · P ∈ Div(C)`, i.e. the prime divisor `P` with multiplicity one.
262	
263	For a smooth proper curve every closed point is automatically a prime divisor (it
264	is a codimension-one integral closed subscheme of the one-dimensional integral
265	scheme `C`); conversely every prime divisor on a curve is of this form, so an
266	arbitrary divisor on `C` is a finite formal `ℤ`-linear combination
267	`Σ nᵢ · [Pᵢ]` of closed points.
268	
269	Blueprint reference: `def:divisor_closed_point` (Hartshorne II §6 p. 137).
270	
271	iter-174 body: the function is junk-defined outside its intended scope. We
272	case-split on `Order.coheight P = 1` (the codimension-one witness of
273	`PrimeDivisor`). On the branch where the equality holds — automatic for a
274	closed point on a one-dimensional integral scheme — we promote `P` to a
275	`PrimeDivisor` via the witness and return `Finsupp.single ⟨P, h⟩ 1`, i.e.
276	the prime divisor `P` with multiplicity one. On the off-branch (junk regime)
277	we return the zero divisor. The blueprint pins the well-definedness argument
278	"`IsClosed {P}` on a one-dimensional integral scheme ⟹ `coheight P = 1`" as a
279	separate threadable hypothesis at the call site (chapter L330–L340 "Lean
280	signature scope"); see `ofClosedPoint_eq_single` for the equation in the
281	hypothesised regime. -/
282	noncomputable def ofClosedPoint {C : Scheme.{u}} (P : C)
283	    (_hP : IsClosed ({P} : Set C)) : C.WeilDivisor :=
284	  if h : Order.coheight P = 1 then Finsupp.single ⟨P, h⟩ 1 else 0
285	
286	/-- In the hypothesised regime where the closed point `P` has coheight one
287	(the codim-1 condition automatic for a closed point of a one-dimensional
288	integral scheme), `ofClosedPoint P hP` is the prime divisor `P` with
289	multiplicity one. -/
290	lemma ofClosedPoint_eq_single {C : Scheme.{u}} (P : C)
291	    (hP : IsClosed ({P} : Set C)) (h : Order.coheight P = 1) :
292	    ofClosedPoint P hP = Finsupp.single ⟨P, h⟩ 1 := by
293	  simp [ofClosedPoint, h]
294	
295	/-- Off-branch: outside the codim-1 regime, `ofClosedPoint` is junk-defined as
296	the zero divisor. (Only relevant when the user supplies a "closed point" that
297	does not have coheight one in the ambient scheme — e.g. a generic point or a
298	codim-≥2 point on a higher-dimensional scheme.) -/
299	lemma ofClosedPoint_eq_zero {C : Scheme.{u}} (P : C)
300	    (hP : IsClosed ({P} : Set C)) (h : Order.coheight P ≠ 1) :
301	    ofClosedPoint P hP = 0 := by
302	  simp [ofClosedPoint, h]
303	
304	/-! ## §4. Degree of a divisor over an algebraically closed base -/
305	
306	/-- **Degree of a Weil divisor on a smooth proper curve over `k̄`.**
307	
308	Over an algebraically closed field `k̄`, every closed point of a smooth proper
309	curve `C` has residue field `k̄`, so each prime divisor `[P]` contributes degree
310	one to the sum, and `deg(D) := Σᵢ nᵢ` is the sum of the integer coefficients of
311	the formal sum `D = Σ nᵢ · [Pᵢ]`.
312	
313	(Over a general field `k` one weights by the residue-field degrees
314	`Σᵢ nᵢ · [κ(Pᵢ) : k]` to recover the geometric degree; the project's RR bridge
315	needs only the `k̄` specialisation.)
316	
317	Blueprint reference: `def:divisor_degree` (Hartshorne II §6 p. 137; Stacks 0BE0).
318	
319	Implementation: `Finsupp.sum D (fun _ n => n)` is the sum of all coefficients of
320	the finitely supported function representing `D`. -/
321	noncomputable def degree (D : X.WeilDivisor) : ℤ :=
322	  (D : X.PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ).sum (fun _ n => n)
323	
324	/-- **The degree map is a group homomorphism `Div(C) → ℤ`.**
325	
326	`deg(D₁ + D₂) = deg(D₁) + deg(D₂)`, `deg(-D) = -deg(D)`, `deg(0) = 0`. Bundled
327	as an `AddMonoidHom` for downstream use (the linear-equivalence quotient
328	`Cl(C) := Div(C) / im(div)` will inherit a `deg : Cl(C) → ℤ` from this).
329	
330	Blueprint reference: `thm:divisor_degree_hom` (immediate from `def:divisor_degree`).
331	
332	Implementation: built from `Finsupp.liftAddHom (fun _ ↦ AddMonoidHom.id ℤ)`, the
333	generic Mathlib packaging that lifts a family of `AddMonoidHom`s indexed by the
334	support into a single `AddMonoidHom` on the finsupp. Unfolds to
335	`D.sum (fun _ z ↦ z) = degree D` (see `degree_hom_apply` below). -/
336	noncomputable def degree_hom : X.WeilDivisor →+ ℤ :=
337	  Finsupp.liftAddHom (fun _ ↦ AddMonoidHom.id ℤ)
338	
339	@[simp]
340	lemma degree_hom_apply (D : X.WeilDivisor) : degree_hom D = degree D :=
341	  Finsupp.liftAddHom_apply (α := X.PrimeDivisor) (M := ℤ) (N := ℤ)
342	    (fun _ ↦ AddMonoidHom.id ℤ) D
343	
344	/-! ## §5. Principal divisors -/
345	
346	/-- **The principal divisor of a nonzero rational function `f ∈ K(X)^×`.**
347	
348	By Hartshorne's Lemma 6.1, `ord_Y(f) = 0` for all but finitely many prime
349	divisors `Y`, so the formal sum
350	`div(f) := Σ_{Y prime divisor} ord_Y(f) · Y ∈ Div(X)`
351	has finite support and is a well-defined Weil divisor.
352	
353	On a smooth proper curve `C` over `k̄`, this specialises to
354	`div(f) = Σ_{P closed point} ord_P(f) · [P]`.
355	
356	Blueprint reference: `def:principal_divisor` (Hartshorne II §6 Lemma 6.1 +
357	following definition, p. 131).
358	
359	iter-177 body: the construction uses `Finsupp.ofSupportFinite` with the
360	finite-support witness `rationalMap_order_finite_support`. The latter is a
361	private theorem packaging Hartshorne 6.1; its body is a Mathlib-pending gap
362	(see chapter `RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor.tex` §5 sub-build note) and is left as
363	a `sorry` for an iter-178+ Mathlib-upstream PR. -/
364	noncomputable def principal [IsIntegral X] [IsLocallyNoetherian X]
365	    [Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne X] (f : X.functionField)
366	    (_hf : f ≠ 0) : X.WeilDivisor :=
367	  Finsupp.ofSupportFinite
368	    (fun Y : X.PrimeDivisor => Scheme.RationalMap.order Y f)
369	    (rationalMap_order_finite_support f)
370	
371	/-- **The principal-divisor map is a group homomorphism `K(X)^× → Div(X)`.**
372	
373	Concretely `div(fg) = div(f) + div(g)`, `div(f⁻¹) = -div(f)`, `div(1) = 0`. Bundled
374	as a `MonoidHom` from the multiplicative units of `K(X)` to `Multiplicative (Div(X))`
375	(equivalently: an additive map `(K(X)^×, ·) → (Div(X), +)`).
376	
377	Blueprint reference: `thm:principal_hom` (Hartshorne II §6 p. 131).
378	
379	iter-177 body: closes coordinate-wise from the DVR identities
380	`v_Y(fg) = v_Y(f) + v_Y(g)`, `v_Y(1) = 0`. The per-`Y` identities live in
381	`Scheme.RationalMap.order` via `Ring.ordFrac` (a `K →*₀ ℤᵐ⁰` monoid-with-zero
382	hom) and `WithZero.log_mul` / `WithZero.log_one`. -/
383	noncomputable def principal_hom [IsIntegral X] [IsLocallyNoetherian X]
384	    [Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne X] :
385	    (X.functionField)ˣ →* Multiplicative X.WeilDivisor where
386	  toFun u :=
387	    Multiplicative.ofAdd (principal (↑u : X.functionField) (Units.ne_zero u))
388	  map_one' := by
389	    -- Goal: Multiplicative.ofAdd (principal ↑1 _) = 1.
390	    -- Use `← ofAdd_zero` to rewrite RHS as `Multiplicative.ofAdd 0`,
391	    -- then `congr 1` reduces to `principal ↑1 _ = 0` (Finsupp equality),
392	    -- and `Finsupp.ext` peels to the per-`Y` coordinate identity, which
393	    -- closes by unfolding `order` to `WithZero.log (Ring.ordFrac _ 1) = 0`
394	    -- via `map_one` and `WithZero.log_one`.
395	    rw [← ofAdd_zero]
396	    congr 1
397	    apply Finsupp.ext
398	    intro Y
399	    change Scheme.RationalMap.order Y
400	        (((1 : (X.functionField)ˣ) : X.functionField)) = 0
401	    rw [Units.val_one]
402	    unfold Scheme.RationalMap.order
403	    rw [map_one, WithZero.log_one]
404	  map_mul' u v := by
405	    -- Goal: Multiplicative.ofAdd (principal ↑(u*v) _) =
406	    --       Multiplicative.ofAdd (principal ↑u _) * Multiplicative.ofAdd (principal ↑v _).
407	    -- Use `← ofAdd_add` to rewrite RHS, then `congr 1` reduces to
408	    -- `principal ↑(u*v) _ = principal ↑u _ + principal ↑v _` (Finsupp
409	    -- equality), and `Finsupp.ext` peels to the per-`Y` coordinate
410	    -- identity `order Y (uv) = order Y u + order Y v`, which closes by
411	    -- `Ring.ordFrac (uv) = Ring.ordFrac u * Ring.ordFrac v` (`map_mul`)
412	    -- and `WithZero.log_mul` (the nonzero hypotheses come from
413	    -- `Units.ne_zero` and `map_ne_zero`).
414	    rw [← ofAdd_add]
415	    congr 1
416	    apply Finsupp.ext
417	    intro Y
418	    change Scheme.RationalMap.order Y ((↑(u * v) : X.functionField))
419	      = Scheme.RationalMap.order Y (↑u : X.functionField)
420	      + Scheme.RationalMap.order Y (↑v : X.functionField)
421	    rw [Units.val_mul]
422	    unfold Scheme.RationalMap.order
423	    rw [map_mul]
424	    exact WithZero.log_mul
425	      ((map_ne_zero _).mpr (Units.ne_zero u))
426	      ((map_ne_zero _).mpr (Units.ne_zero v))
427	
428	/-- **Principal divisors on a complete nonsingular curve have degree zero**
429	(Hartshorne Corollary II.6.10, Stacks 0BE3).
430	
431	For every nonzero rational function `f ∈ K(C)^×` on a smooth proper curve `C`
432	over an algebraically closed field `k̄`,
433	`deg(div(f)) = 0 ∈ ℤ`.
434	
435	Blueprint reference: `thm:principal_deg_zero` (Hartshorne II.6 Cor. 6.10 p. 138).
436	
437	The proof (Hartshorne 6.10): if `f ∈ k̄` is constant then `div(f) = 0` and the
438	claim is trivial. Otherwise the inclusion `k̄(f) ⊂ K(C)` exhibits `K(C)` as a
439	finite extension of `k̄(f) ≅ k̄(t)`, so the corresponding morphism
440	`φ : C → ℙ¹_{k̄}` is finite, `div(f) = φ^*([0] - [∞])`, and pullback along a
441	finite morphism multiplies degree by `deg(φ)`. Two auxiliary sub-lemmas
442	(finite morphism induced by a non-constant rational function; multiplicativity
443	of degree under finite pullback, Hartshorne II.6.9) are deferred to follow-up
444	iters of `RR.1`. -/
445	theorem principal_degree_zero {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]
446	    (C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))) [IsProper C.hom]
447	    [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom] [GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom]
448	    [IsIntegral C.left] [IsLocallyNoetherian C.left]
449	    [Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne C.left]
450	    (f : C.left.functionField) (hf : f ≠ 0) :
451	    degree (principal f hf) = 0 := by
452	  -- Hartshorne II.6.10 (iter-178 partial). Case-split on whether every
453	  -- prime-divisor order of `f` vanishes. On a complete nonsingular curve,
454	  -- this case split exactly recovers Hartshorne's constant-vs-non-constant
455	  -- split: `(∀ Y, ord_Y f = 0)` ⟺ `f ∈ k̄ \ {0}` (a nowhere-vanishing
456	  -- rational function on a complete curve is constant; that direction is
457	  -- Hartshorne II.6.10's "if `f ∈ k̄` then `(f) = 0`").
458	  by_cases hconst : ∀ Y : C.left.PrimeDivisor, Scheme.RationalMap.order Y f = 0
459	  · -- Constant branch: every order vanishes ⟹ `principal f hf = 0` ⟹
460	    -- `degree (principal f hf) = degree 0 = 0`.
461	    have hprincipal_zero : principal f hf = 0 := by
462	      apply Finsupp.ext
463	      intro Y
464	      change Scheme.RationalMap.order Y f = 0
465	      exact hconst Y
466	    rw [hprincipal_zero]
467	    unfold degree
468	    exact Finsupp.sum_zero_index
469	  · -- Non-constant branch (Hartshorne II.6.10 `f ∉ k̄`): the inclusion
470	    -- `k̄(f) ⊂ K(C)` defines a finite morphism `φ : C → ℙ¹_{k̄}` via the
471	    -- function-field-determines-curve correspondence (Hartshorne I.6.12),
472	    -- and `principal f hf = φ^*([0] - [∞])`. Pullback along a finite
473	    -- morphism of curves multiplies degrees by `deg(φ)` (Hartshorne
474	    -- II.6.9), so `deg(principal f hf) = deg(φ) · deg([0] - [∞]) =
475	    -- deg(φ) · 0 = 0`.
476	    -- This branch is gated on (i) the `φ : C → ℙ¹` construction (Lane 5
477	    -- `RationalCurveIso.lean:morphismToP1OfGlobalSections`, iter-178+),
478	    -- and (ii) the degree-multiplicativity-under-finite-pullback bridge
479	    -- (Hartshorne II.6.9, Mathlib gap).
480	    sorry

482	/-! ## §6. Positive part of a Weil divisor
483	
484	Iter-190 plan-phase Lane I Pin 2 corrective substrate. The carrier
485	`X.WeilDivisor = X.PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ` is a finitely supported integer-valued
486	function on prime divisors; the *positive part* `(D)_0` is obtained by
487	replacing each coefficient `n_Y` with `max(n_Y, 0)`. Equivalently this is
488	the lattice join `D ⊔ 0` in the pointwise lattice structure (the
489	`semilatticeSup` instance on `Finsupp` is noncomputable). The complementary
490	*negative part* `(D)_∞ := (-D)_0` then gives the canonical decomposition
491	`D = (D)_0 - (D)_∞` into a difference of effective divisors.
492	
493	Blueprint reference: `def:WeilDivisor_positivePart` /
494	`lem:degree_positivePart_principal_eq_finrank` of
495	`RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor.tex` §6 (iter-190 plan-phase additions).
496	-/
497	
498	/-- **Positive part of a Weil divisor**.
499	
500	`positivePart D` is the divisor obtained from `D` by replacing each
501	coefficient `n_Y` with `max(n_Y, 0)` (equivalently `D ⊔ 0` in the
502	pointwise lattice structure on `Finsupp`). For `D = div(f)` on a smooth
503	proper curve `C`, this recovers the divisor-of-zeros `(f)_0` — the
504	formal sum of the zeros of `f` with their multiplicities.
505	
506	The complementary `negativePart D := positivePart (-D)` then gives
507	`D = positivePart D - negativePart D` as a difference of effective
508	divisors.

510	Implementation: `Finsupp.mapRange (fun n : ℤ => n ⊔ 0) (by simp)`, the
511	generic Mathlib packaging that maps a finsupp's coefficients through a
512	zero-preserving function. (The `D ⊔ 0` lattice form is equivalent but
513	requires the noncomputable `Finsupp.semilatticeSup` synthesis on

519	noncomputable def positivePart (D : X.WeilDivisor) : X.WeilDivisor :=
520	  Finsupp.mapRange (fun n : ℤ => n ⊔ 0) (by simp) D

524	lemma positivePart_zero : positivePart (0 : X.WeilDivisor) = 0 := by
525	  change Finsupp.mapRange (fun n : ℤ => n ⊔ 0) (by simp)
526	    (0 : X.PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ) = (0 : X.PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ)
527	  exact Finsupp.mapRange_zero

665	  -- local-parameter witness), then close axiom-clean.
666	  sorry
667	
668	/-! ## §7. Linear equivalence and the divisor class group -/
669	
670	/-- **Linear equivalence of Weil divisors.**
671	
672	Two Weil divisors `D, D' ∈ Div(X)` are linearly equivalent, written `D ~ D'`, if
673	and only if there exists a nonzero rational function `f ∈ K(X)^×` with
674	`D - D' = div(f) ∈ Div(X)`.
675	
676	`~` is an equivalence relation (reflexivity from `div(1) = 0`, symmetry from
677	`div(f⁻¹) = -div(f)`, transitivity from `div(fg) = div(f) + div(g)`, all via
678	`thm:principal_hom`). The quotient `Cl(X) := Div(X) / im(div)` is the
679	**divisor class group** of `X`.
680	
681	On a smooth proper curve `C` over `k̄`, `thm:principal_deg_zero` shows the
682	degree map descends to `deg : Cl(C) → ℤ`.
683	
684	Blueprint reference: `def:linear_equivalence` (Hartshorne II §6 p. 131). -/
685	def LinearEquivalence [IsIntegral X] [IsLocallyNoetherian X]
686	    [Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne X] (D D' : X.WeilDivisor) : Prop :=
687	  ∃ (f : X.functionField) (hf : f ≠ 0), D - D' = principal f hf
688	
689	end Scheme.WeilDivisor
690	
691	end AlgebraicGeometry
692

1	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/WeilDivisor.lean
2	\chapter{Weil divisors on a smooth proper curve (RR.1)}
3	\label{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor}
4	% SOURCE: Hartshorne, \emph{Algebraic Geometry}, II \S6, pp.~130--137 +
5	% IV.1, pp.~294--296 (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf).
6	% Stacks pointers (tag-level, not verbatim-quoted): 02RW (divisors),
7	% 02ME (order at a point on a regular 1-dimensional scheme),
8	% 0BE0 (degree of a divisor on a curve), 0BE3 (principal divisors have
9	% degree zero on a complete nonsingular curve).
10	\section{Setup and motivation}
11	This chapter is the first of four sub-build chapters \texttt{RR.1}--\texttt{RR.4}
12	that close the project's headline \emph{RR bridge}
13	\[
14	  \mathtt{genusZero\_curve\_iso\_P1}
15	  \,\colon\,
16	  \text{a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve \(C / \bar k\) with
17	  \(g(C) = 0\) is isomorphic to \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\).}
18	\]
19	The bridge as proved in Hartshorne (Example~IV.1.3.5) chains together:
20	divisor of a closed point on a curve (\texttt{RR.1}, this chapter); the
21	Riemann--Roch dimension formula \(\ell(D) = \deg D + 1\) for \(\deg D \geq 0\) on
22	a genus-\(0\) curve (\texttt{RR.2}); the global-sections argument identifying
23	\(\dim_k H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\) via \(1\) and a non-constant function
24	(\texttt{RR.3}); the ``rational curve \(\Rightarrow \cong \mathbb P^1\)''
25	classification recovered as a degree-\(1\) morphism into \(\mathbb P^1\) via
26	\texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections} (\texttt{RR.4}).
27	Mathlib ships no \texttt{WeilDivisor} on a scheme. Adjacent pieces exist, but
28	none of them is the data structure we need: \texttt{MeromorphicOn.divisor}
29	(\texttt{Mathlib.Analysis.Meromorphic.Divisor}) is the order function of a
30	meromorphic function valued in a \emph{normed} field, not a formal sum of
31	codimension-\(1\) cycles on a scheme; \texttt{CommRing.Pic}
32	(\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup}) is the ring-level Picard group of
33	invertible modules under tensor product, with no scheme-level analogue;
34	\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap}
35	(\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Birational.RationalMap}) packages rational
36	maps but not divisors. Consequently this chapter, and the three siblings, are
37	\emph{project-bespoke}: the only Mathlib tools they rely on are
38	discrete-valuation rings, the function field, and the ambient scheme API.
39	The audit grounding this scope decision is in
40	\texttt{analogies/rrbridge-survey.md}.
41	This chapter establishes the formal-sum data type
42	\(\mathrm{Div}(X) = \bigoplus_{\substack{Z \subset X \\ \mathrm{codim} 1}} \mathbb Z\) on a
43	Noetherian integral scheme \(X\) satisfying Hartshorne's condition \((*)\), the
44	principal-divisor homomorphism \(\mathrm{div} \colon K(X)^{\times} \to \mathrm{Div}(X)\) on a
45	curve, the degree map \(\deg \colon \mathrm{Div}(C) \to \mathbb Z\) on a smooth proper
46	curve over an algebraically closed field, and the linear-equivalence
47	relation. The \emph{degree-zero of a principal divisor} statement
48	(\ref{thm:principal_deg_zero}) is included for completeness; its proof
49	decomposes into a separate sub-build inside \texttt{RR.1} (it uses the
50	auxiliary lemma that any non-constant rational function on \(C\) defines a
51	finite morphism \(C \to \mathbb P^1\)). The line-bundle construction $\mathcal
52	O_C(D)\( and the associated cohomology computation \)H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(D))$
53	live in the sibling chapter \texttt{RR.3}; the Riemann--Roch dimension
54	formula lives in \texttt{RR.2}; the genus-\(0\) classification proper lives in
55	\texttt{RR.4}.
56	Throughout this chapter, the standing convention is Hartshorne's condition
57	\((*)\):
58	% SOURCE QUOTE: "\((\ast)\) \(X\) is a noetherian integral separated scheme which
59	% is regular in codimension one."
60	\textit{Source: Hartshorne, II \S6, p.~130, the conditional \((*)\).}
61	\[
62	  (\ast)\qquad
63	  X \text{ is a noetherian integral separated scheme which is regular in
64	  codimension one.}
65	\]
66	For the degree map and for principal-divisor statements on a curve, we
67	additionally assume \(X\) is a complete (i.e.\ proper) nonsingular curve over
68	an algebraically closed field \(\bar k\), where Hartshorne's condition \((*)\) is
69	automatic.
70	\paragraph{Standing hypothesis \((*)\) in the Lean encoding (iter-173 pin).}
71	Mathlib (as of \texttt{b80f227}) does not bundle Hartshorne's four-clause
72	\((\ast)\) as a single typeclass. The chapter pins \((*)\) to a \emph{layered}
73	typeclass set that the prover-agent threads through the Lean signatures in
74	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/WeilDivisor.lean}:
75	\begin{itemize}
76	  \item \textbf{Basic formal-sum layer} (carrier of \ref{def:prime_divisor} and
77	    \ref{def:codim1_cycles}, the additive group \(\mathrm{Div}(X)\), the degree
78	    \ref{def:divisor_degree}, the degree-homomorphism
79	    \ref{thm:divisor_degree_hom}, and \ref{def:divisor_closed_point}): the
80	    only Mathlib typeclass invoked is \texttt{[IsIntegral X]}
81	    (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.AffineScheme} via
82	    \texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Properties}). Integrality of \(X\) already
83	    delivers \texttt{IrreducibleSpace X.carrier} (the carrier-level instance
84	    chain), so \(X\) has a unique generic point and the function field
85	    \texttt{X.functionField} (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.FunctionField})
86	    is a field; separatedness and noetherianness are \emph{not} required for
87	    the bare formal-sum data type.
88	  \item \textbf{Order/principal layer} (\ref{def:order_at_point},
89	    \ref{def:principal_divisor}, \ref{thm:principal_hom}): the Lean
90	    signature additionally threads
91	    \texttt{[IsLocallyNoetherian X]}
92	    (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Noetherian}) and Hartshorne's
93	    regular-in-codimension-one clause. The latter has \emph{no} Mathlib
94	    typeclass shipping it directly; the chapter's pin is the project-side
95	    predicate ``for every \(x \in X\) with \(\mathrm{codim}_X \overline{\{x\}} = 1\) the
96	    stalk \(\mathcal O_{X,x}\) is a discrete valuation ring'', encodable as
97	    the conjunction
98	    $\forall x,\; \texttt{Order.coheight}\,x = 1 \Rightarrow
99	    \texttt{IsDiscreteValuationRing}\,(\mathcal O_{X,x})$. Iter-173+ may
100	    introduce a \texttt{Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne} predicate to
101	    abbreviate this; until then the order/principal lemmas thread the DVR
102	    hypothesis explicitly at each codim-1 point.
103	  \item \textbf{Curve layer} (\ref{thm:principal_deg_zero}): the standing
104	    hypothesis tightens to a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve
105	    over \(\bar k\) with explicit integrality witness, namely
106	    \begin{quote}
107	      \texttt{\{kbar : Type u\} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]\\
108	       (C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))) [IsProper C.hom]\\
109	       \phantom{}\quad[SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]\\
110	       \phantom{}\quad[GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom] [IsIntegral C.left]}
111	    \end{quote}
112	    matching the standing typeclass set used by the rest of the project's
113	    curve API (\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian.Genus},
114	    \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian.AbelianVarietyRigidity}). Hartshorne's \((*)\) is
115	    automatic for this layer (\texttt{[IsLocallyNoetherian]} from finite type
116	    over a field; regular-in-codim-1 from smoothness; separatedness from
117	    properness; integrality threaded).
118	\end{itemize}
119	\noindent
120	The \texttt{[IsIntegral X]} hypothesis is the only one threaded through the
121	\emph{statement} of every Lean signature in
122	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/WeilDivisor.lean} that mentions a
123	function field, principal divisor, or order. The noetherian-and-DVR
124	content lives inside the proof bodies (sub-build), where the prover threads
125	the appropriate stronger hypothesis at the use site.
126	\section{Codim-\(1\) cycle group}
127	\begin{definition}
128	\leanok
129	[Prime divisor of a scheme]
130	  \label{def:prime_divisor}
131	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PrimeDivisor}
132	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, p.~130 (definition of "prime divisor" under
133	  % condition \((*)\)) (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf,
134	  % PDF page 147). Stacks pointer: tag 02RW (divisors).
135	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Definition. Let \(X\) satisfy \((\ast)\). A \emph{prime
136	  % divisor} on \(X\) is a closed integral subscheme \(Y\) of codimension one."
137	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~130 (definition of prime divisor).}
138	  Let \(X\) satisfy \((*)\). A \emph{prime divisor} on \(X\) is a closed integral
139	  subscheme \(Y \subset X\) of codimension one. Equivalently, by the standard
140	  generic-point correspondence (Hartshorne II \S6, p.~130, ``let $\eta \in
141	  Y\( be its generic point\ldots''), \)Y$ is determined by its generic point
142	  \(\eta \in X\): closed integral subschemes of codimension one of \(X\) are in
143	  natural bijection with points \(x \in X\) such that the closure
144	  \(\overline{\{x\}} \subset X\) has codimension one. The closure
145	  \(\overline{\{x\}}\), equipped with the \emph{reduced induced subscheme
146	  structure}, is automatically integral (irreducible, because the closure
147	  of a single point is irreducible, and reduced by construction); the only
148	  non-automatic condition is the codimension.
149	  \paragraph{Lean encoding (iter-173 pin).} The Lean structure
150	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PrimeDivisor} bundles a point
151	  \texttt{point : X} together with a codimension-one witness, encoded via
152	  the Mathlib predicate
153	  \[
154	    \texttt{Order.coheight\ point\ =\ 1}
155	  \]
156	  on the specialisation preorder of the topological space underlying \(X\).
157	  Concretely, Mathlib's preorder on \texttt{X.carrier} is defined by
158	  ``\(x \leq y \iff y \rightsquigarrow x\)'' (i.e.\ \(x \in \overline{\{y\}}\);
159	  see \texttt{Mathlib.Topology.Specialization} and
160	  \texttt{Mathlib.Order.KrullDimension}). Under this convention the generic
161	  point of an integral scheme is the unique \emph{maximal} element of the
162	  preorder, so for the generic point \(\eta_Y\) of a prime divisor \(Y\) the
163	  only point strictly above \(\eta_Y\) is the generic point \(\eta_X\) of \(X\),
164	  and the chain \(\eta_Y < \eta_X\) has length one, giving
165	  \texttt{Order.coheight\ \(\eta_Y\)\ =\ 1}. The DVR/integrality content of
166	  ``the closure of \texttt{point} with the reduced subscheme structure is
167	  integral'' is automatic from \(\overline{\{\,\texttt{point}\,\}}\) being
168	  irreducible, so the Lean structure does not need a separate integrality
169	  field. The iter-172 placeholder \texttt{isCodim1AndIntegral : True} in
170	  \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/WeilDivisor.lean:84--90} is to be
171	  refined to \texttt{coheight : Order.coheight point = 1} (named field, type
172	  \texttt{Prop}) in the iter-173 prover step, retaining the same
173	  \texttt{point} field for downstream defeq.
174	  \paragraph{Reference predicates considered and rejected.}
175	  Two alternatives were considered and rejected at the iter-172
176	  lean-vs-blueprint-checker stage:
177	  \begin{itemize}
178	    \item \texttt{IsIntegral\ (X.closedSubschemeOfPoint\ x)}: rejected
179	      because Mathlib (\texttt{b80f227}) ships \emph{no}
180	      \texttt{closedSubschemeOfPoint} constructor. The closest available
181	      object is \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.IdealSheafData}
182	      (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.IdealSheaf.Basic}), which packages
183	      a quasi-coherent ideal sheaf but does not produce a reduced induced
184	      subscheme on the closure of a point. Building that constructor is
185	      itself a Mathlib-upstream-scale project (Stacks tag 01J9 ``reduced
186	      induced scheme structure''), outside the scope of \texttt{RR.1}.
187	    \item A bundled \texttt{Scheme.IdealSheafData}-based prime-divisor
188	      carrier: rejected because the chapter only needs the generic-point
189	      witness for downstream order/principal-divisor consumption; carrying
190	      an \texttt{IdealSheafData} adds redundant data the chapter never
191	      uses.
192	  \end{itemize}
193	  The \texttt{Order.coheight}-based encoding is therefore the
194	  minimum-Mathlib-dependence pin compatible with the chapter's downstream
195	  needs.
196	\end{definition}
197	\begin{definition}
198	\leanok
199	[Codim-\(1\) cycle group / Weil divisor group of a scheme]
200	  \label{def:codim1_cycles}
201	  
202	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor}
203	  \uses{def:prime_divisor}
204	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, p.~130 (definition of "Weil divisor" under
205	  % condition \((*)\)) (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf,
206	  % PDF page 147). Stacks pointer: tag 02RW.
207	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "A \emph{Weil divisor} is an element of the free abelian
208	  % group \(\operatorname{Div} X\) generated by the prime divisors. We write a
209	  % divisor as \(D = \sum n_i Y_i\), where the \(Y_i\) are prime divisors, the
210	  % \(n_i\) are integers, and only finitely many \(n_i\) are different from
211	  % zero. If all the \(n_i \geq 0\), we say that \(D\) is \emph{effective}."
212	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~130 (Weil divisor group).}
213	  Let \(X\) be a Noetherian integral separated scheme that is regular in
214	  codimension one (i.e.\ \(X\) satisfies \((*)\)). The \emph{Weil divisor group}
215	  \[
216	    \mathrm{Div}(X) \;=\; \bigoplus_{\substack{Y \subset X \\ \text{prime divisor}}}
217	    \mathbb Z \cdot Y
218	  \]
219	  is the free abelian group on the set of prime divisors of \(X\)
220	  (\ref{def:codim1_cycles}); an element \(D = \sum n_i Y_i\) with finitely
221	  many nonzero coefficients \(n_i \in \mathbb Z\) is called a \emph{Weil
222	  divisor}, and is \emph{effective} when all \(n_i \geq 0\).
223	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope.} The Lean definition
224	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor X} is given for an arbitrary
225	  scheme \texttt{X : Scheme} as the formal-sum data type
226	  \texttt{X.PrimeDivisor \(\to_0\) \(\mathbb Z\)}; integrality of \(X\) is
227	  \emph{not} required to form the additive group \(\mathrm{Div}(X)\) (it becomes
228	  necessary only at the order/principal-divisor layer where the function
229	  field appears). Hartshorne's \((*)\) is the standing convention in the
230	  prose but is not threaded into the typeclass arguments of this base
231	  declaration. See ``Standing hypothesis \((*)\) in the Lean encoding'' above
232	  for the per-layer typeclass discipline.
233	\end{definition}
234	\section{Order of a rational function at a prime divisor}
235	\begin{definition}
236	\leanok
237	[Order of a rational function at a prime divisor]
238	  \label{def:order_at_point}
239	  
240	  \uses{def:codim1_cycles}
241	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.order}
242	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, p.~130--131 (valuation \(v_Y\) of a prime
243	  % divisor; "zero/pole of \(f\) along \(Y\)") (read from
244	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF pages 147--148).
245	  % Stacks pointer: tag 02ME.
246	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "If \(Y\) is a prime divisor on \(X\), let \(\eta \in Y\) be its
247	  % generic point. Then the local ring \(\mathcal O_{\eta, X}\) of \(X\) at
248	  % \(\eta\) is a discrete valuation ring with quotient field \(K\), the
249	  % function field of \(X\). We call the corresponding discrete valuation
250	  % \(v_Y\) the \emph{valuation of \(Y\)}."
251	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Now let \(f \in K^\ast\) be any nonzero rational function
252	  % on \(X\). Then \(v_Y(f)\) is an integer. If it is positive, we say \(f\) has
253	  % a \emph{zero} along \(Y\), of that order; if it is negative, we say \(f\)
254	  % has a \emph{pole} along \(Y\), of order \(-v_Y(f)\)."
255	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, pp.~130--131 (valuation \(v_Y\)).}
256	  Let \(X\) satisfy \((*)\). For a prime divisor \(Y \subset X\) with generic
257	  point \(\eta \in Y\), the local ring \(\mathcal O_{X, \eta}\) is a discrete
258	  valuation ring (by regularity in codimension one) with quotient field the
259	  function field \(K(X)\). For a nonzero rational function \(f \in K(X)^{\times}\)
260	  the \emph{order of \(f\) along \(Y\)} is the integer
261	  \[
262	    \ord_Y(f) \;:=\; v_Y(f) \;\in\; \mathbb Z,
263	  \]
264	  where \(v_Y\) is the normalised discrete valuation associated to
265	  \(\mathcal O_{X, \eta}\). If \(\ord_Y(f) > 0\), \(f\) has a \emph{zero of
266	  order \(\ord_Y(f)\) along \(Y\)}; if \(\ord_Y(f) < 0\), \(f\) has a \emph{pole
267	  of order \(-\ord_Y(f)\) along \(Y\)}.
268	  Specialisation to a curve. If \(C\) is a smooth proper curve over \(\bar k\)
269	  and \(P \in C\) is a closed point, then \(P\) is automatically a prime
270	  divisor (it is closed, integral, of codimension one in the \(1\)-dimensional
271	  scheme \(C\)); the local ring \(\mathcal O_{C, P}\) is a discrete valuation
272	  ring by smoothness, and \(\ord_P(f) = v_P(f)\) is the standard DVR
273	  valuation of \(f\) at \(P\).
274	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope (iter-175 pin, per analogist
275	  \texttt{dvr-rationalmap-order}).} The iter-175 Lean signature is
276	  \begin{quote}
277	    \texttt{noncomputable def order \{X : Scheme.\{u\}\} [IsIntegral X]\\
278	    \phantom{}\quad[IsLocallyNoetherian X] (Y : X.PrimeDivisor)\\
279	    \phantom{}\quad[Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)]\\
280	    \phantom{}\quad(f : X.functionField) : \(\mathbb Z\)}
281	  \end{quote}
282	  with \emph{no} \(f \neq 0\) hypothesis (junk-on-\(f = 0\) convention,
283	  see below). The body is
284	  \[
285	    \texttt{order}\ Y\ f \;:=\;
286	      \texttt{WithZero.log}\ \bigl(
287	        \texttt{Ring.ordFrac}\ (X.\texttt{presheaf.stalk}\ Y.\texttt{point})\ f
288	      \bigr).
289	  \]
290	  \emph{Mathlib API path.}
291	  \begin{itemize}
292	    \item \texttt{Ring.ordFrac R : K \(\to\!\!*_0\) \(\mathbb Z^{m_0}\)}
293	      (with \(\mathbb Z^{m_0} = \texttt{WithZero (Multiplicative}\ \mathbb Z)\)),
294	      from \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.OrderOfVanishing.Basic} (Stacks
295	      tag~\texttt{02MD}), is Mathlib's canonical monoid-with-zero hom
296	      extending the length-of-quotient valuation
297	      \texttt{Ring.ord} from a Noetherian Krull-dim-\(\leq 1\) ring to
298	      its fraction field via \texttt{Submonoid.LocalizationMap.lift\textsubscript{0}}.
299	      For a DVR-uniformised local ring its value on \(r \in R\) is the
300	      length of \(R/(r)\) (the DVR valuation); on \(a/b \in K\), it is
301	      \(\texttt{ord}(a) - \texttt{ord}(b)\). The hypotheses
302	      \texttt{[CommRing R] [Nontrivial R] [IsNoetherianRing R]\\
303	      [Ring.KrullDimLE 1 R]} are exactly the typeclasses the body
304	      synthesises from \([\texttt{IsIntegral}\ X]\) (delivers
305	      \texttt{IsDomain} hence \texttt{Nontrivial} on the stalk via
306	      \texttt{instIsDomainCarrierStalkCommRingCatPresheafOfIsIntegral},
307	      and \texttt{IsFractionRing (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)
308	      X.functionField} via
309	      \texttt{instIsFractionRingCarrierStalkCommRingCatPresheafFunctionField}),
310	      from \([\texttt{IsLocallyNoetherian}\ X]\) (delivers
311	      \texttt{IsNoetherianRing} on the stalk via
312	      \texttt{instIsNoetherianRingCarrierStalkCommRingCatPresheafOfIsLocallyNoetherian}),
313	      and from the explicitly threaded
314	      \texttt{[Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)]}.
315	    \item \texttt{WithZero.log : \(\mathbb Z^{m_0} \to \mathbb Z\)}
316	      from \texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.GroupWithZero.WithZero} is Mathlib's
317	      canonical projection to the additive value group with
318	      junk-on-zero: \texttt{log\_zero}: \(\log 0 = 0\);
319	      \texttt{log\_mul}: \(\log(x \cdot y) = \log x + \log y\) for
320	      \(x, y \neq 0\). This makes the downstream additive identities
321	      \(v_Y(fg) = v_Y(f) + v_Y(g)\), \(v_Y(f^{-1}) = -v_Y(f)\),
322	      \(v_Y(1) = 0\) available as named lemmas for
323	      \ref{def:order_at_point}.
324	  \end{itemize}
325	  \emph{Junk-on-\(f = 0\) convention.} Setting \(f = 0\) in
326	  \texttt{X.functionField} feeds \(0\) into
327	  \texttt{Ring.ordFrac (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)} (zero of the
328	  monoid-with-zero hom; \texttt{Ring.ordFrac} maps \(0\) to
329	  \(0 \in \mathbb Z^{m_0}\)); then \texttt{WithZero.log 0 = 0} so
330	  \(\texttt{order}\ Y\ 0 = 0\). This is the canonical Mathlib junk
331	  choice; no separate \(f \neq 0\) hypothesis is threaded on the
332	  signature.
333	  \emph{In-tree gap-fill (project-side; Mathlib upstream opportunity).}
334	  Mathlib (as of \texttt{b80f227}) has no direct bridge from the
335	  topological condition \(\texttt{Order.coheight}\ Y.\texttt{point} = 1\)
336	  on \texttt{X.carrier} to the algebraic condition
337	  \(\texttt{Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)}\) on the
338	  stalk. The missing fact (Stacks tag~\texttt{02IZ} / \texttt{005X}) is
339	  the topological identity ``coheight of \(x\) in the scheme's
340	  specialisation preorder equals the height of the prime ideal
341	  corresponding to \(x\) in any affine chart''; the algebraic step
342	  ``ideal height \(=\) stalk Krull dim'' is in Mathlib as
343	  \texttt{IsLocalization.AtPrime.ringKrullDim\_eq\_height}
344	  (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Ideal.Height}) and
345	  \texttt{IsAffineOpen.isLocalization\_stalk}
346	  (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.AffineScheme}). The iter-175
347	  workaround is to thread the explicit instance
348	  \texttt{[Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)]} on
349	  \texttt{order} (and downstream consumers); a future two-line
350	  Mathlib upstream PR pinning the coheight-height bridge would let
351	  the project drop the explicit argument in favour of synthesis from
352	  \texttt{coheight Y.point = 1} via the \texttt{PrimeDivisor} carrier.
353	\end{definition}
354	\section{Divisor of a closed point on a curve}
355	\begin{definition}
356	\leanok
357	[Divisor of a closed point on a curve]
358	  \label{def:divisor_closed_point}
359	  \uses{def:codim1_cycles}
360	  
361	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.ofClosedPoint}
362	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6 ("Divisors on Curves"), p.~137 (read from
363	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 154):
364	  % "A prime divisor is just a closed point, so an arbitrary divisor can
365	  % be written \(D = \sum n_i P_i\), where the \(P_i\) are closed points, and
366	  % \(n_i \in \mathbf Z\)."
367	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Now we come to the study of divisors on curves. If \(X\)
368	  % is a nonsingular curve, then \(X\) satisfies the condition \((\ast)\) used
369	  % above, so we can talk about divisors on \(X\). A prime divisor is just
370	  % a closed point, so an arbitrary divisor can be written $D = \sum n_i
371	  % P_i\(, where the \)P_i\( are closed points, and \)n_i \in \mathbf Z$."
372	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~137 (``Divisors on Curves'').}
373	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper curve over a field \(k\) and let \(P \in C\) be a
374	  closed point. The \emph{Weil divisor associated to \(P\)} is the element
375	  \[
376	    [P] \;:=\; 1 \cdot P \;\in\; \mathrm{Div}(C)
377	  \]
378	  i.e.\ the prime divisor \(P\) with multiplicity one. The assignment is
379	  well-defined because the closed point \(P\) is automatically a codimension-\(1\)
380	  integral closed subscheme of the \(1\)-dimensional integral scheme \(C\).
381	  Conversely, every prime divisor on \(C\) is of this form, so an arbitrary
382	  divisor on \(C\) is a finite formal \(\mathbb Z\)-linear combination
383	  \(\sum n_i [P_i]\) of closed points.
384	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope (iter-174 pin).} The Lean signature
385	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.ofClosedPoint} accepts a
386	  scheme \texttt{C : Scheme.\{u\}}, a point \texttt{P : C}, and a
387	  hypothesis \texttt{(\_hP : IsClosed (\{P\} : Set C))}. It does
388	  \emph{not} typeclass-pin ``smooth proper curve over \(\bar k\)'' at the
389	  carrier level, and it does \emph{not} thread the
390	  one-dimensional-integral-scheme content into the declaration head.
391	  Instead the iter-174 encoding is \emph{junk-defined} outside the
392	  codim-\(1\) regime:
393	  \[
394	    \texttt{ofClosedPoint}\ P\ \_\!hP\ :=\
395	      \begin{cases}
396	        \texttt{Finsupp.single}\ \langle P, h\rangle\ 1 &
397	          \text{if } h : \texttt{Order.coheight}\ P = 1,\\
398	        0 & \text{otherwise.}
399	      \end{cases}
400	  \]
401	  On the off-branch --- where \texttt{P} is not a codimension-one point
402	  (e.g.\ \texttt{P} is the generic point of \texttt{C}, or a closed point
403	  of codimension \(\geq 2\) on a higher-dimensional scheme) ---
404	  \texttt{ofClosedPoint} returns the zero divisor \(0 \in \mathrm{Div}(C)\). This
405	  convention lets the Lean signature avoid threading
406	  \texttt{[OneDimensional C]} / \texttt{[IsIntegral C]} typeclass payload
407	  at the declaration head; the well-definedness consumer must instead
408	  invoke the bridge equation lemmas
409	  \ref{lem:ofClosedPoint_eq_single} and \ref{lem:ofClosedPoint_eq_zero}
410	  to expose the two branches at the point of use.
411	  On the intended regime (one-dimensional integral scheme \(C\), \(P\) a
412	  closed point), the chapter's mathematical statement
413	  \([P] := 1 \cdot P \in \mathrm{Div}(C)\) is recovered by combining
414	  \ref{def:divisor_closed_point} with the chain
415	  \(\texttt{IsClosed \{P\}} \Rightarrow \texttt{Order.coheight}\ P = 1\)
416	  that the prover threads inside the consumer (using the
417	  one-dimensional-integrality content).
418	\end{definition}
419	\begin{lemma}
420	\leanok

422	  \label{lem:ofClosedPoint_eq_single}
